▸ 14.1.05 Brain Abscess

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, cns, brain-abscess, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Central Nervous System Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Brain Abscess

Related: [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Tuberculous Meningitis]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Post-Transplant Infections]]

[!important]
Brain abscess = focal suppurative infection within brain parenchyma. Ring-enhancing lesion on imaging with surrounding oedema. Polymicrobial (anaerobes, streptococci, staphylococci). Neurosurgical drainage + 6–8 weeks IV antibiotics. Source identification critical (sinusitis, otitis, dental, endocarditis, pulmonary, trauma).

Learning Objectives

  • Recognise subacute presentation: headache, fever, focal deficits, seizures, papilloedema
  • Identify predisposing sources: contiguous spread (sinus/ear/dental), haematogenous, trauma/surgery
  • Select empirical antibiotics covering likely pathogens by source
  • Interpret imaging: ring-enhancing lesion, DWI restriction, MR spectroscopy (lactate, acetate, succinate)
  • Manage raised ICP, seizures, timing of surgical intervention
  • Duration and monitoring of antibiotic therapy

Definition

Brain abscess = encapsulated collection of pus within brain parenchyma. Stages: cerebritis (days 1–3) → capsule formation (days 10–14) → mature abscess. Incidence: ~0.5–1/100,000; higher in immunocompromised, cyanotic heart disease, IVDU.

Core Microbiology — Aetiology by Source

Source / RouteCommon PathogensNotes
Contiguous spread (sinusitis, otitis, mastoiditis, dental)Anaerobes (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus, Fusobacterium, Prevotella), Streptococci (S. milleri group, viridans), EnterobacteriaceaeFrontal lobe (sinusitis), temporal lobe/cerebellum (otitis)
Haematogenous (cyanotic CHD, endocarditis, pulmonary AVM, IVDU)Streptococci (viridans, S. milleri), Staphylococci (S. aureus), Anaerobes, Gram-negatives, Fungi (Aspergillus, Candida), NocardiaMultiple abscesses common (MCA territory); septic emboli
Post-trauma / Post-neurosurgeryS. aureus (MSSA/MRSA), CoNS, Gram-negatives (Pseudomonas, Enterobacter), AnaerobesEarly (<1m) vs late (>1m)
Immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, steroids, chemo)Toxoplasma (HIV CD4<100), Nocardia, Aspergillus, Mucorales, Cryptococcus, Listeria, TB, Lymphoma (mimic)Toxoplasma #1 in HIV CD4<100 — ring-enhancing, multiple, basal ganglia
CryptogenicStreptococci, anaerobesNo source identified (~15–25%)

Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

ParameterBrain AbscessTumour / Necrotic TumourDemyelination
CT/MRIRing-enhancing lesion, smooth thin wall, surrounding oedemaIrregular thick wall, heterogeneous enhancementOpen-ring / incomplete ring, perivenular
DWIRestricted diffusion (pus = high viscosity)Variable (not typically restricted)Not restricted
MR SpectroscopyLactate ↑, acetate ↑, succinate ↑, alanine ↑; NO NAA, NO creatine, NO cholineCholine ↑, NAA ↓, lipids/lactate ↑Choline ↑, NAA ↓
CSFUsually normal or mild neutrophilic pleocytosis; protein ↑; DO NOT LP if mass effectVariableOligoclonal bands, IgG index ↑
Blood culturesPositive 10–25% (haematogenous)NegativeNegative
[!warning]
Contraindication to LP: Mass effect, midline shift, papilloedema, GCS<10 — risk of herniation. Diagnosis by imaging + blood cultures + surgical aspirate.

Clinical Features

FeatureFrequencyNotes
Headache70–90%Progressive, severe, worse with Valsalva
Fever50–70%May be absent in immunocompromised, elderly
Focal neurological deficits50–70%Hemiparesis, aphasia, visual field defect (depends on location)
Seizures25–50%Focal or generalised; may be presenting feature
Altered mental status30–50%Late sign = raised ICP / herniation
Papilloedema25–40%Sign of raised ICP
Nuchal rigidity<20%If rupture → ventriculitis → meningism

Approach / Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Subacute headache + fever + focal deficit/seizure/papilloedema
↓
CT/MRI brain with contrast URGENTLY
↓
Ring-enhancing lesion + DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate
↓
Brain abscess likely
Consider tumour, TB, fungal, Toxoplasma, demyelination, lymphoma
↓
Blood cultures x2-3 BEFORE antibiotics
↓
Empirical IV antibiotics by source
↓
Neurosurgical review: aspiration vs craniotomy vs stereotactic biopsy
↓
Aspirate for Gram stain, culture, PCR, 16S rRNA
↓
Targeted antibiotics 6-8 weeks IV
↓
Serial imaging: size reduction, oedema resolution

Investigations

TestRole
MRI brain (contrast, DWI, MRS)Gold standard: ring-enhancement, DWI restriction (pus), MRS (lactate, acetate, succinate = abscess; choline/NAA = tumour)
CT brain (contrast)Alternative if MRI unavailable; ring-enhancement, oedema
Blood cultures (×2–3 sets)Before antibiotics; positive in haematogenous spread
Surgical aspirateGram stain, aerobic/anaerobic culture, PCR, 16S rRNA, fungal AFB, Nocardia
CRP, PCT, FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagBaseline, monitoring
Echocardiogram (TTE/TOE)If haematogenous suspected (endocarditis, CHD)
Chest CTIf pulmonary source suspected (AVM, abscess, tumour)
Sinuses/ears/dental imagingIf contiguous spread suspected
HIV test, CD4If immunocompromised (Toxoplasma, fungal, Nocardia)
Serology (Toxoplasma IgG)HIV CD4<100 with ring-enhancing lesions

Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (Start AFTER Blood Cultures)

Clinical ScenarioRegimenRationale
Community-acquired (contiguous: sinus/ear/dental)Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6hStreptococci, anaerobes, staphylococci
Haematogenous (endocarditis, cyanotic CHD, IVDU, pulmonary)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h + Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h (+/- Meropenem if Gram-neg suspected)Staphylococci, streptococci, anaerobes, Gram-negatives
Post-neurosurgery / Trauma (<1 month)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h + Meropenem 2g IV 8hMSSA/MRSA, Gram-negatives (Pseudomonas), CoNS
Post-neurosurgery / Trauma (>1 month)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h + Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12hLess Pseudomonas risk late
Immunocompromised (HIV CD4<100)Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Folinic acid (for Toxoplasma) + broad antibacterialToxoplasma #1; empirical anti-Toxoplasma if multiple ring-enhancing
Suspected NocardiaTMP-SMX high-dose (15–20mg/kg TMP IV/PO 6–8h) + Meropenem/Imipenem + AmikacinNocardia: sulfonamides first-line
Suspected Fungal (Aspergillus, Mucorales)Voriconazole/L-AmB per fungal pneumonia guidelinesSee Fungal Pneumonias note
[!important]
Metronidazole essential for anaerobic coverage in contiguous spread. Vancomycin for staphylococcal coverage (MSSA/MRSA). Ceftriaxone/meropenem for streptococci/Gram-negatives. Duration 6–8 weeks IV after surgical drainage.

Targeted Therapy (Post-Aspirate Culture)

PathogenTargeted RegimenDuration
S. milleri / viridans streptococciPenicillin G 24MU/day IV ÷4–6h OR Ceftriaxone 2g 12h6–8 weeks
Anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, etc.)Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h + Penicillin/Ceftriaxone6–8 weeks
S. aureus (MSSA)Flucloxacillin 2g IV 4h OR Cefazolin 2g IV 8h6–8 weeks
S. aureus (MRSA)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h (trough 15–20) OR Daptomycin 8–10mg/kg IV OD6–8 weeks
Gram-negatives (Enterobacteriaceae)Ceftriaxone 2g 12h OR Meropenem 2g 8h (if ESBL)6–8 weeks
PseudomonasMeropenem 2g 8h + Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV 12h6–8 weeks
NocardiaTMP-SMX high-dose + Meropenem/Imipenem + Amikacin12 months (prolonged)
AspergillusVoriconazole 6mg/kg 12h ×2d → 4mg/kg 12h (TDM)8–12 weeks
ToxoplasmaPyrimethamine 75mg load → 25–50mg OD + Sulfadiazine 1–1.5g 6h + Folinic acid 10–25mg OD6 weeks after resolution (then secondary prophylaxis)

Surgical Management

ApproachIndications
Stereotactic aspirationPreferred for deep, eloquent, single, accessible abscess; diagnostic + therapeutic
Craniotomy + excisionMultiloculated, superficial, failed aspiration, cerebellar abscess (herniation risk), fungal (debulking)
Burr hole drainageLimited access; less common now
Ventriculostomy (EVD)Intraventricular rupture → ventriculitis, hydrocephalus
[!tip]
Stereotactic aspiration = lower morbidity, shorter stay, diagnostic yield. Total excision for fungal, multiloculated, cerebellar. Antibiotics continue 6–8w post-drainage.

Duration & Monitoring

ParameterSchedule
Antibiotics IV6–8 weeks total (including pre-drainage empirical)
Oral step-downSometimes used for last 2–4w (e.g., metronidazole PO, co-amoxiclav, linezolid PO) if clinical/radiological improvement
MRI/CTBaseline, 1–2w post-drainage, then 2–4w intervals until resolution
CRP, FBC, LFTsWeekly
Vancomycin trough2–3x/week (target 15–20 for CNS)
Seizure prophylaxisLevetiracetam during acute phase; continue if seizures occurred

Complications

ComplicationManagement
Rupture into ventricleVentriculitis, hydrocephalus, high mortality → EVD + intraventricular antibiotics (vancomycin, gentamicin, amphotericin) + systemic antibiotics
Raised ICP / HerniationMannitol, hypertonic saline, hyperventilation, dexamethasone (controversial — may reduce capsule formation), surgical decompression
SeizuresLevetiracetam; long-term AED if recurrent
RecurrenceRepeat imaging, re-aspiration, extended antibiotics
Empyema (subdural/epidural)Surgical drainage + antibiotics
Neurological deficitsRehabilitation; may be permanent

Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Rapid neurosurgical deterioration (GCS drop, pupil asymmetry) → herniation → emergency decompression
  • Intraventricular rupture → sudden deterioration, meningism, hydrocephalus → EVD + intraventricular antibiotics
  • Cerebellar abscess → high risk of tonsillar herniation → urgent posterior fossa decompression
  • Septic shock (haematogenous source) → fluids, vasopressors, source control

Differential Diagnosis (Ring-Enhancing Lesions)

ConditionKey Differentiators
Brain abscessDWI restriction (pus), MRS: lactate/acetate/succinate, smooth thin ring, clinical infection
Necrotic tumour (glioblastoma, metastasis)Irregular thick wall, MRS: choline↑, NAA↓, lipids; no DWI restriction
Toxoplasmosis (HIV CD4<100)Multiple, basal ganglia, IgG +ve, responds to anti-Toxoplasma Rx in 2w
TB (tuberculoma)Subacute, basal exudates, CSF low glucose, GeneXpert +ve, responds to anti-TB
Fungal (Aspergillus, Mucorales)Immunocompromised, haemorrhagic, angioinvasion, galactomannan/CrAg
NocardiaImmunocompromised, multiple, beading on imaging, TMP-SMX responsive
Demyelination (tumefactive MS)Open ring, perivenular, CSF oligoclonal bands, no infection markers
Primary CNS lymphoma (immunocompromised)Homogeneous enhancement (if not treated with steroids), EBV +ve CSF
Infected aneurysm / mycotic aneurysmVascular location, CT angiography, blood cultures +ve

Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
PregnancyCeftriaxone + metronidazole safe; avoid fluoroquinolones; vancomycin OK
Renal impairmentAdjust vancomycin, aminoglycosides, meropenem; ceftriaxone no adjustment
Hepatic impairmentMetronidazole caution; ceftriaxone (biliary excretion)
Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis)Meropenem + vancomycin (avoid cephalosporins if true IgE)
HIV CD4<100Empirical anti-Toxoplasma + broad antibacterial if multiple ring-enhancing; brain biopsy if no response in 2w

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Brain abscess = ring-enhancing lesion + DWI restriction + MRS (lactate, acetate, succinate)
  • Sources: contiguous (sinus/ear/dental → anaerobes + streptococci), haematogenous (endocarditis, CHD, IVDU → multiple, MCA territory), post-trauma/surgery (S. aureus, Gram-neg)
  • Empirical abx: Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Vancomycin (covers streptococci, anaerobes, staphylococci)
  • Stereotactic aspiration preferred — diagnostic + therapeutic
  • Duration: 6–8 weeks IV antibiotics after drainage
  • CSF usually normal — DO NOT LP if mass effect/papilloedema (herniation risk)
  • Intraventricular rupture = catastrophe → ventriculitis, hydrocephalus, EVD + intraventricular antibiotics
  • Toxoplasma in HIV CD4<100: multiple ring-enhancing, basal ganglia, empirical pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine
  • Nocardia: immunocompromised, TMP-SMX high-dose, 12-month duration
  • Cerebellar abscess: high herniation risk → urgent posterior fossa decompression

Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the classic imaging triad for brain abscess? Ring-enhancing lesion, DWI restriction (pus), MR spectroscopy showing lactate/acetate/succinate peaks.
  2. What are the three main routes of infection? Contiguous spread (sinus/ear/dental), haematogenous (endocarditis, CHD, IVDU), post-trauma/surgery.
  3. What is the empirical antibiotic regimen for a community-acquired brain abscess? Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Metronidazole 500mg 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h.
  4. Why is lumbar puncture contraindicated in brain abscess? Mass effect, risk of tonsillar/uncal herniation.
  5. What is the treatment duration? 6–8 weeks IV antibiotics after surgical drainage.
  6. How do you differentiate brain abscess from necrotic tumour on MRI? DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate = abscess; choline↑/NAA↓ + irregular thick wall = tumour.
  7. What is the management of intraventricular rupture? EVD + intraventricular antibiotics (vancomycin, gentamicin) + systemic antibiotics.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
LP safe in brain abscessCONTRAINDICATED if mass effect, midline shift, papilloedema, GCS<10
Single antibiotic sufficientPolymicrobial — need streptococcal, anaerobic, staphylococcal coverage
Duration same as meningitis6–8 weeks IV (meningitis 10–14d for pneumococcus)
Aspiration not needed if antibiotics startedSurgical drainage essential — antibiotics alone fail in encapsulated abscess
All ring-enhancing = abscessDWI restriction + MRS distinguishes; tumour, TB, Toxoplasma, fungal, lymphoma mimic
Toxoplasma only in HIVAlso in transplant, steroids; but #1 in HIV CD4<100
Nocardia = short course12 months (prolonged)
Dexamethasone routine in abscessNOT routine — may impair capsule formation; only for raised ICP/herniation

Mnemonics

  • BRAIN ABSCESS: Blood cultures first, Ring-enhancing + DWI restriction + MRS lactate, Anaerobes + Strep + Staph, Intracranial source (sinus/ear), Neurosurgical aspiration
  • MRS ABScess: Acetate, Bsuccinate, SLactate = ABS (NO NAA, NO creatine, NO choline)
  • SOURCES: Sinus/ear/dental (contiguous), Oprehaematogenous (endocarditis, CHD), Utrauma/surgery, RIVDU, Cryptic, Endocarditis, Septic emboli

Mind Map

Brain Abscess
Pathogenesis
Cerebritis (1-3d) -> Capsule (10-14d) -> Mature abscess
Routes
Contiguous (sinus/ear/dental) -> anaerobes + strep
Haematogenous (endocarditis, CHD, IVDU) -> multiple, MCA
Trauma/surgery -> Staph + Gram-neg
Immunocompromised -> Toxo, Nocardia, fungal, TB
Imaging
Ring-enhancing, smooth thin wall
DWI RESTRICTION (pus)
MRS: lactate, acetate, succinate (NO NAA, creatine, choline)
Treatment
Aspiration (stereotactic preferred)
Empirical: Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Vancomycin
Targeted 6-8w IV
Complications
Ventricular rupture -> ventriculitis, EVD + intraventricular abx
Raised ICP, herniation
Seizures
Recurrence

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Headache + fever + focal deficit/seizure
↓
MRI brain: ring-enhancing + DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate
↓
Blood cultures x2-3
↓
Empirical IV abx by source
↓
Neurosurgical review: stereotactic aspiration
↓
Aspirate: Gram, culture, PCR, 16S
↓
Targeted IV abx 6-8 weeks
↓
Serial MRI: size reduction, oedema resolution
↓
Oral step-down if improving

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • MRI: ring-enhancement, DWI restriction, MRS spectra (abscess vs tumour)
  • CT: ring-enhancement with oedema
  • Stereotactic aspiration procedure
  • Intraventricular rupture appearance

Suggested Video References

  • Brain abscess imaging (RSNA/ECR)
  • Stereotactic aspiration technique
  • Intraventricular antibiotic administration
  • Toxoplasma encephalitis in HIV

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
ImagingRing-enhancing + DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate
SourcesContiguous (sinus/ear/dental: anaerobes+strep), Haematogenous (endocarditis/CHD/IVDU: multiple), Trauma/surgery (Staph+Gram-neg)
Empirical abxCeftriaxone 2g12h + Metronidazole 500mg8h + Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg6h
SurgeryStereotactic aspiration (preferred); craniotomy if multiloculated/cerebellar/fungal
Duration6–8 weeks IV total
LPContraindicated if mass effect/papilloedema/GCS<10
Rupture into ventricleCatastrophe → EVD + intraventricular abx + systemic
HIV CD4<100Toxoplasma #1: multiple, basal ganglia; empirical pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine
NocardiaTMP-SMX high-dose + meropenem/amikacin ×12 months
Cerebellar abscessUrgent posterior fossa decompression (herniation risk)

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • Name the 3 routes of brain abscess infection.
  • Write the empirical antibiotic regimen.
  • What is the MRI signature of brain abscess (3 features)?
  • Why is LP contraindicated?
  • Duration of treatment?

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Imaging: ring-enhancement + DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate
  • 3 sources: contiguous, haematogenous, post-trauma/surgery
  • Empirical: Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Vancomycin
  • Stereotactic aspiration preferred
  • 6–8 weeks IV antibiotics
  • LP contraindicated with mass effect
  • Ventricular rupture = EVD + intraventricular abx

Should Know

  • Pathogen by source
  • Targeted regimens (Staph, anaerobes, Gram-neg, Nocardia, Toxoplasma, fungal)
  • Cerebellar abscess urgency
  • Differentiation from tumour, TB, Toxoplasma, lymphoma
  • Seizure prophylaxis
  • Oral step-down options

Nice to Know

  • MR spectroscopy details
  • Intraventricular antibiotic dosing
  • Paediatric considerations
  • Outcome predictors
  • Role of dexamethasone (controversial)

My Weak Points

  • [ ] Intraventricular antibiotic exact doses
  • [ ] Nocardia exact combination and duration
  • [ ] Toxoplasma prophylaxis after treatment
  • [ ] Cerebellar abscess surgical timing

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Definition, pathogenesis (cerebritis → capsule)
  2. Aetiology by route (contiguous, haematogenous, trauma, immunocompromised)
  3. Clinical features
  4. Imaging: CT/MRI, DWI, MRS (key differentiators)
  5. Investigations: blood cultures, aspirate, serology, source search
  6. Empirical antibiotics by scenario
  7. Surgical management: aspiration vs craniotomy
  8. Targeted therapy, duration (6–8w IV)
  9. Complications: ventricular rupture, raised ICP, seizures, recurrence
  10. Special populations: immunocompromised (Toxoplasma, Nocardia, fungal), HIV

Short Note Skeleton

  • Ring-enhancing + DWI restrict + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate = abscess
  • Sources: contiguous (anaerobes+strep), haematogenous (multiple), trauma (Staph)
  • Empirical: Ceftriaxone + Metro + Vanco
  • Aspiration (stereotactic) + targeted 6-8w IV
  • LP contraindicated (herniation risk)
  • Ventricular rupture → EVD + intraventricular abx
  • HIV CD4<100: Toxoplasma empirical
  • Nocardia: TMP-SMX high-dose ×12m

Viva One-Liners

  • Brain abscess = ring-enhancing + DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate
  • 3 sources: contiguous (sinus/ear), haematogenous (endocarditis), trauma/surgery
  • Empirical: Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Vancomycin
  • Aspiration essential; 6-8w IV
  • No LP if mass effect
  • Ventricular rupture = catastrophe → EVD + intraventricular abx
  • HIV CD4<100: Toxoplasma empirical

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 40M, frontal headache, fever, right hemiparesis → MRI frontal ring-enhancing + DWI restrict → sinusitis hx → aspiration + Ceftriaxone/Metro/Vanco → cultures: S. milleri + anaerobes → targeted 6w
  • HIV CD4 50, multiple ring-enhancing basal ganglia → empirical pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine + broad abx → Toxoplasma IgG +ve → continue anti-Toxoplasma 6w post-resolution
  • Post-craniotomy day 10, fever, wound breakdown → MRI ring-enhancing → aspiration + Vancomycin + Meropenem → MRSA + Pseudomonas

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

BRAIN ABSCESS: Ring-enhancing + DWI restrict + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate. Sources: contiguous (sinus/ear/dental → anaerobes+strep), haematogenous (endocarditis/CHD/IVDU → multiple), trauma/surgery (Staph+Gram-neg). Empirical: Ceftriaxone+Metro+Vanco. Stereotactic aspiration. 6-8w IV. NO LP if mass effect. Ventricular rupture → EVD + intraventricular abx. HIV CD4<100: Toxoplasma empirical (pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine). Nocardia: TMP-SMX high-dose ×12m. Cerebellar → urgent decompression.

Summary

Brain abscess is a focal suppurative parenchymal infection presenting subacutely with headache, fever, focal deficits, seizures. Imaging hallmark: ring-enhancing lesion with smooth thin wall, surrounding oedema, DWI restriction (pus viscosity), and MR spectroscopy showing lactate, acetate, succinate peaks (NO NAA, creatine, choline). Three main routes: (1) Contiguous spread (sinusitis→frontal, otitis→temporal/cerebellar, dental) → anaerobes + streptococci; (2) Haematogenous (endocarditis, cyanotic CHD, IVDU, pulmonary AVM) → multiple abscesses, MCA territory; (3) Post-trauma/surgery → S. aureus, Gram-negatives. Empirical antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2g 12h + Metronidazole 500mg 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h (covers streptococci, anaerobes, staphylococci). Stereotactic aspiration preferred (diagnostic + therapeutic). Duration: 6–8 weeks IV antibiotics total. LP CONTRAINDICATED if mass effect/papilloedema/GCS<10. Intraventricular rupture = catastrophe → EVD + intraventricular antibiotics (vancomycin, gentamicin) + systemic. HIV CD4<100: Toxoplasma #1 (multiple, basal ganglia) → empirical pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid. Nocardia: immunocompromised, TMP-SMX high-dose + meropenem/amikacin ×12 months. Cerebellar abscess: high herniation risk → urgent posterior fossa decompression.

MCQs (10)

  1. What is the classic MR spectroscopy finding in a brain abscess?

A. Choline ↑, NAA ↓, creatine ↑

B. Lactate ↑, acetate ↑, succinate ↑; NO NAA, NO creatine, NO choline

C. Lipids ↑, lactate ↑, NAA normal

D. Alanine ↑, choline ↑, NAA ↓

E. Normal spectrum

  1. DWI restriction in a ring-enhancing lesion indicates:

A. Tumour necrosis

B. Pus (high viscosity) — brain abscess

C. Acute infarct

D. Demyelination

E. Haemorrhage

  1. A 30-year-old man with frontal sinusitis presents with headache, fever, and right hemiparesis. MRI shows a 3cm right frontal ring-enhancing lesion with DWI restriction. Blood cultures growing Streptococcus milleri group. Best empirical regimen while awaiting aspirate culture?

A. Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h alone

B. Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h

C. Vancomycin 15mg/kg IV 12h + Meropenem 2g IV 8h

D. Penicillin G 24MU/day IV + Metronidazole

E. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin (no metronidazole)

  1. Which is a CONTRAINDICATION to lumbar puncture in brain abscess?

A. Mild headache

B. Papilloedema

C. Fever >39°C

D. Seizure history

E. Age >60

  1. Duration of IV antibiotic therapy for brain abscess after surgical drainage:

A. 2–3 weeks

B. 6–8 weeks

C. 10–14 days

D. 12 weeks

D. Until CRP normalises

  1. A patient with brain abscess deteriorates suddenly with headache, vomiting, meningism, and dropping GCS. CT shows intraventricular extension of pus. Management?

A. Increase IV antibiotics only

B. EVD + intraventricular vancomycin + gentamicin + systemic antibiotics

C. Urgent craniotomy for total excision

D. Dexamethasone 10mg IV 6h

E. Repeat LP for CSF analysis

  1. Toxoplasma encephalitis in HIV (CD4<100): typical imaging?

A. Single frontal ring-enhancing lesion

B. Multiple ring-enhancing lesions, basal ganglia predilection

C. Diffuse white matter enhancement

D. Cerebellar lesion only

E. Normal MRI

  1. Nocardia brain abscess: first-line treatment and duration?

A. Ceftriaxone ×6 weeks

B. TMP-SMX high-dose + Meropenem/Imipenem + Amikacin ×12 months

C. Vancomycin + Meropenem ×8 weeks

D. Linezolid ×6 weeks

E. Doxycycline ×12 months

  1. Cerebellar brain abscess: why is it particularly dangerous?

A. High seizure risk

B. Risk of tonsillar herniation and brainstem compression

C. Poor antibiotic penetration

D. Always fungal

E. No surgical access

  1. Differentiation of brain abscess from necrotic glioblastoma on MRI:

A. Both show DWI restriction

B. Abscess: DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate; Tumour: irregular thick wall, MRS choline↑/NAA↓

C. Tumour has smooth thin wall

D. Absence of oedema favours tumour

E. MRS identical in both

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 45-year-old woman, 2 weeks post-craniotomy for meningioma, develops fever, headache, and new left hemiparesis. MRI shows a 2.5cm right frontal ring-enhancing lesion with DWI restriction. Empirical regimen?

A. Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole

B. Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h + Meropenem 2g IV 8h

C. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin + Metronidazole

D. Penicillin + Gentamicin

E. Meropenem alone

  1. An IV drug user with known tricuspid valve endocarditis (MSSA) presents with multiple ring-enhancing lesions in MCA territories. Blood cultures positive for MSSA. Best antibiotic regimen?

A. Vancomycin + Meropenem

B. Flucloxacillin 2g IV 4h (or Cefazolin) + Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole

C. Vancomycin + Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole

D. Daptomycin + Ceftriaxone

E. Linezolid + Meropenem

  1. A 35-year-old man with HIV (CD4 60, not on ART) presents with 2 weeks confusion, fever, and right hemiparesis. MRI: multiple ring-enhancing lesions in basal ganglia and cortex. Toxoplasma IgG positive. Empirical treatment while arranging brain biopsy?

A. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin + Metronidazole

B. Pyrimethamine 75mg load → 50mg OD + Sulfadiazine 1.5g 6h + Folinic acid 25mg OD + broad antibacterial cover

C. High-dose dexamethasone + anti-TB

D. Aciclovir + Ceftriaxone

E. Voriconazole + Amphotericin B

  1. A patient with brain abscess on Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Vancomycin develops neutropenia (neutrophils 0.8) on week 3. Most likely culprit?

A. Ceftriaxone

B. Metronidazole

C. Vancomycin

D. All equally likely

E. None (unrelated to antibiotics)

  1. Stereotactic aspiration vs craniotomy for brain abscess: which is TRUE?

A. Craniotomy always preferred for better visualisation

B. Stereotactic aspiration preferred for deep, eloquent, single abscess; lower morbidity

C. Aspiration has higher recurrence than excision

D. Craniotomy required for all fungal abscesses

E. Aspiration contraindicated in multiloculated abscess

  1. Intraventricular antibiotics for ventriculitis post-abscess rupture: standard regimen includes:

A. Vancomycin 10mg IV daily via EVD + Ceftriaxone IV

B. Vancomycin 10–20mg IV daily via EVD + Gentamicin 1–2mg IV daily via EVD + systemic antibiotics

C. Meropenem only via EVD

D. No intraventricular antibiotics needed if systemic given

E. Amphotericin B only via EVD

  1. A 25-year-old man with tetralogy of Fallot (cyanotic CHD) presents with brain abscess. Most likely route?

A. Contiguous sinusitis

B. Haematogenous (right-to-left shunt bypasses pulmonary filtration)

C. Post-traumatic

D. Dental spread

E. Cryptogenic

  1. Metronidazole in brain abscess empirical regimen covers:

A. Staphylococcus aureus

B. Streptococcus pneumoniae

C. Anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella, Peptostreptococcus)

D. Gram-negative bacilli

E. Nocardia

  1. Which immunocompromised patient group has the HIGHEST risk of Toxoplasma encephalitis?

A. Renal transplant on tacrolimus

B. HIV CD4 <100 (not on prophylaxis)

C. Rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate

D. Post-splenectomy

E. Diabetes mellitus

  1. Oral step-down therapy for brain abscess (after clinical/radiological improvement on IV):

A. Always required for full 6-8 weeks

B. Metronidazole PO 400mg 8h + Co-amoxiclav 1.2g 8h (or Linezolid 600mg 12h PO) for last 2-4 weeks

C. Switch all to PO at 2 weeks

D. Only fluoroquinolone PO

E. No oral options available

Flashcards

  • Q: Brain abscess imaging triad

A: Ring-enhancing + DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate

  • Q: 3 routes of infection

A: Contiguous (sinus/ear/dental), Haematogenous (endocarditis/CHD/IVDU), Trauma/surgery

  • Q: Empirical antibiotics

A: Ceftriaxone 2g12h + Metronidazole 500mg8h + Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg6h

  • Q: LP contraindication

A: Mass effect, midline shift, papilloedema, GCS<10 → herniation risk

  • Q: Duration IV antibiotics

A: 6-8 weeks total

  • Q: Ventricular rupture management

A: EVD + intraventricular vancomycin + gentamicin + systemic abx

  • Q: HIV CD4<100 + ring-enhancing =

A: Toxoplasma empirical (pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine/folinic acid)

  • Q: Nocardia treatment

A: TMP-SMX high-dose + meropenem/amikacin ×12 months

  • Q: Cerebellar abscess

A: Urgent posterior fossa decompression (herniation risk)

  • Q: Abscess vs tumour MRS

A: Abscess = lactate/acetate/succinate (NO NAA/creatine/choline); Tumour = choline↑/NAA↓

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Brain abscess MRS: lactate, acetate, succinate peaks; absence of normal brain metabolites (NAA, creatine, choline). Tumour shows choline↑, NAA↓.
  2. B — DWI restriction = high viscosity fluid (pus) — hallmark of brain abscess. Tumour necrosis typically not restricted.
  3. B — Contiguous spread from sinusitis → streptococci + anaerobes → Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Vancomycin covers Strep, anaerobes, Staph.
  4. B — Papilloedema = raised ICP = mass effect = contraindication to LP (herniation risk).
  5. B — Standard duration 6–8 weeks IV after drainage. Longer than meningitis.
  6. B — Intraventricular rupture → ventriculitis → EVD + intraventricular vancomycin (10–20mg daily) + gentamicin (1–2mg daily) + systemic antibiotics.
  7. B — Toxoplasma in HIV CD4<100: multiple ring-enhancing lesions, basal ganglia predilection.
  8. B — Nocardia: TMP-SMX high-dose (sulfonamide backbone) + meropenem/imipenem + amikacin for 12 months (prolonged).
  9. B — Cerebellar abscess → risk of tonsillar herniation through foramen magnum → urgent decompression.
  10. B — Abscess: DWI restriction + MRS lactate/acetate/succinate. Tumour: irregular thick wall, MRS choline↑/NAA↓.

SBAs

  1. B — Post-neurosurgery (<1 month): MRSA + Gram-negatives (Pseudomonas) → Vancomycin + Meropenem.
  2. B — MSSA endocarditis → haematogenous septic emboli → MSSA covered by flucloxacillin/cefazolin; add ceftriaxone + metronidazole for possible polymicrobial.
  3. B — HIV CD4<100 + multiple ring-enhancing basal ganglia + Toxo IgG +ve → empirical anti-Toxoplasma (pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine/folinic acid) + broad antibacterial.
  4. C — Vancomycin can cause neutropenia (usually after 1–2 weeks); monitor CBC weekly.
  5. B — Stereotactic aspiration preferred for deep, eloquent, single abscess; lower morbidity, diagnostic yield. Craniotomy for multiloculated, superficial, cerebellar, fungal.
  6. B — Intraventricular antibiotics: vancomycin 10–20mg daily + gentamicin 1–2mg daily via EVD + systemic antibiotics.
  7. B — Cyanotic CHD (right-to-left shunt) → haematogenous spread bypassing pulmonary filter.
  8. C — Metronidazole = anaerobic coverage (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella, Peptostreptococcus).
  9. B — HIV CD4<100 not on prophylaxis = highest Toxoplasma risk. Transplant also at risk but lower incidence.
  10. B — Oral step-down after improvement: metronidazole PO + co-amoxiclav/linezolid PO for last 2–4 weeks.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.06 Brucellosis

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, brucellosis, brucella, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Zoonotic Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Brucellosis

Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Osteoarticular Infections]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Q Fever]], [[TB in HIV]]

[!important]
Brucellosis = zoonosis from Brucella spp. (B. melitensis, B. abortus, B. suis, B. canis). Transmission: unpasteurised dairy, animal contact, aerosols. Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly. Culture = gold standard (slow); serology (SAT/Brucella IgG/IgM) = standard. Treatment: Doxycycline + Rifampicin ×6w (acute); doxycycline + rifampicin + aminoglycoside ×3m (endocarditis/CNS). Relapse 5–15%.

Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical presentations (acute, subacute, chronic, focal)
  • Apply diagnostic criteria (culture, serology, PCR)
  • Select appropriate combination therapy by disease form
  • Manage complications (endocarditis, neurobrucellosis, osteoarticular)
  • Apply prevention (pasteurisation, animal vaccination, occupational hygiene)

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetails
AgentsBrucella spp.: B. melitensis (goats/sheep — most virulent), B. abortus (cattle), B. suis (pigs), B. canis (dogs)
ReservoirsDomestic livestock (sheep, goats, cattle, pigs); wildlife
TransmissionUnpasteurised dairy (milk, cheese, butter); Direct contact with infected tissues/fluids (placenta, fetus, blood); Aerosol inhalation (lab, abattoir); Rare: human-to-human (sexual, breastfeeding, transplant)
Risk GroupsFarmers, veterinarians, abattoir workers, lab workers, travellers consuming unpasteurised dairy
GeographyMediterranean, Middle East, Central Asia, Latin America, India (endemic)

Clinical Manifestations

FormFeatures
Acute/SubacuteUndulant fever (spiking fever, remission, recurrence), arthralgia/myalgia, headache, sweats, fatigue, hepatosplenomegaly
Focal/ComplicatedOsteoarticular (sacroiliitis, spondylitis, peripheral arthritis — 20–60%); Endocarditis (aortic valve, high mortality); Neurobrucellosis (meningoencephalitis, radiculopathy); Genitourinary (orchitis, epididymitis); Hepatic/Splenic (abscesses)
ChronicPersistent fatigue, depression, arthralgia >1 year; often misdiagnosed as CFS
[!key]
Osteoarticular = most common focal (20–60%); sacroiliitis characteristic. Endocarditis = most serious (aortic valve, culture-negative). Neurobrucellosis = meningitic/encephalitic; CSF lymphocytic, ↑ protein, low glucose.

Diagnosis

TestSensitivity/SpecificityTimingNotes
Blood CultureGold standard; 50–70% (acute), <30% (chronic)Weeks 1–3Castaneda biphasic media; slow (3–7 days); BSL-3
Bone Marrow Culture90%Any timeHighest yield if antibiotics given
Standard Agglutination Test (SAT)80–95% / 95%Week 2+≥1:160 = positive; ≥1:320 = active; prozone phenomenon
Coombs Anti-BrucellaDetects incomplete (IgG) antibodiesChronic/relapseHigher titre than SAT
Brucella IgG/IgM ELISAHigh sensitivityWeek 1+IgM = acute; IgG = past/chronic; Lack of standardisation
Brucellacapt (Lateral Flow)High sensitivity/specificityAcute/chronicDetects IgG+IgM+IgA; quantitative
PCR (Blood/Tissue)High sensitivityEarlyRapid; not universally available
CSF Analysis (Neuro)Lymphocytic, ↑ protein, low glucoseNeurobrucellosisPCR + culture + serology
[!key]
Culture = gold standard but slow; SAT ≥1:160 + compatible clinical = probable; SAT ≥1:320 + clinical = confirmed. Bone marrow culture > blood culture. PCR = rapid adjunct.

Treatment

Acute/Uncomplicated Brucellosis (Adults)

RegimenDoseDurationNotes
Doxycycline + RifampicinDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h + Rifampicin 600mg PO OD6 weeks1st-line (WHO); reload if relapse
Alternative (pregnancy/children <8)TMP-SMX 160/800mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD6 weeksDoxycycline contraindicated

Complicated/Focal Brucellosis

SyndromeRegimenDuration
EndocarditisDoxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD + Gentamicin 5mg/kg IV OD (first 2w)3–6 months (valve replacement often needed)
NeurobrucellosisDoxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD + TMP-SMX DS 12h3–6 months
Osteoarticular (Sacroiliitis/Spondylitis)Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD3–6 months
CNS/Endocarditis (Doxycycline contraindicated)TMP-SMX DS 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD + Gentamicin (2w)3–6 months
[!key]
Doxycycline + Rifampicin ×6w = standard for uncomplicated. Endocarditis/CNS = add aminoglycoside (first 2w) + prolong to 3–6m. Relapse 5–15% — retreat with same or alternative regimen.

Pregnancy/Children

PopulationRegimen
PregnancyTMP-SMX DS 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD (avoid doxycycline)
Children <8 yearsTMP-SMX 8mg/kg TMP 12h + Rifampicin 15mg/kg OD
Children ≥8 yearsDoxycycline 2.2mg/kg 12h + Rifampicin 15mg/kg OD

Complications

ComplicationFrequencyManagement
Osteoarticular20–60%Doxy + Rifampicin ×3–6m; sacroiliitis → MRI; joint aspiration if septic
Endocarditis1–2%Doxy + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (2w) ×3–6m; valve replacement often needed
Neurobrucellosis<5%Doxy + Rifampicin + TMP-SMX ×3–6m; steroids if meningoencephalitis
Genitourinary2–20% (orchitis/epididymitis)Standard regimen; scrotal support
Hepatosplenic AbscessRareDrainage + prolonged antibiotics
Relapse5–15%Retreat with same/alternative regimen

Prevention

MeasureDetails
PasteurisationMilk, cheese, dairy products — most effective
Animal VaccinationRB51 (cattle), Rev-1 (sheep/goats) — reduces animal prevalence
Occupational HygienePPE (gloves, goggles, N95) for high-risk workers; lab safety (BSL-3)
Food SafetyAvoid unpasteurised dairy (raw milk, soft cheese); cook meat thoroughly
Animal ControlTest-and-slaughter; quarantine; movement restrictions

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Brucellosis: zoonosis from unpasteurised dairy/animal contact; B. melitensis (goats) most virulent
  • Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly; sacroiliitis (characteristic)
  • Diagnosis: Blood/bone marrow culture (gold); SAT ≥1:160; Brucella IgG/IgM ELISA
  • Acute treatment: Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6w
  • Endocarditis/Neuro/CNS: Doxy + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (2w) ×3–6m
  • Pregnancy/children <8: TMP-SMX + Rifampicin (doxy contraindicated)
  • Relapse 5–15%; retreat with same/alternative regimen
  • Prevention: pasteurisation, animal vaccination (RB51, Rev-1)

Common Viva Questions

  1. Most common Brucella species causing human disease? B. melitensis (goats/sheep) — most virulent.
  2. Classic clinical triad of brucellosis? Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly.
  3. Most common focal complication? Osteoarticular (sacroiliitis, spondylitis) — 20–60%.
  4. Diagnostic gold standard? Blood/bone marrow culture (bone marrow > blood).
  5. Standard agglutination test (SAT) cut-off? ≥1:160 positive; ≥1:320 active disease.
  6. Treatment for uncomplicated brucellosis? Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6w.
  7. Treatment for brucellar endocarditis? Doxycycline + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (first 2w) ×3–6m.
  8. Treatment in pregnancy/children? TMP-SMX + Rifampicin (doxycycline contraindicated).
  9. Relapse rate? 5–15%; retreat with same/alternative regimen.
  10. Prevention? Pasteurisation of dairy; animal vaccination (RB51, Rev-1).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
SAT negative = no brucellosisProzone phenomenon (high antibody = false low); do serial dilutions / Coombs
Doxycycline safe in pregnancyCONTRAINDICATED (bone/teeth); use TMP-SMX + Rifampicin
Rifampicin monotherapyNever — rapid resistance; always combine
Doxycycline + Rifampicin = 2 weeks6 weeks minimum for uncomplicated; 3–6m for focal
Brucella endocarditis = same as other culture-negativeAortic valve predominant; high mortality; needs prolonged combo + surgery
SAT single titre diagnosticNeed rising titre (4-fold) or ≥1:320 + clinical
Doxycycline alone sufficientMonotherapy = relapse/resistance; always combine with Rifampicin
Chronic fatigue = brucellosisPost-brucellosis fatigue syndrome = exclusion diagnosis

Mnemonics

  • BRUCELLOSIS: Brucella; Ruminants (goats/sheep/cattle); Unpasteurised dairy; Cattle/Sheep/Goats; Endocarditis; Long treatment (6w+); Low relapse (5-15%); Osteoarticular (sacroiliitis); Serology SAT; Intracellular; Synergy (Doxy+Rif)
  • SAT: Standard Agglutination Test; ≥1:160 = positive; ≥1:320 = active; Prozone = false negative
  • TREATMENT: Doxy + Rifampicin 6w = DR6; Endocarditis = Doxy + Rif + Genta 18m
  • PREGNANCY: Doxy NO; TMP-SMX + Rif = TSR
  • SACROILIITIS: Sacroiliitis = Brucellosis hallmark
  • RELAPSE: 5-15% = Retreat same or Alternative

Mind Map

Brucellosis
Epidemiology
Zoonosis: B. melitensis (goats), B. abortus (cattle), B. suis (pigs)
Unpasteurised dairy, animal contact, aerosol
Clinical
Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly
Focal: Sacroiliitis (20-60%), Endocarditis, Neurobrucellosis
Diagnosis
Culture: Blood (50-70%), Bone marrow (90%)
Serology: SAT ≥1:160, Coombs, ELISA, Brucellacapt
Treatment
Uncomplicated: Doxy + Rifampicin 6w
Endocarditis: Doxy + Rif + Genta (2w) 18m
Neuro: Doxy + Rif + TMP-SMX 3-6m
Pregnancy/Kids: TMP-SMX + Rifampicin
Complications
Sacroiliitis, Endocarditis, Neurobrucellosis
Relapse 5-15%
Prevention
Pasteurisation, Animal vaccination (RB51, Rev-1)

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Brucellosis
↓
Blood Culture + Bone Marrow Culture + SAT
↓
SAT ≥1:160 + Clinical?
↓
Probable Brucellosis
↓
Treatment by Syndrome
↓
Form
↓
Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6w
Doxy + Rif + Genta (2w) ×18m
Doxy + Rif + TMP-SMX 3-6m
Doxy + Rif ×3-6m
TMP-SMX + Rifampicin 6w
↓
Follow-up: Relapse? → Retreat

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • Brucella culture (Castaneda bottle)
  • SAT titration curve (prozone)
  • Sacroiliitis MRI (sacroiliitis)
  • Brucellar endocarditis (vegetation on aortic valve)
  • Brucella organism (small coccobacilli)

Suggested Video References

  • Brucellosis diagnosis and management
  • Sacroiliitis in brucellosis
  • Brucellar endocarditis
  • Brucellosis prevention

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
AgentBrucella spp.: B. melitensis (goats, most virulent), B. abortus (cattle), B. suis (pigs)
TransmissionUnpasteurised dairy, animal contact, aerosol
ClinicalUndulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly
FocalSacroiliitis (20–60%), Endocarditis (aortic), Neurobrucellosis
DiagnosisCulture (blood 50–70%, bone marrow 90%); SAT ≥1:160; ELISA; PCR
Uncomplicated RxDoxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6w
EndocarditisDoxy + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (2w) ×18m
Neuro/EndocarditisDoxy + Rif + Genta/TMP-SMX ×3–6m
Pregnancy/KidsTMP-SMX + Rifampicin (doxy contraindicated)
Relapse5–15% (retreat same/alternative)
PreventionPasteurisation, animal vaccination (RB51, Rev-1)

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • Classic triad: undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly.
  • Most common species: B. melitensis (goats).
  • Focal: sacroiliitis > endocarditis > neuro.
  • Diagnosis: bone marrow culture > blood culture; SAT ≥1:160.
  • Acute Rx: Doxy + Rifampicin ×6w.
  • Endocarditis: Doxy + Rif + Genta (2w) ×18m.
  • Pregnancy/children: TMP-SMX + Rifampicin (no doxy).
  • Relapse 5–15%.
  • Prevention: pasteurisation, animal vaccination.

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly
  • Sacroiliitis (characteristic focal)
  • Bone marrow culture > blood culture
  • SAT ≥1:160; Coombs for prozone
  • Doxy + Rifampicin ×6w (standard)
  • Endocarditis: Doxy + Rif + Genta ×18m
  • Pregnancy: TMP-SMX + Rifampicin

Should Know

  • Neurobrucellosis CSF findings
  • Relapse management
  • Brucellacapt test
  • Animal vaccines (RB51, Rev-1)
  • Chronic fatigue syndrome post-brucellosis

Nice to Know

  • B. canis (dogs) — mild disease
  • B. microti (rodents)
  • New PCR panels
  • Bioterrorism potential
  • Chronic fatigue syndrome differentiation

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Epidemiology, transmission, species
  2. Clinical features (acute, focal, chronic)
  3. Diagnosis (culture, serology, PCR)
  4. Treatment by syndrome (uncomplicated, endocarditis, neuro, osteoarticular, pregnancy)
  5. Complications and relapse
  6. Prevention (pasteurisation, animal vaccination)

Short Note Skeleton

  • B. melitensis (goats); unpasteurised dairy; aerosol
  • Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly
  • Focal: sacroiliitis (20-60%), endocarditis, neuro
  • Culture: bone marrow > blood; SAT ≥1:160
  • Acute: Doxy + Rif 6w
  • Endocarditis: Doxy + Rif + Genta 18m
  • Neuro: Doxy + Rif + TMP-SMX 3-6m
  • Pregnancy/kids: TMP-SMX + Rifampicin
  • Relapse 5-15%
  • Prevention: pasteurisation, animal vaccines

Viva One-Liners

  • Brucella: goats/sheep/cattle; unpasteurised dairy
  • Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly
  • Sacroiliitis = hallmark focal
  • Culture: bone marrow > blood
  • Doxy + Rif 6w standard
  • Endocarditis = Doxy + Rif + Genta 18m
  • Pregnancy: no doxy; TMP-SMX + Rif
  • Pasteurisation prevents

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Farmer, fever, back pain, sacroiliitis on MRI → Brucella serology + blood culture → Doxy + Rif 6w
  • Prosthetic valve endocarditis, culture-negative, goat farmer → Brucella serology → Doxy + Rif + Genta 18m + valve surgery
  • Pregnant goat farmer, fever, arthralgia → Brucella serology → TMP-SMX + Rifampicin (no doxy)
  • Lab worker, fever, culture-negative endocarditis → Brucella serology Phase I IgG → chronic Q fever? Brucella PCR valve

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

BRUCELLOSIS: B. melitensis (goats) most virulent. Unpasteurised dairy, aerosol. Clinical: Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly. Focal: Sacroiliitis (20-60%), Endocarditis (aortic), Neuro. Dx: Bone marrow culture > blood; SAT ≥1:160. Acute Rx: Doxy 100mg 12h + Rif 600mg OD ×6w. Endocarditis: Doxy + Rif + Genta 18m. Neuro: Doxy + Rif + TMP-SMX 3-6m. Pregnancy/Kids: TMP-SMX + Rif (NO doxy). Relapse 5-15%. Prevention: pasteurisation, animal vaccines.

Summary

Brucellosis is a zoonotic infection caused by Brucella spp. (B. melitensis — goats/sheep, most virulent; B. abortus — cattle; B. suis — pigs; B. canis — dogs). Transmission: unpasteurised dairy (milk, cheese), direct contact with infected animal tissues/fluids, aerosol inhalation (abattoir, lab). Clinical: undulant fever (spiking fever with remissions), arthralgia (sacroiliitis characteristic), hepatosplenomegaly, sweats, fatigue. Focal complications (20–60%): osteoarticular (sacroiliitis, spondylitis, peripheral arthritis), endocarditis (aortic valve, high mortality), neurobrucellosis (meningoencephalitis, radiculopathy), genitourinary (orchitis). Diagnosis: Blood culture (50–70%), bone marrow culture (90%, gold standard); Standard Agglutination Test (SAT) ≥1:160 positive, ≥1:320 active; Coombs for prozone; Brucella IgG/IgM ELISA; PCR (rapid). Treatment — Uncomplicated: Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6 weeks (WHO 1st-line). Endocarditis: Doxycycline + Rifampicin + Gentamicin 5mg/kg IV OD ×2 weeks ×18–24 months (valve replacement often needed). Neurobrucellosis: Doxycycline + Rifampicin + TMP-SMX ×3–6 months. Pregnancy/Children <8: TMP-SMX + Rifampicin (doxycycline contraindicated). Relapse: 5–15% (retreat same/alternative). Prevention: pasteurisation of dairy, animal vaccination (RB51 cattle, Rev-1 sheep/goats), occupational PPE, lab biosafety (BSL-3).

MCQs (10)

  1. Most virulent Brucella species for humans:

A. B. abortus

B. B. melitensis

C. B. suis

D. B. canis

E. B. ovis

  1. Classic clinical triad of brucellosis:

A. Fever, cough, dyspnoea

B. Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly

C. Fever, rash, lymphadenopathy

D. Fever, jaundice, renal failure

E. Fever, headache, photophobia

  1. Most common focal complication of brucellosis:

A. Endocarditis

B. Neurobrucellosis

C. Osteoarticular (Sacroiliitis — 20–60%)

D. Genitourinary (Orchitis)

E. Hepatosplenic abscess

  1. Gold standard for diagnosis of brucellosis:

A. Blood culture

B. Bone marrow culture

C. SAT

D. Brucella IgG ELISA

E. PCR

  1. Standard Agglutination Test (SAT) titre indicating active brucellosis:

A. ≥1:80

B. ≥1:160

C. ≥1:320

D. ≥1:640

E. ≥1:1280

  1. First-line treatment for uncomplicated brucellosis (adult):

A. Doxycycline monotherapy

B. Rifampicin monotherapy

C. Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6 weeks

D. Ciprofloxacin + Rifampicin

E. TMP-SMX + Doxycycline

  1. Treatment for brucellar endocarditis:

A. Doxycycline + Rifampicin ×6 weeks

B. Doxycycline + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (first 2 weeks) ×18–24 months

C. Rifampicin + Gentamicin ×6 weeks

D. Ceftriaxone + Gentamicin ×4 weeks

E. Doxycycline + Cotrimoxazole ×3 months

  1. Treatment of brucellosis in pregnancy:

A. Doxycycline + Rifampicin

B. TMP-SMX + Rifampicin (doxycycline contraindicated)

C. Rifampicin + Gentamicin

C. Ciprofloxacin + Rifampicin

E. Azithromycin + Rifampicin

  1. Brucellar endocarditis — most commonly affected valve:

A. Mitral

B. Aortic

C. Tricuspid

D. Pulmonary

E. Prosthetic (any)

  1. Relapse rate after adequate treatment of brucellosis:

A. <1%

B. 5–15%

C. 20–30%

D. 30–50%

E. >50%

  1. Prevention of brucellosis — most effective measure:

A. Animal vaccination only

B. Pasteurisation of dairy products

C. Human vaccination

D. Antibiotic prophylaxis for farmers

E. Culling of infected animals

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 35-year-old goat farmer presents with 6 weeks of intermittent fever, severe lower back pain radiating to thighs, and night sweats. MRI pelvis shows sacroiliitis. Blood culture grows Brucella melitensis. Best treatment?

A. Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×6 weeks

B. Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6 weeks

C. Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6 weeks

D. Ciprofloxacin + Rifampicin ×6 weeks

E. TMP-SMX + Rifampicin ×6 weeks

  1. A 45-year-old man with a prosthetic aortic valve presents with 3 months of fever, weight loss, and new diastolic murmur. Blood cultures negative ×3. Serology: Brucella SAT 1:640. Echocardiogram: vegetation on prosthetic valve. Best treatment?

A. Doxycycline + Rifampicin ×6 weeks

B. Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD + Gentamicin 5mg/kg IV OD ×2w, then Doxy + Rif ×18m total

C. Ceftriaxone + Gentamicin ×6w

C. Vancomycin + Rifampicin ×6w

E. Ciprofloxacin + Rifampicin ×6w

  1. A 28-year-old pregnant woman (20 weeks) from an endemic area presents with fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly. Brucella SAT 1:320. Best treatment?

A. Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD

B. TMP-SMX DS 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6 weeks

C. Rifampicin 600mg OD alone

D. Ciprofloxacin 500mg 12h + Rifampicin

E. Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin

  1. A 10-year-old child presents with fever, limping, and lower back pain. Sacroiliitis on MRI. Brucella culture positive. Best treatment?

A. Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD

B. TMP-SMX 8mg/kg TMP 12h + Rifampicin 15mg/kg OD ×6 weeks

C. Rifampicin alone

D. Ciprofloxacin + Rifampicin

E. Azithromycin + Rifampicin

  1. A 30-year-old man with brucellosis (completed 6 weeks doxycycline + rifampicin) presents 2 months later with recurrent fever and sacroiliitis. Blood culture positive for Brucella. Management?

A. Extend doxycycline + rifampicin for 3 more months

B. Retreat with doxycycline + rifampicin ×6 weeks (or alternative regimen)

C. Add gentamicin for 2 weeks

D. Switch to ciprofloxacin + rifampicin

E. Surgical sacroiliac joint debridement

  1. A laboratory worker accidentally inhales aerosolised Brucella culture. Post-exposure prophylaxis?

A. No prophylaxis needed

B. Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×3 weeks

C. Rifampicin alone ×3 weeks

D. Ciprofloxacin ×3 weeks

E. No prophylaxis, monitor for symptoms

  1. Brucella blood culture — optimal media and conditions:

A. Standard aerobic bottle, 24h

B. Castaneda biphasic medium, 5–10% CO2, 3–7 days

C. Anaerobic bottle, 48h

D. Chocolate agar, 5% CO2, 24h

E. MacConkey agar, 48h

  1. Prozone phenomenon in Brucella SAT — correct statement:

A. False positive at high dilutions

B. False negative at low dilutions due to antibody excess; overcome by serial dilutions

C. Only occurs with IgM

D. Indicates past infection

E. Not seen in brucellosis

  1. Brucellar spondylitis — most commonly affected level:

A. Cervical

B. Thoracic

C. Lumbar (L4-L5) / Sacroiliac joints

D. Sacroiliac only

E. Thoracolumbar junction

  1. Brucellar orchitis — management:

A. Orchiectomy

B. Doxycycline + Rifampicin ×6 weeks + scrotal support

C. Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline

C. Surgical drainage + antibiotics

E. Conservative only

  1. Most effective public health measure to prevent human brucellosis:

A. Human vaccination

B. Pasteurisation of milk and dairy products

C. Mass antibiotic prophylaxis

D. Culling all infected animals

E. Public education only

Flashcards

  • Q: Most virulent Brucella

A: B. melitensis (goats)

  • Q: Classic triad

A: Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly

  • Q: Most common focal

A: Sacroiliitis (20-60%)

  • Q: Gold standard Dx

A: Bone marrow culture (90%)

  • Q: SAT positive

A: ≥1:160; active ≥1:320

  • Q: Acute Rx

A: Doxy + Rif 6w

  • Q: Endocarditis Rx

A: Doxy + Rif + Genta (2w) 18m

  • Q: Neuro Rx

A: Doxy + Rif + TMP-SMX 3-6m

  • Q: Pregnancy Rx

A: TMP-SMX + Rif (no doxy)

  • Q: Relapse rate

A: 5-15%

  • Q: Prevention

A: Pasteurisation + animal vaccines

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — B. melitensis (goats/sheep) is the most virulent and common cause of human brucellosis.
  2. B — Classic triad: undulant fever, arthralgia (especially sacroiliitis), hepatosplenomegaly.
  3. C — Osteoarticular involvement (sacroiliitis, spondylitis) is the most common focal complication (20–60%).
  4. B — Bone marrow culture has highest sensitivity (90%); blood culture 50–70%.
  5. C — SAT ≥1:320 indicates active disease; ≥1:160 is positive but may be past/exposed.
  6. C — WHO 1st-line: Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×6 weeks.
  7. B — Endocarditis: Doxy + Rifampicin + Gentamicin (first 2w) ×18–24 months; valve replacement often needed.
  8. B — Doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy; TMP-SMX + Rifampicin is standard.
  9. B — Relapse rate 5–15% despite adequate treatment.
  10. B — Pasteurisation of dairy products is the single most effective prevention measure.

SBAs

  1. B — Sacroiliitis + B. melitensis = brucellar sacroiliitis → Doxy + Rifampicin ×6w.
  2. B — Prosthetic valve endocarditis + high Brucella titre = brucellar endocarditis → Doxy + Rif + Genta (2w) ×18m.
  3. B — Pregnancy = doxycycline contraindicated; TMP-SMX + Rifampicin standard.
  4. B — Children <8: doxycycline contraindicated; TMP-SMX + Rifampicin (weight-based).
  5. B — Relapse (5–15%) = retreat with standard or alternative regimen (same duration).
  6. B — Lab exposure: Doxycycline 100mg 12h + Rifampicin 600mg OD ×3w (WHO recommendation).
  7. B — Castaneda biphasic medium, 5–10% CO2, 3–7 days incubation for Brucella culture.
  8. B — Prozone = antibody excess → false low titre; overcome by serial dilutions.
  9. C — Lumbar/sacroiliac most common for brucellar spondylitis/sacroiliitis.
  10. B — Pasteurisation of dairy = single most effective prevention of human brucellosis.

Flashcards

  • Q: Most virulent Brucella

A: B. melitensis (goats)

  • Q: Classic triad

A: Undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly

  • Q: Hallmark focal complication

A: Sacroiliitis

  • Q: Gold standard Dx

A: Bone marrow culture (90%)

  • Q: SAT interpretation

A: ≥1:160 positive; ≥1:320 active

  • Q: Acute treatment

A: Doxy 100mg 12h + Rif 600mg OD ×6w

  • Q: Endocarditis Rx

A: Doxy + Rif + Gentamicin 2w ×18-24m

  • Q: Pregnancy Rx

A: TMP-SMX + Rif (no doxy)

  • Q: Relapse rate

A: 5-15%

  • Q: Prevention

A: Pasteurisation + animal vaccines

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — B. melitensis (goats/sheep) is most virulent and common cause of human brucellosis.
  2. B — Classic triad: undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly.
  3. C — Osteoarticular (sacroiliitis) is most common focal complication (20–60%).
  4. B — Bone marrow culture has 90% sensitivity vs 50–70% for blood culture.
  5. C — SAT ≥1:320 indicates active disease; ≥1:160 is positive/borderline.
  6. C — WHO 1st-line: Doxycycline + Rifampicin ×6 weeks (synergistic, prevents resistance).
  7. B — Endocarditis requires prolonged combination: Doxy + Rif + Gentamicin (2w) ×18–24m.
  8. B — Doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy; TMP-SMX + Rifampicin is standard alternative.
  9. B — Relapse occurs in 5–15% despite adequate treatment.
  10. B — Pasteurisation of milk/dairy is the single most effective public health measure.

SBAs

  1. B — Sacroiliitis = brucellar osteoarthritis → Doxy + Rifampicin ×6w (standard).
  2. B — Brucellar prosthetic valve endocarditis → Doxy + Rif + Gentamicin (2w) ×18m (standard for endocarditis).
  3. B — Pregnancy = doxycycline contraindicated; TMP-SMX + Rifampicin is safe standard.
  4. B — Child <8: doxycycline contraindicated; TMP-SMX 8mg/kg TMP 12h + Rifampicin 15mg/kg OD ×6w.
  5. B — Relapse (confirmed by culture) = retreat with standard 6w regimen (or alternative).
  6. B — Lab aerosol exposure = Doxy + Rifampicin ×3w post-exposure prophylaxis (WHO).
  7. B — Castaneda biphasic medium is classic for Brucella; 5–10% CO2, 3–7 days.
  8. B — Prozone = antibody excess → false low titre; serial dilutions correct it.
  9. C — Lumbar/sacroiliac most common for brucellar spondylitis/sacroiliitis.
  10. B — Pasteurisation of dairy = single most effective prevention of human brucellosis.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.07 Cellulitis And Necrotising Fasciitis

Related: [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]], [[Diabetic Foot Infections]], [[Staphylococcal Infections]], [[Streptococcal Infections]]

[!important]
Cellulitis = spreading bacterial skin infection (dermis/subcutis). Necrotising fasciitis = life-threatening deep infection of fascia with rapid necrosis. Time-critical: NF = surgical emergency (hours matter). LRINEC score aids NF suspicion.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate cellulitis, erysipelas, and necrotising fasciitis
  • Apply clinical scoring (LRINEC, Eron) for severity
  • Choose empirical antibiotics by syndrome and severity
  • Recognise surgical emergency criteria for NF
  • Manage diabetic foot and special populations

2. Cellulitis and Erysipelas

Definitions

ConditionDepthTypical Appearance
ErysipelasUpper dermis + superficial lymphaticsWell-demarcated, raised, bright red, shiny
CellulitisDeep dermis + subcutaneous tissuePoorly demarcated, deeper red, less raised
Necrotising fasciitisDeep fascia + subcutaneous tissueRapid progression, pain out of proportion, crepitus, necrosis

Aetiology

SyndromeCommon Pathogens
ErysipelasStrep pyogenes (GAS) > Staph aureus
Cellulitis (non-purulent)Strep pyogenes, other Strep (Groups B, C, G)
Cellulitis (purulent)Staph aureus (MSSA/MRSA)
Cellulitis (diabetic foot)Polymicrobial (Staph, Strep, Gram-neg, anaerobes)
Bite woundsPasteurella, Capnocytophaga, Staph, anaerobes
Water exposureVibrio vulnificus, Aeromonas, Mycobacterium marinum

Clinical Features

FeatureErysipelasCellulitisNecrotising Fasciitis
MarginsSharp, well-demarcatedIndistinct, diffuseRapidly expanding
DepthSuperficial dermisDeep dermis + subcutisDeep fascia + muscle
PainModerateModerateSevere, out of proportion
SystemicFever, chills commonFever, malaiseSevere sepsis, shock
SkinRaised, shiny, bright redRed, hot, swollen, pitting oedemaBronzing, blisters, necrosis, crepitus
LymphangitisCommonLess commonLate

Eron Classification (Cellulitis Severity)

ClassFeaturesManagement
INo systemic signsOral antibiotics, outpatient
IISystemic signs (fever, tachycardia) OR comorbidities (lymphoedema, venous insufficiency)IV antibiotics, consider admission
IIISignificant comorbidity (immunosuppression, cardiac/renal failure)IV antibiotics, admit
IVSepsis, septic shockICU, IV antibiotics + vasopressors, surgical eval

LRINEC Score (Life-threatening Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection)

ParameterPoints
CRP >150 mg/L4
WBC >25 ×10⁹/L2
WBC 15–251
Haemoglobin <11 g/dL1
Sodium <135 mmol/L2
Creatinine >141 µmol/L2
Glucose >18 mmol/L1

Score ≥6 = high risk NF; 6–7 intermediate; ≥8 high probability. Not diagnostic — clinical suspicion paramount.

3. Necrotising Fasciitis

Types

TypePathogensTypical Context
Type I (Polymicrobial)Aerobes + anaerobes (Staph, Strep, Enterobacterales, Bacteroides, Clostridium)Post-surgical, perineal (Fournier's), diabetic
Type II (Monomicrobial)Strep pyogenes (GAS) ± Staph aureusHealthy, trauma, varicella
Type III (Marine)Vibrio vulnificus, AeromonasSaltwater exposure, liver disease
Type IV (Fungal)Candida, ZygomycetesImmunocompromised

Clinical Diagnosis (Clinical Suspicion > Scores)

Red FlagSignificance
Pain out of proportion to examHallmark of NF
Rapid progression (hours)Surgical emergency
Pain beyond erythemaDeep involvement
Crepitus (gas in tissues)Gas-forming organisms
Skin anaesthesia (nerve destruction)Deep fascia involvement
Bronzing/dusky discolorationNecrosis
Bullae/haemorrhagic blistersVascular thrombosis
Rapidly expandingHourly progression

Management

Immediate (Emergency)

  1. Call surgery/orthopaedics — debridement within hours
  2. IV fluids + vasopressors (septic shock common)
  3. Empirical IV antibiotics (broad-spectrum, see below)
  4. Tetanus prophylaxis

Empirical Antibiotics (NF)

RegimenCoverage
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6hBroad (Gram+, Gram-, anaerobes)
OR Meropenem 1g IV q8hIf penicillin allergy / severe sepsis
+ Clindamycin 900mg IV q8hToxin inhibition (GAS, Clostridium)
+ Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg q12hIf MRSA suspected
Clindamycin essential for toxin-mediated NF (GAS, Clostridium) — inhibits protein synthesis

Surgical Management

  • Early, aggressive, repeated debridement (every 24–48h until healthy tissue)
  • Fasciotomy if compartment syndrome
  • Amputation if limb non-viable
  • Negative pressure wound therapy post-debridement
  • Hyperbaric oxygen (adjunct, evidence limited)

4. Cellulitis Treatment

Outpatient (Eron I)

RegimenDoseDuration
Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO1st-line (MSSA, Strep)5–7d (extend if slow response)
Clarithromycin 500mg BD (penicillin allergy)Alternative5–7d
Doxycycline 100mg BD (if MRSA risk)Alternative5–7d

Inpatient (Eron II–IV)

SeverityRegimen
Non-purulent (Eron II)Flucloxacillin 2g IV q6h (+/− benzylpenicillin 2.4g IV q6h if Strep suspected)
Purulent / MRSA riskVancomycin 15–20mg/kg q12h IV (trough 15–20)
Severe / sepsis (Eron III–IV)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g q6h +/− Vancomycin
Diabetic foot / polymicrobialPip-tazo or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole

Special Situations

ScenarioKey Points
Diabetic footOffloading + vascular assessment + polymicrobial cover (Pip-tazo)
Lymphoedema / venous insufficiencyCompression + long-term penicillin prophylaxis if recurrent
Periorbital / orbitalCeftriaxone + metronidazole; ENT/ophthalmology review
Bite woundsCo-amoxiclav (Pasteurella, anaerobes); rabies/tetanus
Water exposureDoxycycline + ceftriaxone (Vibrio, Aeromonas)
Recurrent (>2/year)Prophylactic penicillin V 250mg BD / erythromycin

5. Fournier's Gangrene (Perineal NF)

FeatureDetails
SiteScrotum, perineum, genitalia, abdominal wall
CommonDiabetic males, perineal trauma, colorectal surgery
PathogensPolymicrobial (Type I)
Urethral involvementSuprapubic catheter if needed
ManagementEmergency debridement + broad antibiotics + orchidectomy if testicular involvement

6. Complications

ComplicationRisk Factors
Sepsis / septic shockDelayed treatment, NF, immunocompromise
AmputationNF, severe diabetic foot, vascular compromise
Scarring / contractureDeep NF, delayed debridement
Recurrent cellulitisLymphoedema, venous insufficiency, immunosuppression
OsteomyelitisDiabetic foot, contiguous spread

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • NF = surgical emergency — debridement within hours, not days
  • Pain out of proportion = hallmark of NF
  • LRINEC ≥6 = consider NF (but clinical suspicion > score)
  • Clindamycin essential for toxin-mediated NF (GAS, Clostridium)
  • Erysipelas = well-demarcated, raised, superficial (Strep)
  • Cellulitis = poorly demarcated, deep (Staph/Strep)
  • Eron classification guides admission/IV vs oral
  • Recurrent cellulitis → prophylactic penicillin V 250mg BD

8. Common Viva Questions

  1. How do you differentiate cellulitis from necrotising fasciitis?
  2. What is the LRINEC score and how is it used?
  3. What antibiotics for necrotising fasciitis?
  4. When do you admit cellulitis?
  5. What is Fournier's gangrene?

9. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Waiting for imaging/LRINEC before surgery in NF = WRONG (clinical diagnosis)
  • Using flucloxacillin alone for NF = inadequate (need broad + clindamycin)
  • Confusing erysipelas/cellulitis = erysipelas = raised, well-demarcated
  • Missing diabetic foot = check pulses, sensation, offloading
  • Not giving clindamycin in NF = misses toxin inhibition
  • LRINEC <6 does not rule out NF = clinical suspicion > score

10. Mnemonics

  • NF signs: Pain out of proportion, Rapid progression, Anaesthesia, Crepitus, Erythema spreading = PRACE
  • LRINEC: CRP, WBC, Hb, Na, Cr, Glucose = CWHNCG
  • Cellulitis vs Erysipelas: Erysipelas = Elevated, Edge well-defined
  • NF antibiotics: Pip-tazo + Clindamycin + Vanco = PCV
  • Clindamycin in NF: Toxin Inhibition = TI

11. One-Page Revision Summary

Cellulitis = deep skin infection; NF = fascial necrosis (surgical emergency)

  • Cellulitis: Flucloxacillin PO/IV; admit if systemic (Eron II–IV)
  • Erysipelas: Superficial, well-demarcated, Strep
  • NF: Pain out of proportion, rapid progression, crepitus, anaesthesia
  • LRINEC ≥6 = suspect NF; clinical suspicion > score
  • NF antibiotics: Pip-tazo + Clindamycin + Vancomycin
  • Surgery: Debridement within hours, repeat q24–48h
  • Fournier's = perineal NF; emergency debridement
  • Recurrent cellulitis → Penicillin V 250mg BD prophylaxis

12. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • NF = surgical emergency (debridement within hours)
  • Pain out of proportion = NF hallmark
  • Clindamycin essential for toxin inhibition in NF
  • Broad antibiotics: Pip-tazo + Clinda + Vanco
  • Erysipelas vs cellulitis clinical difference
  • Eron classification for admission decisions

Should Know

  • LRINEC score components and interpretation
  • Diabetic foot management (offloading, vascular, polymicrobial)
  • Fournier's gangrene management
  • Bite wound / water exposure pathogens
  • Recurrent cellulitis prophylaxis

Nice to Know

  • Hyperbaric oxygen evidence
  • Negative pressure wound therapy
  • Biomarkers for NF (LRINEC, procalcitonin)
  • Necrotising infections in immunocompromised
  • New antibiotics (cefiderocol, tedizolid)

13. MCQs (10)

  1. Hallmark clinical feature of necrotising fasciitis:

A. Well-demarcated erythema

B. Pain out of proportion to examination findings

C. Mild fever

D. Slow progression over days

  1. LRINEC score ≥6 indicates:

A. Low risk of NF

B. Intermediate/high risk of NF (clinical suspicion still needed)

C. Definite NF

D. Need for MRI

  1. Essential antibiotic for toxin-mediated necrotising fasciitis (GAS, Clostridium):

A. Vancomycin

B. Clindamycin (protein synthesis inhibition)

C. Ceftriaxone

D. Metronidazole

  1. Erysipelas vs cellulitis — key difference:

A. Erysipelas = poorly demarcated

B. Erysipelas = well-demarcated, raised, superficial

C. Cellulitis = superficial

D. Erysipelas = no fever

  1. Eron Class II cellulitis — management:

A. Oral flucloxacillin, outpatient

B. IV antibiotics, consider admission

C. ICU admission

C. Surgical debridement

  1. Necrotising fasciitis — surgical timing:

A. Within 24 hours

B. Within hours (emergency)

C. After MRI confirmation

D. Next available list

  1. Fournier's gangrene — site and typical patient:

A. Leg, athlete

B. Perineum/scrotum, diabetic male

C. Face, child

D. Arm, IVDU

  1. Cellulitis in lymphoedema — prophylaxis:

A. Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS

B. Penicillin V 250mg BD (long-term)

C. Vancomycin PO

D. No prophylaxis needed

  1. Necrotising fasciitis antibiotics — must include:

A. Flucloxacillin only

B. Clindamycin (toxin inhibition) + broad Gram-negative cover

C. Metronidazole only

D. Ceftriaxone only

  1. Diabetic foot infection — first step:

A. Start antibiotics

B. Offloading + vascular assessment + then antibiotics

C. Immediate amputation

D. MRI

  1. Gas in tissues on X-ray (crepitus) — suggests:

A. Strep only

B. Gas-forming organisms (Clostridium, mixed anaerobes, E. coli)

C. Staph only

D. Viral

14. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 60-year-old diabetic, severe left leg pain 12h, rapidly spreading erythema, tense blisters, crepitus on palpation, BP 85/50. LRINEC 8. Best immediate action:

A. IV flucloxacillin + clindamycin, review in 24h

B. Emergency surgical debridement + IV pip-tazo + clindamycin + vancomycin + fluids/vasopressors

C. MRI leg, then decide

D. IV ceftriaxone + metronidazole, review in 6h

  1. A 30-year-old, bright red, well-demarcated, raised plaque on face, fever 38.5°C. No systemic upset. Best treatment:

A. IV benzylpenicillin

B. Oral flucloxacillin 500mg QDS × 5d (or penicillin V)

C. IV co-amoxiclav

D. IV vancomycin

  1. A 70-year-old with lymphoedema, 3rd episode cellulitis leg in 6 months. Recurrence prevention:

A. No prophylaxis

B. Penicillin V 250mg BD long-term

C. Rotating antibiotics

C. Compression only

  1. A 45-year-old, dog bite hand, swelling, pain 6h. Best empirical antibiotic:

A. Flucloxacillin

B. Co-amoxiclav (Pasteurella, anaerobes)

C. Doxycycline

D. Ceftriaxone

  1. A 25-year-old post-varicella, severe thigh pain, erythema spreading rapidly, crepitus. Suspect NF. Antibiotics:

A. Ceftriaxone + metronidazole

B. Pip-tazo + clindamycin + vancomycin

C. Flucloxacillin + gentamicin

C. Ciprofloxacin + clindamycin

  1. A diabetic with foot ulcer, surrounding erythema 4cm, no systemic signs, palpable pulses, no purulence. Best management:

A. IV pip-tazo + urgent surgery

B. Oral flucloxacillin + offloading + vascular review + podiatry

C. IV vancomycin + meropenem

D. Amputation

  1. Recurrent cellulitis >2/year in lymphoedema — long-term:

A. Co-amoxiclav

B. Penicillin V 250mg BD

C. Doxycycline

D. No prophylaxis

  1. Periorbital cellulitis with orbital signs (proptosis, ophthalmoplegia). Best antibiotics:

A. Flucloxacillin

B. Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + metronidazole 500mg q8h IV

C. Vancomycin + ceftriaxone

D. Pip-tazo

  1. A 40-year-old post-abdominal surgery, day 3: severe abdominal wall pain, crepitus, spreading erythema, septic. Best action:

A. IV antibiotics, CT abdomen

B. Emergency surgical exploration/debridement + broad IV antibiotics

C. Laparoscopy

D. Drains + antibiotics

  1. Recurrent erysipelas face (>2/year). Prophylaxis:

A. Aciclovir

B. Penicillin V 250mg BD long-term

C. Flucloxacillin

D. No prophylaxis

15. Flashcards

  • Q: NF hallmark

A: Pain out of proportion to exam

  • Q: NF surgical timing

A: Emergency (hours), not days

  • Q: NF antibiotics

A: Pip-tazo + Clindamycin + Vancomycin

  • Q: Clindamycin in NF

A: Toxin inhibition (GAS, Clostridium)

  • Q: Erysipelas vs Cellulitis

A: Erysipelas = raised, well-demarcated, superficial

  • Q: LRINEC ≥6

A: Suspect NF (clinical suspicion > score)

  • Q: Cellulitis Eron II

A: IV antibiotics, consider admission

  • Q: Recurrent cellulitis prophylaxis

A: Penicillin V 250mg BD

  • Q: Diabetic foot

A: Offloading + vascular + polymicrobial abx

  • Q: Fournier's gangrene

A: Perineal NF, diabetic male, emergency debridement

16. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Pain out of proportion = NF hallmark.

MCQ 2: B — LRINEC ≥6 = intermediate/high risk (not diagnostic).

MCQ 3: B — Clindamycin inhibits toxin production (protein synthesis inhibitor).

MCQ 4: B — Erysipelas = well-demarcated, raised, superficial dermis.

MCQ 5: B — Eron II = systemic signs/comorbidities → IV abx, consider admission.

MCQ 6: B — NF = surgical emergency within hours.

MCQ 7: B — Perineum/scrotum, diabetic males = Fournier's.

MCQ 7: B — Penicillin V 250mg BD long-term for recurrent cellulitis/erysipelas in lymphoedema.

MCQ 8: B — Clindamycin (toxin inhibition) + broad Gram-neg cover (pip-tazo/meropenem).

MCQ 9: B — Offloading, vascular assessment, then polymicrobial abx.

MCQ 10: B — Crepitus = gas-forming organisms (Clostridium, mixed, E. coli).

SBA 1: B — NF = emergency surgery + broad IV abx (pip-tazo + clinda + vanco) + resuscitation.

SBA 2: B — Erysipelas face, non-severe = oral flucloxacillin/penicillin V.

SBA 3: B — Recurrent cellulitis in lymphoedema = penicillin V 250mg BD prophylaxis.

SBA 4: B — Bite wound = co-amoxiclav (Pasteurella + anaerobes).

SBA 5: B — NF = pip-tazo + clindamycin + vancomycin.

SBA 6: B — Diabetic foot mild = oral flucloxacillin + offloading + vascular review.

SBA 7: B — Recurrent cellulitis/erysipelas in lymphoedema = penicillin V 250mg BD.

SBA 8: B — Periorbital/orbital = ceftriaxone + metronidazole (covers anaerobes).

SBA 9: B — Post-op NF = emergency surgical exploration + broad IV antibiotics.

SBA 10: B — Recurrent erysipelas = penicillin V prophylaxis.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13
