▸ 14.1.08 Cestode And Trematode Infections

tags: [medicine, davidson, infectious-disease, cestodes, trematodes, taeniasis, cysticercosis, echinococcosis, schistosomiasis, clonorchis, fasciola, paragonimus, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 11: Infectious disease

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: high

exam_relevance: "FCPS: Neurocysticercosis, hydatid disease, schistosomiasis | MRCP: Food-borne trematodes, liver flukes, lung fluke, praziquantel/albendazole"

see_also: "[[Neurocysticercosis]], [[Hydatid Disease]], [[Schistosomiasis]], [[Liver Fluke Infections]], [[Lung Fluke]], [[Travel Medicine]]"

created: 2025-06-17

modified: 2025-06-17

Cestode & Trematode Infections

[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Parasites → Helminths → Cestodes (Tapeworms) & Trematodes (Flukes)
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Neurocysticercosis (T. solium), hydatid disease (E. granulosus), schistosomiasis, liver flukes (Clonorchis, Fasciola), lung fluke (Paragonimus), praziquantel/albendazole

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify cestodes (tapeworms): Taenia (intestinal), Echinococcus (hydatid), Hymenolepis, Diphyllobothrium
  • [ ] Classify trematodes (flukes): Blood (Schistosoma), Liver (Clonorchis, Opisthorchis, Fasciola), Lung (Paragonimus), Intestinal (Fasciolopsis, Heterophyes)
  • [ ] Recognise Neurocysticercosis: Seizures, cysts (vesicular, colloidal, granular, calcified), Del Bruto criteria
  • [ ] Recognise Hydatid disease: Liver/lung cysts, anaphylaxis risk, "water-lily" sign, PAIR procedure
  • [ ] Recognise Schistosomiasis: Katayama fever, hepatic/intestinal/urinary chronic disease, bladder cancer
  • [ ] Diagnose: Stool O&P, serology, imaging (CT/MRI/US), antigen detection
  • [ ] Manage: Praziquantel (most trematodes/cestodes), Albendazole (neurocysticercosis, hydatid), Triclabendazole (Fasciola)

📚 Cestodes (Tapeworms)

SpeciesDiseaseTransmissionKey Features
Taenia soliumTaeniasis (adult worm), Cysticercosis (larval)Undercooked pork (taeniasis); faecal-oral eggs (cysticercosis)Neurocysticercosis = major cause of epilepsy in endemic areas
Taenia saginataTaeniasis onlyUndercooked beefNo cysticercosis in humans; proglottids motile (pass per anus)
Taenia asiaticaTaeniasisUndercooked pig visceraSE Asia; similar to T. saginata
Echinococcus granulosusCystic echinococcosis (Hydatid disease)Dog feces (sheep/dog cycle)Liver (65%), Lung (25%); unilocular cysts, anaphylaxis if rupture
Echinococcus multilocularisAlveolar echinococcosisFox/rodent cycleInfiltrative, malignant-like, liver → metastases; high mortality
Hymenolepis nanaHymenolepiasisFaecal-oral (auto-infection)Dwarf tapeworm; children, institutionalised; asymptomatic or GI
Diphyllobothrium latumDiphyllobothriasisRaw/undercooked fishVitamin B12 deficiency (worm competes), megaloblastic anaemia
Dipylidium caninumDipylidiasisDog/cat fleasChildren; proglottids like rice grains

📚 Trematodes (Flukes)

Blood Flukes — Schistosoma

SpeciesLocationDistributionKey Features
S. haematobiumVenous plexus of bladder/uretersAfrica, Middle EastHaematuria, bladder fibrosis, SCC bladder, hydronephrosis
S. mansoniPortal veins → mesentericAfrica, S. America, CaribbeanHepatosplenic disease, portal hypertension, varices, colonic polyposis
S. japonicumPortal veins → mesentericChina, Philippines, IndonesiaMore severe hepatic disease, higher egg output, animal reservoirs
S. intercalatumMesenteric veinsCentral/West AfricaMild intestinal disease
S. mekongiMesenteric veinsMekong River basinSimilar to S. japonicum

Liver Flukes

SpeciesTransmissionKey Features
Clonorchis sinensisRaw/undercooked freshwater fishBiliary dilatation, cholangitis, cholangiocarcinoma (high risk), stones
Opisthorchis viverrini/felineusRaw fishSimilar to Clonorchis; cholangiocarcinoma (NE Thailand)
Fasciola hepatica/giganticaWatercress, aquatic plants (metacercariae)Acute: fever, RUQ pain, hepatomegaly, eosinophilia; Chronic: biliary obstruction, stones

Lung Fluke

SpeciesTransmissionKey Features
Paragonimus westermaniRaw/undercooked crab/crayfishHaemoptysis, "rusty sputum", eosinophilia, CXR: ring shadows, cavities; mimics TB; ectopic (brain, skin)

Intestinal Flukes

SpeciesTransmissionKey Features
Fasciolopsis buskiAquatic plants (water chestnut)Intestinal: diarrhoea, malabsorption, oedema
Heterophyes/MetagonimusRaw fishMild intestinal symptoms

🩺 Key Clinical Syndromes

1. Neurocysticercosis (T. solium larval cysts in CNS)

StageImagingClinical
Vesicular (viable)Single/multiple cysts, thin wall, clear fluid, scolex (dot)Often asymptomatic
Colloidal (degenerating)Ring-enhancing, perilesional oedemaSeizures, headache, focal deficits
Granular-nodularEnhancing nodule, decreasing oedemaResolving
CalcifiedCalcified nodulesLate seizures (epileptogenic focus)

Del Bruto Diagnostic Criteria (Definite = 1 Absolute + 1 Major OR 2 Major + 1 Minor + Epidemiologic)

  • Absolute: Scolex on CT/MRI, histopathology
  • Major: Lesions suggestive (ring-enhancing, calcifications), positive serology (EITB), cysticercosis elsewhere
  • Minor: Clinical manifestations, epidemiologic exposure

2. Cystic Echinococcosis / Hydatid Disease (E. granulosus)

Stage (WHO Ultrasound Classification)Features
CE1Active, unilocular, anechoic (pure fluid)
CE2Active, multiple septa ("honeycomb", "wheel-spoke")
CE3Detached membrane ("water-lily sign") - transitional
CE4Inactive, heterogeneous, hyperechoic (degenerate)
CE5Inactive, calcified wall

Complications: Anaphylaxis (rupture), secondary infection, biliary fistula, mass effect

3. Schistosomiasis

PhaseFeatures
Acute (Katayama fever)4-8 weeks post-exposure: fever, cough, urticaria, hepatosplenomegaly, marked eosinophilia
Chronic - S. haematobiumTerminal haematuria, dysuria, bladder calcification, SCC bladder, obstructive uropathy
Chronic - S. mansoni/japonicumPortal hypertension, varices, ascites, splenomegaly, colonic polyposis (pipe-stem fibrosis)

🔬 Diagnosis

ParasiteDiagnostic Tests
NeurocysticercosisCT/MRI (stages), EITB/Western blot (serum/CSF), stool O&P (taeniasis)
Hydatid diseaseUltrasound (WHO classification), serology (ELISA, IgG), CT/MRI, PCR (cyst fluid)
SchistosomiasisStool/urine microscopy (eggs: S. haematobium = terminal spine; S. mansoni = lateral spine; S. japonicum = small lateral spine), CAAs/CCAs (antigen), serology, rectal biopsy
Clonorchis/OpisthorchisStool O&P (small operculated eggs), ERCP/MRCP (biliary dilatation), serology
FasciolaStool O&P (large operculated eggs - late), serology (early), imaging (liver tracts)
ParagonimusSputum/stool O&P (large operculated eggs), CXR/CT (ring shadows, cavities), serology
[!tip] Exam Pearl: T. solium eggs = indistinguishable from T. saginata on microscopy; need proglottids or PCR. Cysticercosis from faecal-oral eggs, not pork.

💊 Management

Cestodes

InfectionDrugDoseDuration
Taeniasis (T. solium, T. saginata)Praziquantel5-10 mg/kg single dose1 dose
Niclosamide2 g single dose1 dose
NeurocysticercosisAlbendazole + Dexamethasone15 mg/kg/day (max 800mg) bd14-28 days
Praziquantel (alternative)50 mg/kg/day tds14 days
Seizure controlAEDs (levetiracetam, valproate)As needed
Hydatid disease (CE1, CE3a)Albendazole15 mg/kg/day bd (max 800mg)3-6 months (cycles 28d on/14d off)
PAIR (Puncture, Aspiration, Injection, Re-aspiration)95% ethanol / hypertonic salineSingle procedure + albendazole
SurgeryPerioperative albendazoleDefinitive for CE2, CE3b, complicated
Hymenolepis nanaPraziquantel25 mg/kg single dose1 dose (repeat 2 weeks)
DiphyllobothriumPraziquantel25 mg/kg single dose1 dose + B12 replacement

Trematodes

InfectionDrugDoseDuration
Schistosomiasis (all species)Praziquantel40 mg/kg single dose (or split)1 day
S. haematobium: 40 mg/kg; S. mansoni/japonicum: 60 mg/kg (2 doses 4h apart)
Clonorchis / OpisthorchisPraziquantel25 mg/kg tds1-3 days
FasciolaTriclabendazole10 mg/kg single dose (or bd)1-2 days
Praziquantel (ineffective)--
ParagonimusPraziquantel25 mg/kg tds2-3 days
Triclabendazole10 mg/kg bd1 day
Fasciolopsis / HeterophyesPraziquantel25 mg/kg tds1 day
[!warning] Neurocysticercosis: ALWAYS give dexamethasone (0.1 mg/kg/day) before and during albendazole to reduce perilesional oedema/seizures. Contraindicated in cysticercal encephalitis (numerous cysts + oedema) — treat oedema first.

📋 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
NeurocysticercosisMost common cause of acquired epilepsy in endemic areas; Albendazole + Dexamethasone
Hydatid diseaseE. granulosus; liver/lung cysts; WHO US classification; PAIR for CE1/CE3a; Albendazole adjunct
Alveolar echinococcosisE. multilocularis; infiltrative, malignant-like, poor prognosis
Schistosomiasishaematobium = haematuria/bladder cancer; mansoni/japonicum = portal HTN; Praziquantel
Clonorchis/OpisthorchisRaw fish → cholangiocarcinoma; Praziquantel
FasciolaWatercress → acute hepatitis + eosinophilia; Triclabendazole (NOT praziquantel)
ParagonimusRaw crab → haemoptysis, ring shadows on CXR; Praziquantel/Triclabendazole
DiphyllobothriumRaw fish → B12 deficiency; Praziquantel + B12

❓ Viva Questions (FCPS/MRCP)

  1. Differentiate Taenia solium taeniasis from cysticercosis. How does human cysticercosis occur?
  2. What are the Del Bruto criteria for neurocysticercosis?
  3. Describe WHO ultrasound classification of hydatid cysts. Which stages are suitable for PAIR?
  4. Compare E. granulosus vs E. multilocularis clinical features and prognosis.
  5. A patient presents with terminal haematuria after travel to Egypt. Diagnosis and treatment?
  6. Why is praziquantel ineffective for Fasciola? What is the drug of choice?
  7. Describe the life cycle of Paragonimus westermani.
  8. How do you differentiate Schistosoma eggs on microscopy?
  9. Management of neurocysticercosis with multiple viable cysts vs calcified nodules.
  10. A vegetarian has B12 deficiency and passes proglottids. Diagnosis?

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
T. solium vs T. saginataT. solium: Pork, cysticercosis risk, non-motile proglottids (<13 uterine branches); T. saginata: Beef, no cysticercosis, motile proglottids (>15 branches)
Cysticercosis vs TaeniasisCysticercosis = larval cysts in tissue (ingest eggs); Taeniasis = adult worm in intestine (ingest cysticerci in pork)
Hydatid cyst vs Liver abscessHydatid: WHO US classification, daughter cysts, water-lily sign, eosinophilia, anaphylaxis risk; Abscess: Pyogenic, septations, no daughter cysts
Fasciola vs ClonorchisFasciola: Watercress, acute hepatitis + eosinophilia, Triclabendazole; Clonorchis: Raw fish, chronic biliary dilatation, cholangiocarcinoma, Praziquantel
Paragonimus vs TBParagonimus: Eosinophilia, haemoptysis, ring shadows/cavities on CXR, responds to praziquantel; TB: AFB+, caseating, ATT

Mnemonic - Cestodes: "TAENIA" → Taenia solium (pork, cysticercosis), Albendazole (neurocysticercosis), Echinococcus granulosus (hydatid), Niclosamide/Praziquantel (taeniasis), Infective eggs (faecal-oral), Alveolar (multilocularis = bad)

Mnemonic - Trematodes: "SCHISTO" → Schistosoma (blood fluke, praziquantel), Clonorchis (liver fluke, fish, cholangiocarcinoma), Hepatic fasciola (watercress, triclabendazole), Intestinal flukes (fasciolopsis), Stages of schisto (Katayama → chronic), Trematodes = flukes, Operculated eggs (most)

Mnemonic - Neurocysticercosis Stages: "VESC" → Vesicular (viable, scolex), Enhancing/Colloidal (oedema, seizures), Stage/Calcified (epilepsy focus)

🗺️ Mind Map

Helminths
Cestodes (Tapeworms)
Taenia solium
– Taeniasis (pork)
– Cysticercosis (eggs)
– Neurocysticercosis → Albendazole + Dex
Taenia saginata (beef, no cysticercosis)
Echinococcus granulosus (Hydatid)
– Liver/Lung cysts, WHO US
– PAIR, Albendazole, Surgery
E. multilocularis (Alveolar - malignant)
Hymenolepis nana, Diphyllobothrium (B12)
Trematodes (Flukes)
Blood: Schistosoma
– haematobium (bladder, haematuria, SCC)
– mansoni/japonicum (liver, portal HTN)
– Praziquantel
Liver: Clonorchis/Opisthorchis (fish, CCA)
– Fasciola (watercress, triclabendazole)
Lung: Paragonimus (crab, haemoptysis)
Intestinal: Fasciolopsis

📄 One-Page Revision Card

CestodesDrugTrematodesDrug
T. solium taeniasisPraziquantel 5-10mg/kgSchistosomaPraziquantel 40-60mg/kg
T. solium neurocysticercosisAlbendazole + DexClonorchis/OpisthorchisPraziquantel 25mg/kg tds
E. granulosus hydatidAlbendazole ± PAIR/SurgeryFasciolaTriclabendazole 10mg/kg
E. multilocularisAlbendazole long-term + SurgeryParagonimusPraziquantel / Triclabendazole
H. nanaPraziquantel 25mg/kgFasciolopsisPraziquantel
D. latum (B12 deficiency)Praziquantel + B12

📊 Spaced Repetition Tracker

Review IntervalDateScore (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

TopicKnown? (✓/✗)Last Reviewed

▸ 14.1.09 Chlamydial Pneumonia Psittacosis

tags: [medicine, davidson, infectious-disease, chlamydia, psittacosis, pneumonia, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 11: Infectious disease

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: high

exam_relevance: "FCPS: High-yield | MRCP: Core | C. pneumoniae (CAP), C. psittaci (Psittacosis), C. trachomatis (LGV/STI - Ch 15), serology, PCR, doxycycline/azithromycin"

see_also: "[[Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)]], [[Fever in Returned Traveller & FUO]], [[Rickettsial Infections]], [[Viral Pneumonias]]"

created: 2025-06-17

modified: 2025-06-17

Chlamydial Pneumonia & Psittacosis

[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Chlamydiae
FCPS/MRCP Focus: C. pneumoniae (Atypical CAP), C. psittaci (Psittacosis — Bird Exposure), C. trachomatis (LGV/STI — Ch 15), Serology/PCR, Doxycycline/Azithromycin

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Distinguish Chlamydia Species: C. pneumoniae (Respiratory), C. psittaci (Psittacosis — Birds), C. trachomatis (LGV/STI — Ch 15)
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Syndromes: C. pneumoniae = Atypical CAP (Young Adults, Persistent Cough), C. psittaci = Psittacosis (Bird Exposure, Severe Pneumonia, Horder Nodes)
  • [ ] Diagnose: Serology (MIF — Gold Standard), PCR (Respiratory Specimens), Culture (Specialised, Slow)
  • [ ] Manage: Doxycycline 100mg BD / Azithromycin 500mg Day 1 then 250mg OD / Levofloxacin/Moxifloxacin (Alternatives)
  • [ ] Recognise Complications: Endocarditis, Encephalitis, Hepatitis, Reactive Arthritis

📖 Chlamydia Species Overview

SpeciesPrimary DiseaseTransmissionKey Features
C. pneumoniae (TWAR)Atypical CAP, Bronchitis, Pharyngitis, SinusitisRespiratory Droplets (Person-to-Person)Young Adults, Persistent Cough (Weeks), Wheeze, Hoarseness, Mild
C. psittaciPsittacosis (Ornithosis) — Zoonotic PneumoniaBird Droppings/Secretions (Aerosol), Parrot, Pigeon, Turkey, DuckBird Exposure, Severe Pneumonia, Horder Nodes (Splenic), Hepatitis, Endocarditis, Meningitis
C. trachomatis (L1-L3)LGV (L1-L3) — Genital Ulcer, Buboes, Proctitis; Ocular (Trachoma)Sexual Contact (L1-L3) / Flies/Fomites (Trachoma)LGV: Painful Inguinal Buboes, Proctocolitis, Genital Ulcer; Trachoma: Blindness
[!tip] C. pneumoniae = Atypical CAP in Young Adults (Persistent Dry Cough, Hoarseness, Low-Grade Fever). C. psittaci = Bird Exposure + Severe Pneumonia + Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses).

📖 C. pneumoniae — "Walking Pneumonia" Agent

Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
AgeChildren (5-15y) & Young Adults; Reinfection Common
TransmissionRespiratory Droplets (Person-to-Person); Incubation 3-4 Weeks
SeasonalityYear-Round (Peaks Winter/Spring)
Seroprevalence50-70% Adults (Past Infection Common)

Clinical Features

FeatureTypical Presentation
OnsetInsidious (1-3 Weeks Prodrome: Sore Throat, Hoarseness, Dry Cough)
CoughPersistent, Dry → Productive, Weeks (Hallmark: Prolonged Cough >3 Weeks)
FeverLow-Grade / Absent (Often Afebrile)
Extra-PulmonaryPharyngitis, Laryngitis, Sinusitis, Otitis Media, Reactive Arthritis, Guillain-Barré (Rare)
CXRPatchy/Interstitial Infiltrates (Often Lower Lobe), Minimal for Symptom Severity
[!tip] C. pneumoniae = "Walking Pneumonia": Young Adult, Persistent Cough >3 Weeks, Hoarseness/Laryngitis, Mild Constitutional, Patchy CXR — Doxycycline/Azithromycin.

📖 Psittacosis (Ornithosis) — C. psittaci

Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
ReservoirBirds: Psittacines (Parrots, Macaws, Cockatiels), Pigeons, Poultry (Turkeys, Ducks), Wild Birds
TransmissionInhalation of Dried Droppings/Secretions/Feathers (Aerosol); Bird Handlers, Pet Owners, Poultry Workers, Vets
Incubation5-19 Days (Median 10 Days)
OutbreaksPet Shops, Aviaries, Poultry Farms, Abattoirs, Bird Fairs

Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
ConstitutionalHigh Fever, Severe Headache, Myalgia, Photophobia, Prostration
RespiratoryDry Cough → Productive, Dyspnoea, Pleuritic Pain, Rales/Consolidation
HepaticHepatomegaly, Mild Transaminitis, Jaundice (Rare)
SplenicHorder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses) — Pathognomonic (20-30%)
NeurologicalMeningitis, Encephalitis, Cranial Nerve Palsies (Rare)
CardiacMyocarditis, Pericarditis, Endocarditis (Rare)
DermatologicalMaculopapular Rash (10-20%), Erythema Nodosum, Erythema Multiforme
[!tip] Psittacosis = Bird Exposure + Severe Pneumonia + Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses) + Relative Bradycardia. Horder Nodes = Pathognomonic (Splenic Microabscesses on Imaging).

🔬 Diagnostic Workup

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Atypical Pneumonia / Bird Exposure + Fever
↓
**Clinical Suspicion**
↓
**Suspect C. pneumoniae / C. psittaci**
↓
**Serology: MIF (Gold Standard)**
**PCR (Sputum/BAL/Throat Swab)**
**Culture** (Specialised, Egg Yolk Sac / Cell Culture, BSL-3)
**Antigen Detection** (DFA/IHC — Sputum/Tissue)
↓
**MIF IgM/IgG** — *4-fold Rise Paired Sera (2-4w)*
**CF Test** — *Historical, Less Used*
**Rapid, High Sensitivity**
**Slow (Weeks), Low Sensitivity**

Diagnostic Tests Comparison

TestC. pneumoniaeC. psittaciTimingUtility
MIF (Microimmunofluorescence)Gold StandardGold StandardAcute + Convalescent (2-4w Apart)Confirmatory (4-fold Rise)
CF (Complement Fixation)Older StandardOlder StandardConvalescentHistorical
PCR (Sputum/BAL/Throat/NP)High SensitivityHigh SensitivityAcute Phase (Day 1-14)Early Diagnosis
CultureSpecialised (HEL/HL Cells/Egg Yolk)Specialised (Yolk Sac/Cell Culture)WeeksReference Only (BSL-3)
Serum IgM/IgG (EIA/ELISA)Commercial AvailableCommercial AvailableAcute/ConvalescentScreening
[!tip] MIF = Gold Standard for both. PCR = Early Diagnosis. IgM/IgG Rise (4-fold) in Paired Sera = Confirmatory.

💊 Management

Antibiotic Therapy

Infection1st LineAlternativeDuration
C. pneumoniae (CAP)Doxycycline 100mg PO BDAzithromycin 500mg Day 1 → 250mg OD / Levofloxacin 500mg OD / Moxifloxacin 400mg OD10-14 Days (Persistent Cough → Extend)
C. psittaci (Psittacosis)Doxycycline 100mg PO BD (or IV if Severe)Azithromycin 500mg Day 1 → 250mg OD / Erythromycin 500mg QID (Pregnancy)14-21 Days (Prevent Relapse)
Severe Psittacosis (ICU)Doxycycline 100mg IV BD + Hydrocortisone (If Shock)IV Azithromycin / Levofloxacin IV14-21 Days
C. trachomatis (LGV/STI)Doxycycline 100mg BD ×21dAzithromycin 1g Single Dose (Genital) / Erythromycin (Pregnancy)21 Days (LGV)
[!warning] Tetracyclines Contraindicated in Pregnancy/Children <8y → Azithromycin / Erythromycin.
[!tip] Psittacosis = Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14-21 Days (Prevent Relapse). Severe: IV Doxycycline + ICU Support.

🔬 Laboratory Findings

TestC. pneumoniaeC. psittaci
CBCNormal / Mild LeukocytosisLeukopenia (Early), Thrombocytopenia (Possible)
LFTNormal / Mild ↑ ALT/AST↑ ALT/AST (Hepatitis Common), ↑ ALP
CXRPatchy/Interstitial Infiltrates (Lower Lobe)Consolidation / Multifocal Infiltrates, Pleural Effusion
SerologyMIF IgM/IgG (4-fold Rise)MIF IgM/IgG (4-fold Rise)
PCRSputum/BAL/Throat SwabSputum/BAL/Throat Swab
[!tip] Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses) = Pathognomonic for Psittacosis (Seen on CT/US in 20-30%).

💊 Treatment Summary

Infection1st LineDurationNotes
C. pneumoniae CAPDoxycycline 100mg PO BD10-14 DaysPersistent Cough → Extend to 21d
C. psittaci (Psittacosis)Doxycycline 100mg BD PO/IV14-21 DaysPrevent Relapse
Severe PsittacosisDoxycycline 100mg IV BD + ICU Support14-21 DaysICU Monitoring
Pregnancy/ChildrenAzithromycin 500mg Day 1 → 250mg OD / Erythromycin10-14 DaysAvoid Tetracyclines

🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionC. pneumoniaeC. psittaci
Mycoplasma pneumoniaeYounger Age, Cold Agglutinins, Cold Haemolysis, Rash, Stevens-JohnsonBird Exposure, Horder Nodes, Hepatitis
LegionellaDiarrhoea, Confusion, Hyponatraemia, Urinary Ag+Bird Exposure, Horder Nodes, Relative Bradycardia
Viral PneumoniaMyalgia, GI Symptoms, Viral PCR+Bird Exposure, Hepatitis, Horder Nodes
Typical Bacterial CAPAbrupt Onset, High Fever, Lobar Consolidation, LeucocytosisBird Exposure, Relative Bradycardia, Hepatitis
Viral Pneumonia (Influenza/COVID)Rapid Onset, Myalgia, GI, Viral PCR+Bird Exposure, Horder Nodes, Relative Bradycardia

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
C. pneumoniaeAtypical CAP in Young Adults, Persistent Dry Cough >3 Weeks, Hoarseness, Mild, Patchy CXR
C. psittaci (Psittacosis)Bird Exposure (Parrots, Pigeons, Poultry), Severe Pneumonia, Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses), Relative Bradycardia
DiagnosisMIF (Gold Standard) — 4-fold Rise IgG/IgM Paired Sera; PCR (Early, Sputum/BAL)
TreatmentDoxycycline 100mg BD (1st Line All Chlamydia); Azithromycin/Macrolide (Pregnancy/Alternative); 14-21 Days (Psittacosis Longer to Prevent Relapse)
Pregnancy/ChildrenAzithromycin/Erythromycin (Avoid Tetracyclines)
Psittacosis CluesBird Exposure, Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses), Relative Bradycardia, Hepatitis, Relative Bradycardia
ComplicationsEndocarditis, Myocarditis, Meningitis, Hepatitis, Reactive Arthritis

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the typical clinical presentation of C. pneumoniae pneumonia?
  • Young Adult, Insidious Onset, Persistent Dry Cough >3 Weeks, Hoarseness/Laryngitis, Low-Grade Fever, Patchy CXR.
  1. What are the classic features of Psittacosis?
  • Bird Exposure, High Fever, Severe Headache, Myalgia, Pneumonia, Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses), Relative Bradycardia, Hepatitis.
  1. What is the diagnostic test of choice for Chlamydia species?
  • Microimmunofluorescence (MIF) — Gold Standard; 4-fold Rise in Paired Sera (IgG/IgM); PCR for Early Diagnosis.
  1. How do you treat C. pneumoniae pneumonia?
  • Doxycycline 100mg PO BD × 10-14 Days (or Azithromycin 500mg Day 1 → 250mg OD ×5 Days).
  1. How does Psittacosis differ from typical bacterial pneumonia?
  • Bird Exposure, Relative Bradycardia, Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses), Hepatitis, Relative Bradycardia, Photophobia, Severe Headache.
  1. What are Horder Nodes and their significance?
  • Splenic Microabscesses seen on Imaging (US/CT) in 20-30% of Psittacosis; Pathognomonic.
  1. What is the treatment of choice for Psittacosis?
  • Doxycycline 100mg BD PO/IV × 14-21 Days; Azithromycin/Erythromycin if Pregnant/Child.
  1. How do you differentiate C. pneumoniae from Mycoplasma pneumoniae?
  • C. pneumoniae: Younger Adults, Prolonged Cough, Hoarseness, No Cold Agglutinins; M. pneumoniae: Younger, Cold Agglutinins, Haemolysis, Rash, Stevens-Johnson Risk.
  1. What is the significance of Relative Bradycardia in Psittacosis?
  • Relative Bradycardia = Fever without Expected Tachycardia; Seen in Psittacosis, Typhoid, Legionella, Brucellosis, Drug Effect (Beta-Blockers).
  1. How do you diagnose C. pneumoniae in a patient with persistent cough?
  • Serology (MIF) — 4-fold Rise Paired Sera; PCR (Sputum/BAL) — Rapid; CXR: Patchy Interstitial Infiltrates.
  1. What is the treatment duration for Psittacosis to prevent relapse?
  • 14-21 Days Doxycycline (Shorter Courses → Relapse Risk).
  1. Differentiate C. pneumoniae from C. psittaci clinically.
  • C. pneumoniae: Community-Acquired, Young Adult, Persistent Cough, Hoarseness, Mild; C. psittaci: Bird Exposure, Severe, Horder Nodes, Hepatitis, Relative Bradycardia.

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
C. pneumoniae vs M. pneumoniaeC. pneumoniae: Hoarseness, No Cold Agglutinins, Older Age; M. pneumoniae: Cold Agglutinins+, Rash, Younger, Haemolysis
Psittacosis vs OrnithosisSame Disease; Psittacosis = Parrot-Borne, Ornithosis = Bird-Borne (General)
C. pneumoniae vs LegionellaC. pneumoniae: No Diarrhoea/Confusion/Hyponatraemia, No Urinary Ag; Legionella: GI/Neuro, Urinary Ag+, Hyponatraemia
C. psittaci vs TyphoidPsittacosis: Bird Exposure, Horder Nodes, Relative Bradycardia, Hepatitis; Typhoid: Relative Bradycardia, Rose Spots, Blood Culture+
C. pneumoniae vs Viral PneumoniaC. pneumoniae: Persistent Cough >3 Weeks, Hoarseness, Patchy CXR; Viral: Rapid Onset, Myalgia, GI, Viral PCR+
MnemonicMeaning
"Psittacosis = Bird + Horder Nodes + Bradycardia"Psittacosis Triad
"C. pneumoniae = Persistent Cough >3 Weeks + Hoarseness"C. pneumoniae Hallmarks
"Doxycycline = All Chlamydia"Universal Treatment
"Horder Nodes = Splenic Microabscesses = Psittacosis"Pathognomonic
"MIF = Gold Standard = 4-fold Rise Paired Sera"Serology Gold Standard
"Bird Exposure = Think Psittacosis"Exposure History

🗺️ Mind Map

Chlamydial Pneumonia & Psittacosis
C. pneumoniae
Atypical CAP
Young Adults
Persistent Cough >3wks
Hoarseness/Laryngitis
Patchy CXR
MIF/PCR Diagnosis
Doxycycline 10-14d

Bird Exposure (Parrots/Pigeons/Poultry)
Severe Pneumonia

Hepatitis/Photophobia

Doxycycline 14-21d
Relapse if Short Course
C. trachomatis
LGV (L1-L3): Genital Ulcer, Buboes, Proctitis
Trachoma (A-C): Blindness
STI: Azithromycin 1g Single Dose
LGV: Doxycycline 21d
Diagnosis
MIF: Gold Standard (4-fold Rise)
PCR: Early (Sputum/BAL)
Culture: Specialised, BSL-3
CF: Historical
Treatment
Doxycycline 100mg BD (All)
Azithromycin/Erythromycin (Pregnancy/Children)
Duration: C.pneum 10-14d, C.psittaci 14-21d
Complications
Endocarditis, Meningitis, Hepatitis

📋 One-Page Revision Card

CHLAMYDIAL PNEUMONIA & PSITTACOSIS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
C. pneumoniae: Atypical CAP, Young Adult, Persistent Cough >3wks, Hoarseness, Patchy CXR, Doxy 10-14d
C. psittaci (Psittacosis): Bird Exposure, Severe Pneumonia, Horder Nodes (Splenic Microabscesses), Relative Bradycardia, Hepatitis, Doxy 14-21d
Diagnosis: MIF (Gold Standard) — 4-fold Rise Paired Sera (IgG/IgM); PCR (Sputum/BAL) Early
Treatment: Doxycycline 100mg BD (All Chlamydia); Azithromycin/Erythro (Pregnancy/Children)
Psittacosis Clues: Bird Exposure, Horder Nodes, Relative Bradycardia, Hepatitis, Photophobia
Horder Nodes: Splenic Microabscesses (CT/US) — Pathognomonic
C. trachomatis LGV: Genital Ulcer + Buboes, Doxy 21d; Trachoma: Azithromycin

🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowC. pneumoniae = Atypical CAP (Persistent cough, hoarseness); C. psittaci = Bird exposure + Horder nodes + Bradycardia; MIF gold standard (4-fold rise); Doxycycline 1st line all Chlamydia; Azithromycin/Erythromycin for pregnancy/children; Psittacosis 14-21d doxy to prevent relapse
Should KnowC. pneumoniae vs M. pneumoniae differentiation; Psittacosis vs Q fever vs Legionella; Horder nodes CT findings; PCR vs MIF sensitivity/timing; Complications (endocarditis, meningitis, reactive arthritis); Pregnancy management (azithromycin/erythromycin); Bird exposure history taking
Nice to KnowC. pneumoniae role in atherosclerosis (controversial); C. pneumoniae in asthma exacerbations; Chlamydial genomics; C. psittaci zoonotic cycles; Psittacosis in poultry workers; C. trachomatis serovars (LGV vs ocular); Trachoma elimination strategy (SAFE); Chlamydial vaccine research; C. pneumoniae in Alzheimer's (controversial)
