Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Definitions (HAP, VAP, NV-HAP, early vs late onset)
  2. MDR risk factors
  3. Diagnostic criteria (clinical, radiological, microbiological)
  4. Quantitative vs qualitative cultures
  5. Empirical therapy by MDR risk (local antibiogram)
  6. De-escalation strategy at 48–72h
  7. Treatment duration
  8. VAP prevention bundle
  9. Pathogen-specific targeted therapy (MSSA, MRSA, Pseudomonas, ESBL, CRE, Acinetobacter, Stenotrophomonas)
  10. Special populations

Short Note Skeleton

  • HAP: ≥48h admission; VAP: ≥48h intubation; Early ≤4d CAP-like; Late ≥5d MDR
  • MDR risk: prior abx, hosp≥5d, ICU, vent, colonisation
  • Diagnosis: infiltrate + signs + quant culture (BAL≥10⁴, PSB≥10³)
  • Low MDR: single β-lactam; High MDR: anti-Pseudo β-lactam + anti-MRSA
  • De-escalate 48-72h: neg quant→STOP; pos→narrow; IV→Oral if stable
  • Duration: 7 days
  • VAP bundle: HOB 30-45°, sedation hold+SBT, subglottic suction, CHX, cuff pressure
  • CRE: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin; Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX

Viva One-Liners

  • HAP ≥48h admission; VAP ≥48h intubation
  • Early ≤4d = CAP flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora
  • MDR risk: PHI VK (Prior abx, Hosp≥5d, ICU, Vent, Known colonisation)
  • VAP culture: BAL 10⁴, PSB 10³, ETA 10⁵
  • High MDR empirical: Anti-Pseudo β-lactam + Vanco/Linezolid
  • 7 days duration
  • VAP bundle: HOB, SBT, subglottic, CHX, cuff
  • CRE: CAV/MV/IR; Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 65M, post-op day 6, vented, new infiltrate, purulent secretions. Prior pip-tazo 2w ago. MDR risk: YES (prior abx, ≥5d, vent). Empirical: Meropenem + Vancomycin. BAL at 48h: Pseudomonas susceptible → Meropenem monotherapy 7d.
  • 40F, ICU day 10, VAP. BAL: CRE (NDM+). Empirical: Meropenem + Vanco. Targeted: Ceftazidime-avibactam 2.5g 8h ×7-14d.
  • 55M, bronchiectasis, HAP day 4. CXR: new consolidation. Low MDR risk (early, no prior abx). Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin (atypical cover). Sputum: H. influenzae → Amoxicillin 7d.

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

HAP/VAP: HAP ≥48h admission; VAP ≥48h intubation. Early ≤4d = CAP flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora. MDR risk: prior abx 90d, hosp≥5d, ICU, vent, colonisation. Diagnosis: infiltrate + signs + quant culture (BAL≥10⁴, PSB≥10³). Low MDR: single β-lactam. High MDR: anti-Pseudo β-lactam + anti-MRSA (Vanco/Linezolid). De-escalate 48-72h: neg quant→STOP; pos→narrow; IV→Oral if stable. 7 days duration. VAP bundle: HOB 30-45°, daily sedation hold+SBT, subglottic suction, CHX oral care, cuff 20-30. CRE: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin (avoid tigecycline VAP). Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX.

Summary

Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP) = pneumonia ≥48h after hospital admission, not incubating at admission. Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) = pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation. Non-ventilator HAP (NV-HAP) = HAP without mechanical ventilation. Early-onset (≤4 days hospitalisation) = CAP-like flora (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, MSSA). Late-onset (≥5 days) = MDR flora (Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter, ESBL Enterobacteriaceae). MDR risk factors: prior IV antibiotics ≤90 days, hospitalisation ≥5 days, ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, known colonisation. Diagnosis: new/progressive infiltrate + ≥2 clinical signs (fever, leukocytosis/leukopenia, purulent secretions, worsening gas exchange) + microbiological confirmation. Quantitative cultures preferred for VAP: BAL ≥10⁴ CFU/mL, PSB ≥10³ CFU/mL, ETA ≥10⁵ CFU/mL. Empirical antibiotics: guided by LOCAL antibiogram + MDR risk. Low MDR risk = single anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo, Cefepime, Meropenem, Ceftriaxone, Levofloxacin). High MDR risk = anti-pseudomonal β-lactam + anti-MRSA agent (Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h or Linezolid 600mg 12h). De-escalation at 48–72h: negative quantitative culture + improving → STOP; single pathogen → narrow to targeted agent; switch IV→oral if clinically stable. Duration: 7 days standard for uncomplicated HAP/VAP. VAP prevention bundle: HOB 30–45°, daily sedation interruption + SBT, subglottic secretion drainage, chlorhexidine oral care, cuff pressure 20–30 cmH₂O, DVT/PUD prophylaxis. CRE: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-relebactam, plazomicin, polymyxin-based; avoid tigecycline for VAP (poor lung penetration). Stenotrophomonas maltophilia: TMP-SMX 5mg/kg (TMP) 6h first-line.

MCQs (10)

  1. VAP is defined as pneumonia developing:

A. ≥24h after intubation

B. ≥48h after intubation

C. ≥72h after intubation

D. ≥5 days after intubation

E. Any time after intubation

  1. Which is a risk factor for MDR pathogens in HAP/VAP?

A. Hospitalisation 2 days

B. Prior IV antibiotics within 90 days

C. Age >65

D. COPD without recent antibiotics

E. Single comorbidity

  1. Quantitative culture threshold for BAL in VAP:

A. ≥10³ CFU/mL

B. ≥10⁴ CFU/mL

C. ≥10⁵ CFU/mL

D. ≥10⁶ CFU/mL

E. Any growth

  1. Preferred empirical regimen for HIGH MDR risk VAP:

A. Ceftriaxone alone

B. Levofloxacin alone

C. Piperacillin-tazobactam + Vancomycin

D. Meropenem alone

E. Cefepime + Azithromycin

  1. Standard treatment duration for uncomplicated HAP/VAP:

A. 5 days

B. 7 days

C. 10 days

D. 14 days

E. 21 days

  1. Which VAP bundle component has the STRONGEST evidence for reducing VAP incidence?

A. Chlorhexidine oral care

B. Subglottic secretion drainage

C. HOB elevation 30–45°

D. Daily sedation hold

E. Peptic ulcer prophylaxis

  1. First-line treatment for Stenotrophomonas maltophilia pneumonia:

A. Meropenem

B. TMP-SMX

C. Ciprofloxacin

C. Ceftazidime-avibactam

E. Tigecycline

  1. Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) VAP — AVOID:

A. Ceftazidime-avibactam

B. Meropenem-vaborbactam

C. Tigecycline (poor lung penetration)

D. Imipenem-relebactam

E. Plazomicin

  1. De-escalation at 48–72h: quantitative BAL culture negative (<10⁴) + patient improving:

A. Continue empirical antibiotics for 7 days

B. Stop antibiotics

C. Switch to oral antibiotics

D. Repeat BAL in 24h

E. Add antifungal

  1. Early-onset HAP (day 3 post-admission) most likely pathogen:

A. Pseudomonas aeruginosa

B. S. pneumoniae

C. Acinetobacter baumannii

D. MRSA

E. ESBL Klebsiella

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 70-year-old man, post-abdominal surgery day 6, mechanically ventilated 4 days. New infiltrate, purulent secretions, fever 39°C. Received piperacillin-tazobactam for intra-abdominal infection 3 weeks ago. MDR risk factors?

A. None

B. Prior antibiotics (within 90d) + hospitalisation ≥5d + mechanical ventilation

C. Only hospitalisation ≥5d

D. Only mechanical ventilation

E. Only prior antibiotics

  1. A patient with VAP. BAL quantitative culture: 5×10³ CFU/mL P. aeruginosa. Clinical status improving. Action?

A. Continue empirical broad-spectrum

B. De-escalate to targeted anti-pseudomonal monotherapy ×7 days total

C. Stop antibiotics (culture <10⁴)

D. Add second Gram-negative agent

E. Extend to 14 days

  1. A 60-year-old woman with VAP day 10. Known MRSA colonised (nares). Empirical regimen?

A. Cefepime alone

B. Meropenem + Vancomycin

C. Pip-tazo + Linezolid

D. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin

E. Levofloxacin + Vancomycin

  1. A patient with VAP. Quantitative PSB culture: no growth. Patient afebrile, improving gas exchange, WBC normalising. Best action?

A. Continue antibiotics 7 days

B. Stop antibiotics

C. Switch to oral levofloxacin

D. Repeat PSB in 24h

E. Start antifungals

  1. CRE VAP (NDM-producing Klebsiella). Targeted regimen?

A. Tigecycline + Polymyxin B

B. Ceftazidime-avibactam 2.5g IV 8h

C. High-dose meropenem alone

D. Ertapenem + Polymyxin

E. Fosfomycin IV

  1. VAP prevention: subglottic secretion drainage endotracheal tube indicated for:

A. All intubated patients

B. Expected ventilation >24–48 hours

C. Only COPD patients

D. Only if VAP already diagnosed

E. Only immunocompromised

  1. A 55-year-old man with HAP day 5. No MDR risk factors. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation. Blood cultures negative. Sputum: H. influenzae (ampicillin-susceptible). Targeted therapy?

A. Meropenem ×7d

B. Amoxicillin/Amoxicillin-clavulanate ×7d

C. Levofloxacin ×7d

D. Ceftriaxone ×14d

E. Pip-tazo ×7d

  1. A patient with Acinetobacter baumannii VAP (carbapenem-susceptible). Preferred targeted therapy?

A. Tigecycline

B. Meropenem 1g IV 8h (or Ampicillin-sulbactam high-dose)

C. Polymyxin B

C. Ceftazidime-avibactam

E. Minocycline

  1. IV to oral switch in HAP/VAP — criteria same as CAP EXCEPT:

A. Afebrile 24h

B. HR ≤100

C. Chest X-ray cleared (NOT required)

D. SBP ≥90

E. Tolerating oral

  1. Duration of therapy for uncomplicated VAP with clinical stability at day 7:

A. Stop antibiotics (7 days total)

B. Continue to 10 days

C. Continue to 14 days

D. Switch to oral for 7 more days

E. Extend until CXR clear

Flashcards

  • Q: HAP definition

A: Pneumonia ≥48h after admission, not incubating at admission

  • Q: VAP definition

A: Pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation

  • Q: Early vs Late onset

A: Early ≤4d = CAP flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora

  • Q: MDR risk factors

A: Prior abx 90d, hosp ≥5d, ICU, vent, colonisation

  • Q: VAP quant culture thresholds

A: BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³, ETA ≥10⁵

  • Q: High MDR empirical

A: Anti-Pseudo β-lactam + Anti-MRSA

  • Q: Low MDR empirical

A: Single β-lactam

  • Q: De-escalation 48-72h

A: Neg quant→STOP; Pos→narrow; IV→Oral if stable

  • Q: Duration

A: 7 days standard

  • Q: VAP bundle

A: HOB 30-45°, sedation hold+SBT, subglottic suction, CHX, cuff 20-30

  • Q: CRE treatment

A: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin/Polymyxin (not tigecycline for VAP)

  • Q: Stenotrophomonas

A: TMP-SMX first-line

  • Q: Acinetobacter

A: Meropenem/Sulbactam/Polymyxin

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — VAP = pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation.
  2. B — Prior IV antibiotics within 90 days is a major MDR risk factor. Hospitalisation ≥5d, ICU, ventilation, and known colonisation are the others.
  3. B — BAL quantitative threshold for VAP = ≥10⁴ CFU/mL. PSB = ≥10³, ETA = ≥10⁵.
  4. C — High MDR risk VAP: anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo/Meropenem/Cefepime) + anti-MRSA agent (Vancomycin/Linezolid).
  5. B — 7 days is standard for uncomplicated HAP/VAP (IDSA/ATS 2016).
  6. B — Subglottic secretion drainage reduces VAP incidence by ~50% (strongest evidence). Chlorhexidine reduces VAP but not mortality.
  7. B — Stenotrophomonas maltophilia is intrinsically resistant to most β-lactams; TMP-SMX is first-line.
  8. C — Tigecycline has poor lung penetration (low ELF concentrations); avoid for VAP. Use newer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitors.
  9. B — Negative quantitative culture (BAL <10⁴) has high NPV; if patient improving, STOP antibiotics.
  10. B — Early-onset (≤4d) HAP/VAP has CAP-like flora; S. pneumoniae most common.

SBAs

  1. B — Prior antibiotics 3 weeks ago (within 90d) + hospitalisation day 6 (≥5d) + mechanical ventilation = 3 MDR risk factors.
  2. B — BAL 5×10³ = below threshold (10⁴) but P. aeruginosa grown; targeted anti-pseudomonal monotherapy for 7 days total. Not "stop" because pathogen identified.
  3. B — MRSA colonisation = MDR risk factor for MRSA; Meropenem (anti-pseudomonal) + Vancomycin (anti-MRSA) covers both. Cefepime less reliable for Pseudomonas in high-resistance settings.
  4. B — Negative quantitative PSB + clinical improvement = STOP antibiotics (high NPV).
  5. B — NDM CRE: Ceftazidime-avibactam preferred (active vs NDM). Meropenem-vaborbactam/Imipenem-relebactam NOT active vs NDM. Tigecycline avoid for VAP.
  6. B — Subglottic suction tubes indicated for expected ventilation >24–48h (cost-benefit).
  7. B — H. influenzae ampicillin-susceptible: narrow to amoxicillin/co-amoxiclav ×7 days. No need for broader agents.
  8. B — Carbapenem-susceptible Acinetobacter: Meropenem high-dose (or ampicillin-sulbactam high-dose) preferred. Tigecycline/polymyxin for resistant strains.
  9. C — CXR clearance NOT required for IV→oral switch; same clinical stability criteria as CAP.
  10. A — Clinical stability at day 7 = stop antibiotics (7 days total). Do not extend for CXR clearance.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.23 Infection In Immunocompromised Host Transplant Biologics

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, immunocompromised, transplant, biologics, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Immunocompromised Host Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics, Chemotherapy)

Related: [[Febrile Neutropenia]], [[Invasive Candidiasis]], [[Mucormycosis]], [[Pneumocystis Pneumonia]], [[CMV Disease]], [[TB in HIV]], [[Healthcare-Associated Infections (CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI, VAP)]], [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]]

[!important]
Immunocompromised host = defective innate/adaptive immunity → ↑ risk, atypical presentation, opportunistic pathogens. Risk stratification by deficit: neutropenia, T-cell deficiency, B-cell/humoral, complement, phagocyte, barrier. Transplant timeline: 0–1m (surgical, hospital flora), 1–6m (opportunistic: CMV, PCP, fungi), >6m (community + chronic rejection). Biologics: anti-TNF → TB, intracellular; anti-CD20 → HBV reactivation, PML; IL-17/23 → Candida, TB.

Learning Objectives

  • Classify immunodeficiency by immune deficit and risk profile
  • Apply transplant timeline for infection risk (0–1m, 1–6m, >6m)
  • Recognise biologic-specific infection risks (anti-TNF, anti-CD20, integrin inhibitors, JAK inhibitors)
  • Select appropriate prophylaxis (PCP, fungal, viral, TB)
  • Diagnose and manage key opportunistic infections (CMV, PCP, EBV-PTLD, BK virus, HHV-6)
  • Apply vaccination principles in immunocompromised (timing, live vs inactivated)

Classification by Immune Deficit

DeficitCausesTypical Infections
NeutropeniaChemo, HSCT, drugs (ganciclovir, valproate), aplastic anaemiaBacteria (Gram-neg, Staph), Candida, Aspergillus, Gram-neg sepsis
T-cell DeficiencyHIV, transplant (CNI, mTOR, steroids), biologics (anti-TNF, alemtuzumab), chemoViruses (CMV, EBV, BK, HHV-6, VZV), TB, fungi (Pneumocystis, Cryptococcus), Listeria, Nocardia, Toxoplasma
B-cell / HumoralAnti-CD20 (rituximab), BTK inhibitors, CLL, myeloma, hypogammaglobulinaemiaEncapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae), Giardia, enteroviruses, chronic sinopulmonary
ComplementC1q–C9 deficiency, eculizumab, C1-INH deficiencyEncapsulated bacteria (Neisseria, S. pneumoniae), immune complex disease
PhagocyteCGD, diabetes, steroids, dialysis, BurnsCatalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Burkholderia, Nocardia, Aspergillus)
Barrier DefectBurns, surgery, lines, drains, mucositisSkin flora (Staph, Strep), Candida, Gram-neg

Transplant Timeline — Infection Risk by Period

PeriodTimeframeKey RisksTypical Pathogens
Early0–1 monthSurgical, hospital-acquiredWound infection, bacteremia (CoNS, Gram-neg), UTI, C. difficile, donor-derived
Intermediate1–6 monthsOPPORTUNISTIC (peak)CMV (peak 1–4m), PCP, invasive fungi (Aspergillus, Candida), EBV-PTLD, BK virus, HHV-6, adenovirus, TB, Nocardia, Listeria
Late>6 monthsCommunity-acquired + chronic rejectionCommunity viruses, encapsulated bacteria, chronic viral (BK, CMV), chronic rejection
All periodsAny timeNet state of immunosuppressionNet state = baseline risk + current immunosuppression + exposures
[!key]
CMV = MOST IMPORTANT opportunistic infection in transplant. Peak 1–4 months. PCP prophylaxis universal 6–12 months. Antifungal prophylaxis in high-risk (lung, liver, heart, re-transplant).

Biologic-Specific Infection Risks

Biologic ClassTargetKey Infection RisksScreening/Monitoring
Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab, golimumab)TNF-αTB (reactivation), intracellular bacteria (Listeria, Salmonella, Nocardia), histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, hepatitis B reactivationTB screen (IGRA/TST) BEFORE; HBV screen; monitor for infection
Anti-IL-12/23 (ustekinumab)IL-12/IL-23TB, fungal, viral (similar to anti-TNF but lower risk)TB screen before
Anti-IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab)IL-17ACandida (mucocutaneous), TB (rare)Monitor for Candida
Anti-IL-23 (guselkumab, risankizumab)IL-23TB, fungal (lower risk than anti-TNF)TB screen before
Anti-IL-6 (tocilizumab, sarilumab)IL-6RBacterial, TB, diverticulitis, hepatotoxicityTB screen; monitor LFTs, neutrophils
Anti-CD20 (rituximab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab)CD20 (B-cells)HBV reactivation (HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+), PML (JCV), hypogammaglobulinaemia, encapsulated bacteria, VZVHBV screen (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; IgG levels; JCV index if natalizumab hx
Anti-CD52 (alemtuzumab)CD52 (T/B/NK)Severe T-cell depletion → CMV, VZV, Listeria, Nocardia, TB, fungal, PMLIntensive monitoring; prophylaxis (PCP, viral, fungal)
Anti-integrin (natalizumab, vedolizumab)α4β1/α4β7 integrinPML (JCV) — natalizumab; vedolizumab gut-selective = lower PML riskJCV antibody index (natalizumab); monitor
JAK Inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib)JAK1/2/3Herpes zoster (VZV) ↑↑, TB, bacterial, fungal, CMV, EBV, lymphopeniaVZV vaccination before; TB screen; monitor lymphocytes
BTK Inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib)BTKBleeding, atrial fibrillation, infections (sinopulmonary, fungal), PML riskMonitor IgG, infections
BCL-2 Inhibitor (venetoclax)BCL-2TLS, neutropenia, infectionsTLS prophylaxis; monitor counts
[!tip]
TB screen (IGRA/TST) REQUIRED BEFORE starting anti-TNF, anti-IL-12/23, anti-IL-17, anti-IL-23, JAK inhibitors, alemtuzumab. HBV screen (HBsAg, anti-HBc) REQUIRED BEFORE anti-CD20, anti-TNF, JAK inhibitors.

Key Opportunistic Infections in Transplant

CMV Disease

AspectDetails
RiskD+/R− (highest), lung/heart/intestinal transplant, lymphocyte-depleting induction
SyndromesViraemia, syndrome (fever, leucopenia, hepatitis), tissue-invasive (pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, encephalitis)
DiagnosisQuantitative PCR (blood) — viral load guides pre-emptive therapy
Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptiveHigh-risk: prophylaxis (valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m); Standard-risk: pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m)
TreatmentValganciclovir 900mg BD (oral) OR Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h ×2–3w → maintenance; Foscarnet/Cidofovir if resistant (UL97/UL54 mut)
ResistanceUL97 mutation = ganciclovir/valganciclovir resistance; UL54 = cidofovir/foscarnet resistance

PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia)

AspectDetails
RiskAll transplant (T-cell suppression); HSCT; biologics (anti-TNF, alemtuzumab); HIV CD4<200
ProphylaxisTMP-SMX 960mg PO daily (or 3×/wk); alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, inhaled pentamidine
Duration≥6–12 months post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
TreatmentHigh-dose TMP-SMX 15–20mg/kg TMP IV/PO q6h ×21d + steroids if hypoxic

EBV-PTLD (Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder)

AspectDetails
RiskEBV D+/R−, intense immunosuppression (OKT3, ATG), paediatric, intestinal/lung transplant
SpectrumEarly lesions → polymorphic PTLD → monomorphic PTLD (lymphoma)
DiagnosisEBV PCR (blood) — rising viral load; biopsy for histology
TreatmentReduce immunosuppression (RI) = cornerstone; Rituximab (anti-CD20); EBV-CTL; chemo for monomorphic

BK Virus Nephropathy

AspectDetails
RiskKidney transplant; immunosuppression (Tacrolimus, MMF); ureteric stent
DiagnosisQuantitative PCR (blood, urine) — decoy cells in urine; biopsy (SV40 staining)
TreatmentReduce immunosuppression (reduce/stop Tac/MMF); Leflunomide, Cidofovir, IVIG (adjunctive)

Fungal Infections in Immunocompromised

FungusHigh-Risk HostProphylaxisTreatment
AspergillusLung transplant, HSCT, prolonged neutropenia, steroidsPosaconazole (HSCT, AML), voriconazole (lung)Voriconazole 1st-line; isavuconazole; L-AmB
CandidaICU, abdominal surgery, neutropenia, TPN, broad antibioticsFluconazole (selective), echinocandin (high-risk)Echinocandin 1st-line; step-down fluconazole
MucormycosisDKA, neutropenia, deferoxamine, steroids, transplantNone standardL-AmB + urgent surgery
PneumocystisAll transplant, HIV CD4<200, biologicsTMP-SMX universalHigh-dose TMP-SMX + steroids
CryptococcusHIV CD4<100, transplant, steroidsFluconazole (targeted)L-AmB + flucytosine → fluconazole

Prophylaxis Protocols — Transplant (Example: Kidney)

PathogenAgentDurationHigh-Risk Indications
PCPTMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk6–12 monthsAll (lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression)
CMVValganciclovir 900mg OD3–6 months (prophylaxis) OR pre-emptiveD+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion
FungalFluconazole 200mg OD / Posaconazole1–3 monthsLung, liver, heart, re-transplant, prolonged ICU
VZVAcyclovir 400mg BD3–6 monthsSeropositive recipients
HBVEntecavir/TDFIndefiniteHBsAg+ recipients
UTITMP-SMX / Nitrofurantoin3–6 monthsRecurrent UTI, structural abnormality

Vaccination in Immunocompromised

VaccineTypeTimingNotes
Inactivated (Flu, Pneumococcal, Hep A/B, HPV, Tdap, COVID, Rabies, IPV, Hib, MenACWY)InactivatedAnytime; ideally ≥2 weeks BEFORE immunosuppressionMay have reduced response; give extra doses per schedule
Live (MMR, Varicella, Zoster live, Yellow Fever, BCG, Oral Typhoid, Rotavirus, LAIV)Live≥4 weeks BEFORE immunosuppression; CONTRAINDICATED duringExcept: Zoster recombinant (Shingrix) = safe during; MMR/Varicella if CD4>200 in HIV
Zoster Recombinant (Shingrix)Recombinant (non-live)Safe during immunosuppressionPreferred for immunocompromised ≥18y
Post-TransplantInactivated: 3–6m post; Live: ≥2 years post (if off immunosuppression)Check local guidelines
[!warning]
Live vaccines CONTRAINDICATED during immunosuppression. Exception: Shingrix (recombinant) safe. HIV CD4>200: MMR/Varicella may be given.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Transplant timeline: 0–1m (surgical/hospital), 1–6m (opportunistic: CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV-PTLD), >6m (community)
  • CMV = most important opportunistic infection; D+/R− highest risk; prophylaxis vs pre-emptive; valganciclovir/ganciclovir
  • PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX universal 6–12m post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
  • Anti-TNF: TB reactivation (screen IGRA/TST BEFORE); histoplasma, coccidioides, HBV reactivation
  • Anti-CD20 (rituximab): HBV reactivation (screen HBsAg/anti-HBc BEFORE); PML risk; hypogammaglobulinaemia
  • JAK inhibitors: VZV (shingles) ↑↑; TB; vaccinate VZV before; screen TB
  • Alemtuzumab: severe T-cell depletion; intense prophylaxis (PCP, viral, fungal)
  • PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 6–12m post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
  • Fungal prophylaxis: posaconazole/voriconazole in high-risk (lung, liver, heart, HSCT)
  • Vaccination: live vaccines contraindicated during immunosuppression; Shingrix safe; give inactivated ≥2w before

Common Viva Questions

  1. Transplant infection timeline? 0–1m: surgical/hospital; 1–6m: opportunistic (CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV); >6m: community.
  2. CMV prophylaxis vs pre-emptive? High-risk (D+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion) = prophylaxis 3–6m; standard-risk = pre-emptive PCR-guided.
  3. Anti-TNF screening? TB (IGRA/TST), HBV BEFORE starting.
  4. Anti-CD20 (rituximab) screening? HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; monitor IgG, infections.
  5. JAK inhibitor infection risk? VZV (shingles) ↑↑, TB, bacterial, fungal; vaccinate VZV before; monitor lymphocytes.
  6. PCP prophylaxis post-transplant? TMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk ×6–12m; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression.
  7. CMV treatment? Valganciclovir 900mg BD or ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h; foscarnet/cidofovir if resistant.
  8. BK virus nephropathy management? Reduce immunosuppression (Tac/MMF); leflunomide/cidofovir/IVIG adjunct.
  9. Anti-CD20 HBV reactivation? Screen HBsAg/anti-HBc BEFORE; prophylactic antivirals if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+.
  10. JAK inhibitor VZV risk? Shingles ↑↑; vaccinate with Shingrix BEFORE starting.
  11. Alemtuzumab prophylaxis? PCP (TMP-SMX), viral (acyclovir/valganciclovir), fungal (posaconazole) — intensive.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
CMV prophylaxis for all transplantOnly high-risk (D+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion); standard-risk = pre-emptive
PCP prophylaxis only 6 monthsContinue lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
Anti-TNF safe if TB screen negativeScreen IGRA/TST BEFORE; but can still reactivate latent TB (false neg) — educate patient
Rituximab only affects B-cellsAlso causes hypogammaglobulinaemia → encapsulated bacteria; PML risk (JCV)
JAK inhibitors = just oral DMARDsSignificant VZV ↑↑, TB, infection risk; vaccinate Shingrix BEFORE
Live vaccines always contraindicatedShingrix (recombinant) = SAFE during immunosuppression
CMV treatment = valganciclovir onlyResistance common (UL97/UL54); foscarnet/cidofovir for resistance
PCP only in HIVTransplant, biologics, steroids, malignancy all at risk
Fungal prophylaxis for all transplantOnly high-risk (lung, liver, heart, re-tx, prolonged neutropenia, steroids)

Mnemonics

  • TRANSPLANT TIMELINE: 0-1m = Surgical/Hospital; 1-6m = Opportunistic (CMV, PCP, Fungi, EBV); >6m = Community
  • CMV RISK: Donor+/Recipient-Neg = Highest; Lung/Heart/Intestinal Tx; Lymphocyte depletion
  • ANTI-TNF SCREEN: TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV BEFORE
  • RITUXIMAB SCREEN: HBV (HBsAg, Anti-HBc) BEFORE
  • JAK INHIBITORS: VZV ↑↑, TB, Lymphopenia → Shingrix BEFORE
  • PCP PROPHYLAXIS: TMP-SMX 6-12m post-tx; Lifelong if Immunosuppressed
  • LIVE VACCINES: Contraindicated During Immunosuppression; Shingrix = Safe
  • ALEMTUZUMAB: Severe T-cell depletion → Intense Prophylaxis (PCP, Viral, Fungal)

Mind Map

Immunocompromised Host
Transplant Timeline
0-1m: Surgical, Hospital
1-6m: Opportunistic (CMV, PCP, Fungi, EBV-PTLD, BK)
>6m: Community + Chronic rejection
Biologics
Anti-TNF: TB, Histo, Cocci, HBV
Anti-CD20: HBV reactivation, PML, Hypogammaglobulinaemia
JAK Inhibitors: VZV↑↑, TB, Lymphopenia
Alemtuzumab: Severe T-cell depletion
Anti-integrin: PML (natalizumab)
Key OIs
CMV: D+/R- highest; Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptive; Valganciclovir
PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal
EBV-PTLD: Reduce IS + Rituximab
BK Virus: Reduce IS
Fungal: Aspergillus (Vorico), Candida (Echinocandin), Mucor (L-AmB+Surg)
Prophylaxis
PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m
CMV: Valganciclovir (high-risk)
Fungal: Posaconazole/Vorico (high-risk)
VZV: Acyclovir
Vaccines
Inactivated: OK (give before if possible)
Live: Contraindicated (except Shingrix)

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Immunocompromised Host
↓
Transplant?
Vaccination: Inactivated OK; Live CONTRAINDICATED (Shingrix OK)
↓
Timeline: 0-1m / 1-6m / >6m
Biologics?
↓
CMV Risk Stratification: D+/R- = Prophylaxis
PCP Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal
Fungal Prophylaxis: High-risk only (Posaconazole/Vorico)
Screen TB + HBV BEFORE
Screen HBV BEFORE; Monitor IgG, PML
Shingrix BEFORE; Screen TB; Monitor Lymphocytes
Intense Prophylaxis: PCP + Viral + Fungal
JCV Index (Natalizumab)

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
Timeline0-1m: surgical/hospital; 1-6m: opportunistic (CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV, BK); >6m: community
CMVD+/R− highest; prophylaxis (high-risk) vs pre-emptive; valganciclovir 1st-line
PCPTMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk ×6–12m (lifelong if ongoing IS)
Anti-TNFScreen TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV BEFORE
Anti-CD20Screen HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; PML risk; hypogammaglobulinaemia
JAK InhibitorsVZV ↑↑; TB; screen TB; Shingrix BEFORE
AlemtuzumabSevere T-cell depletion; intense prophylaxis (PCP + viral + fungal)
PCP ProphylaxisTMP-SMX 6–12m; lifelong if ongoing IS
Fungal ProphylaxisHigh-risk only (lung, liver, heart, HSCT, re-tx)
VaccinesInactivated OK (≥2w before); Live CONTRAINDICATED (Shingrix safe)
BK VirusReduce immunosuppression (Tac/MMF); leflunomide/cidofovir/IVIG adjunct
EBV-PTLDReduce IS = cornerstone; Rituximab; EBV-CTL

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • Transplant timeline 3 periods.
  • CMV risk factors (D+/R− highest).
  • CMV prophylaxis vs pre-emptive.
  • PCP prophylaxis drug/duration.
  • Anti-TNF screen (TB + HBV).
  • Anti-CD20 screen (HBV).
  • JAK inhibitor VZV risk + Shingrix.
  • Alemtuzumab prophylaxis.
  • Live vaccines contraindicated (Shingrix exception).
  • BK virus management (reduce IS).

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Transplant timeline (0-1m, 1-6m, >6m)
  • CMV: D+/R− highest; valganciclovir prophylaxis/pre-emptive
  • PCP: TMP-SMX universal 6-12m
  • Anti-TNF: TB + HBV screen BEFORE
  • Anti-CD20: HBV screen BEFORE; PML risk
  • JAK inhibitors: VZV ↑↑; Shingrix before
  • Live vaccines contraindicated (Shingrix safe)

Should Know

  • CMV pre-emptive vs prophylaxis criteria
  • BK virus nephropathy management
  • EBV-PTLD: reduce IS + rituximab
  • Fungal prophylaxis indications
  • Alemtuzumab intensive prophylaxis
  • Vaccination timing (inactivated ≥2w before; live contraindicated)

Nice to Know

  • CMV resistance mutations (UL97/UL54)
  • EBV-CTL therapy
  • Novel biologics infection risks
  • Post-transplant vaccination schedules
  • HSCT vs solid organ differences

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Transplant timeline and infection risks by period
  2. CMV (risk factors, prophylaxis vs pre-emptive, treatment, resistance)
  3. PCP (prophylaxis, treatment)
  4. Biologic-specific risks (anti-TNF, anti-CD20, JAK, alemtuzumab, anti-integrin)
  5. Other OIs (EBV-PTLD, BK virus, fungal, PCP)
  6. Prophylaxis protocols (PCP, CMV, fungal, viral)
  7. Vaccination principles (live vs inactivated, timing)
  8. Key screening before biologics

Short Note Skeleton

  • Timeline: 0-1m surgical/hospital; 1-6m opportunistic; >6m community
  • CMV: D+/R− high; prophylaxis vs pre-emptive; valganciclovir
  • PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal
  • Anti-TNF: TB+HBV screen
  • Anti-CD20: HBV screen; PML; hypogammaglobulinaemia
  • JAK: VZV↑↑; Shingrix before; TB screen
  • Live vaccines contraindicated; Shingrix safe
  • BK: reduce IS; EBV-PTLD: reduce IS + rituximab

Viva One-Liners

  • Transplant: 0-1m surgical, 1-6m opportunistic, >6m community
  • CMV: D+/R− highest; valganciclovir prophylaxis/pre-emptive
  • PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal
  • Anti-TNF: TB+HBV screen BEFORE
  • Anti-CD20: HBV screen BEFORE; PML risk
  • JAK: VZV↑↑; Shingrix BEFORE
  • Live vaccines NO; Shingrix YES

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Kidney transplant D+/R−, day 45: fever, leukopenia → CMV PCR → valganciclovir pre-emptive
  • Anti-TNF patient, 6 months: fever, weight loss → TB IGRA+ → anti-TB treatment; hold biologic
  • Rituximab patient, HBsAg+ → entecavir prophylaxis; monitor HBV DNA
  • JAK inhibitor patient → shingles → hold JAK, treat with valacyclovir, vaccinate Shingrix after recovery
  • Lung transplant day 30: fever, pulmonary nodule → voriconazole prophylaxis; CT halo sign → Aspergillus

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

IMMUNOCOMPROMISED: Timeline: 0-1m surgical/hospital; 1-6m opportunistic (CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV, BK); >6m community. CMV: D+/R− highest; prophylaxis (high-risk) vs pre-emptive; valganciclovir. PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal. Anti-TNF: TB+HBV screen BEFORE. Anti-CD20: HBV screen BEFORE; PML; hypogammaglobulinaemia. JAK: VZV↑↑; Shingrix BEFORE; TB screen. Live vaccines NO; Shingrix SAFE. BK virus: reduce IS (Tac/MMF). EBV-PTLD: reduce IS + rituximab. Fungal prophylaxis: high-risk only (lung/liver/heart/HSCT).

Summary

Infection in the immunocompromised host varies by type and degree of immune deficit. Transplant recipients follow a timeline: 0–1 month (surgical, hospital flora: wound infection, bacteremia, UTI, C. difficile); 1–6 months (peak opportunistic: CMV, PCP, invasive fungi, EBV-PTLD, BK virus, HHV-6, adenovirus, TB, Nocardia, Listeria); >6 months (community-acquired, chronic rejection). CMV = most important opportunistic infection; D+/R− highest risk; prophylaxis (valganciclovir) for high-risk vs pre-emptive PCR-guided for standard-risk; treatment: valganciclovir/ganciclovir; resistance via UL97/UL54 mutations. PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk ×6–12 months (lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression). Fungal prophylaxis only in high-risk (lung, liver, heart, re-transplant, prolonged neutropenia, steroids). Biologics: Anti-TNF — screen TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV BEFORE; risk: TB reactivation, histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, HBV reactivation; Anti-CD20 (rituximab) — screen HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; risk: HBV reactivation, PML (JCV), hypogammaglobulinaemia, encapsulated bacteria; JAK inhibitors — VZV/reactivation ↑↑; Shingrix BEFORE; screen TB; monitor lymphocytes; Alemtuzumab — severe T-cell depletion; intensive prophylaxis (PCP, viral, fungal). Key OIs: EBV-PTLD — reduce immunosuppression = cornerstone; rituximab; BK virus nephropathy — reduce Tac/MMF; leflunomide/cidofovir/IVIG adjunct. Vaccination: inactivated OK (give ≥2 weeks before immunosuppression); live CONTRAINDICATED during immunosuppression; exception: Shingrix (recombinant) SAFE. HIV CD4>200: MMR/Varicella may be given.

MCQs (10)

  1. Highest risk period for opportunistic infections post-solid organ transplant:

A. 0–1 month

B. 1–6 months

C. 6–12 months

D. >12 months

E. All periods equal

  1. Highest risk CMV donor/recipient serostatus combination:

A. D+/R+

B. D+/R−

C. D−/R+

D. D−/R−

E. All equal

  1. PCP prophylaxis duration post-kidney transplant:

A. 1 month

B. 3 months

C. 6–12 months (lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression)

D. 2 weeks

E. Not needed

  1. Anti-TNF therapy — mandatory screening BEFORE initiation:

A. HIV only

B. TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs)

C. Hepatitis C only

D. VZV IgG only

E. No screening needed

  1. Rituximab — mandatory screening BEFORE initiation:

A. TB only

B. HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc) — reactivation risk

C. HIV only

D. HCV only

E. CMV IgG only

  1. JAK inhibitors — most significantly increased infection risk:

A. TB only

B. Herpes zoster (VZV) reactivation ↑↑

C. Invasive aspergillosis

D. PCP

E. CMV

  1. Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine) in immunocompromised:

A. Contraindicated (live vaccine)

B. Safe and recommended (non-live recombinant)

C. Only if CD4 >200

D. Only if off immunosuppression

E. Give simultaneously with immunosuppression start

  1. BK virus nephropathy — cornerstone of management:

A. Cidofovir IV

B. Leflunomide

C. Reduce immunosuppression (reduce/stop Tacrolimus/MMF)

D. IVIG alone

E. Ciprofloxacin

  1. Anti-TNF therapy — mandatory pre-treatment screening:

A. HBV only

B. TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV

C. HCV only

D. VZV IgG

E. No screening needed

  1. Live vaccines in immunocompromised host:

A. All safe

B. Contraindicated (except Shingrix recombinant)

C. Only MMR safe

D. Only varicella safe

E. All safe if CD4 >200

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 45-year-old man, 3 months post-kidney transplant (D+/R− for CMV), on Tac/MMF/Pred, presents with fever 39°C, leukopenia. CMV PCR: 50,000 IU/mL. Management?

A. Valganciclovir 900mg OD (prophylaxis dose)

B. Valganciclovir 900mg BD (treatment dose) + reduce immunosuppression

C. Ganciclovir IV only

C. Observe, repeat PCR in 1 week

E. Reduce Tacrolimus only

  1. A 50-year-old woman, 6 months post-lung transplant, on Tac/MMF/Pred, presents with fever, cough, hypoxia. CT: multiple nodules with halo sign. BAL galactomannan +ve. Empirical treatment?

A. Fluconazole

B. Voriconazole 6mg/kg BD IV ×2d → 4mg/kg BD (with TDM)

C. Caspofungin

D. Liposomal Amphotericin B

E. Itraconazole

  1. A 35-year-old man with Crohn's disease on infliximab (anti-TNF) for 2 years presents with fever, weight loss, cough. IGRA positive (was negative pre-treatment). Chest X-ray: upper lobe cavitation. Next step?

A. Continue infliximab, start anti-TB

B. Stop infliximab, start standard anti-TB regimen (2HRZE/4HR)

C. Switch to adalimumab, start anti-TB

D. Start isoniazid prophylaxis only

E. Bronchoscopy only, no anti-TB

  1. A 40-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on rituximab (last dose 4 months ago) presents with fever, jaundice. HBsAg positive, HBV DNA 10⁷ IU/mL, anti-HBc positive. She was HBsAg negative pre-rituximab. Management?

A. Stop rituximab only

B. Entecavir/TDF + hepatology referral; hold rituximab; monitor HBV DNA

C. Lamivudine

D. Interferon-alpha

E. No antiviral (self-limiting)

  1. A 50-year-old man with ulcerative colitis on tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor) for 6 months develops painful vesicular rash in T5 dermatome. Management?

A. Continue tofacitinib, start acyclovir

B. Hold tofacitinib; start valacyclovir 1g 8h ×7d; consider Shingrix after recovery

C. Stop tofacitinib permanently

D. Acyclovir IV

E. VZIG + acyclovir

  1. A 60-year-old man, 3 months post-kidney transplant, on Tac/MMF/Pred, presents with rising creatinine, decoy cells in urine. Urine BK PCR: 10⁷ copies/mL. Management?

A. Increase Tacrolimus

B. Reduce Tacrolimus/MMF; consider leflunomide/cidofovir/IVIG adjunct

C. Cidofovir IV alone

D. Switch to cyclosporine

E. Biopsy only, no treatment change

  1. A 30-year-old woman with SLE on mycophenolate and prednisolone 20mg daily. Pre-travel advice for yellow fever endemic area. YF vaccine?

A. Give YF vaccine (single dose)

B. Contraindicated (live vaccine); provide waiver letter; bite avoidance

C. Give YF vaccine if off mycophenolate

D. Give fractional dose YF

E. Give YF + IVIG

  1. A 50-year-old man, 2 years post-heart transplant, presents with fever, confusion. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, EBV PCR positive. Diagnosis?

A. CMV encephalitis

B. CNS PTLD (EBV-driven)

C. HSV encephalitis

D. Cryptococcal meningitis

E. Listeria meningitis

  1. A 25-year-old man with MS on natalizumab (anti-integrin) for 3 years. Routine monitoring:

A. Annual MRI only

B. Anti-JCV antibody index every 6 months (stratify PML risk)

C. Monthly EBV PCR

D. Quarterly CMV PCR

E. No specific monitoring

  1. A 40-year-old woman, 6 months post-kidney transplant (D+/R− CMV), on valganciclovir prophylaxis. Day 2: fever, neutropenia. ANC 0.4. Next step?

A. Stop valganciclovir

B. Reduce valganciclovir dose (450mg OD) + G-CSF; monitor ANC

C. Switch to foscarnet

D. Switch to cidofovir

E. Stop all immunosuppression

  1. A 60-year-old man scheduled for rituximab for ANCA vasculitis. Pre-treatment screening:

A. TB only

B. HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs (HBV screen)

C. HCV only

D. HIV only

E. No screening needed

Flashcards

  • Q: Transplant timeline

A: 0-1m surgical/hospital; 1-6m opportunistic; >6m community

  • Q: CMV highest risk

A: D+/R-

  • Q: PCP prophylaxis

A: TMP-SMX 960mg 3x/wk x6-12m (lifelong if ongoing IS)

  • Q: CMV prophylaxis

A: Valganciclovir 900mg OD (high-risk D+/R-)

  • Q: CMV treatment

A: Valganciclovir 900mg BD or Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h

  • Q: Anti-TNF screen

A: TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV

  • Q: Rituximab screen

A: HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc)

  • Q: JAK inhibitor

A: VZV↑↑; Shingrix BEFORE; TB screen

  • Q: Shingrix

A: Safe in immunocompromised (recombinant)

  • Q: Live vaccines

A: Contraindicated (except Shingrix)

  • Q: BK virus

A: Reduce IS (Tac/MMF)

  • Q: EBV-PTLD

A: Reduce IS + Rituximab

  • Q: Alemtuzumab

A: Severe T-cell depletion; intense prophylaxis

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — 1–6 months post-transplant is the peak period for opportunistic infections (CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV, BK).
  2. B — D+/R− (donor positive, recipient negative) has the highest risk of primary CMV infection.
  3. C — PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX for 6–12 months, continued lifelong if immunosuppression persists.
  4. B — Anti-TNF requires TB (IGRA/TST) and HBV screening before initiation.
  5. B — Rituximab carries HBV reactivation risk; HBsAg and anti-HBc must be checked before starting.
  6. B — JAK inhibitors markedly increase herpes zoster risk; Shingrix vaccination before starting is recommended.
  7. B — Shingrix is a non-live recombinant vaccine; safe and recommended for immunocompromised.
  8. C — Reducing immunosuppression (Tacrolimus/MMF) is the primary intervention for BK virus nephropathy.
  9. B — Anti-TNF requires both TB (IGRA/TST) and HBV screening before initiation.
  10. B — Live vaccines contraindicated; exception is Shingrix (recombinant, non-live).

SBAs

  1. B — CMV viraemia >1000 IU/mL in D+/R− recipient = pre-emptive treatment with valganciclovir 900mg BD (not prophylaxis dose).
  2. B — Lung transplant + halo sign = invasive aspergillosis; voriconazole 1st-line with TDM.
  3. B — Anti-TNF + TB reactivation = stop biologic, start standard anti-TB regimen (2HRZE/4HR).
  4. B — Rituximab + HBV reactivation (HBsAg+) = entecavir/TDF + hold rituximab; monitor HBV DNA.
  5. B — JAK inhibitor + shingles = hold JAK, treat with valacyclovir; Shingrix after recovery.
  6. B — BK virus nephropathy = reduce immunosuppression (Tac/MMF) is cornerstone; adjuncts: leflunomide, cidofovir, IVIG.
  7. B — YF vaccine = live; contraindicated in immunosuppressed; provide waiver + bite avoidance.
  8. B — CNS PTLD in transplant = EBV-driven; reduce immunosuppression + rituximab.
  9. B — Natalizumab PML risk monitoring = anti-JCV antibody index every 6 months.
  10. B — Valganciclovir-induced neutropenia = dose reduction + G-CSF; do not stop if CMV viraemia.

▸ 14.1.24 Infection In Pregnancy Neonates

modified: 2025-06-17

Infection in Pregnancy & Neonates

[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Infection in Special Hosts → Pregnancy & Neonates
FCPS/MRCP Focus: TORCH infections, syphilis in pregnancy, HIV/hepatitis vertical transmission, GBS prevention, neonatal sepsis, congenital infections, breastfeeding with infections

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Apply TORCH Screen: Toxoplasmosis, Other (Syphilis, Varicella, Parvovirus B19, Zika), Rubella, CMV, Herpes Simplex
  • [ ] Manage Syphilis in Pregnancy: Penicillin, Partner Treatment, Neonatal Evaluation
  • [ ] Manage HIV in Pregnancy: ART, Viral Load, Delivery Mode, Infant Prophylaxis, Breastfeeding
  • [ ] Manage Hepatitis B/C in Pregnancy: Vertical Transmission Prevention, HBIG + Vaccine, Tenofovir, Breastfeeding
  • [ ] Prevent GBS Neonatal Sepsis: Screening 36-37w, IAP Penicillin, Penicillin Allergy
  • [ ] Manage Neonatal Sepsis: Early vs Late Onset, Empirical Antibiotics, Duration
  • [ ] Manage Congenital Infections: CMV, HSV, Rubella, Syphilis, Toxoplasmosis, Zika, Parvovirus B19
  • [ ] Advise Breastfeeding with Maternal Infections (HIV, HBV, HCV, CMV, HSV, TB)

📖 TORCH Infections — Congenital Infections Overview

PathogenTransmissionClassic Triad / Key FeaturesDiagnosisTreatment
ToxoplasmaTransplacentalChorioretinitis, Hydrocephalus, Intracranial CalcificationsIgM/IgG + Avidity, PCR (Amniotic Fluid)Spiramycin (<18w) → Pyrimethamine/Sulfadiazine/Folinic Acid (>18w)
Syphilis (Treponema pallidum)Transplacental (Any Trimester)Hepatosplenomegaly, Rash, Snuffles, Bone Lesions, PseudoparalysisVDRL/RPR (Non-treponemal), TPPA/FTA-ABS (Treponemal)Penicillin G (Penicillin Allergy → Desensitisation)
RubellaTransplacentalCataracts, Deafness, PDA, IUGR, "Blueberry Muffin" RashIgM/IgG, PCRSupportive; Prevention = MMR Vaccine Pre-Pregnancy
CMVMost Common Congenital InfectionSensorineural Hearing Loss, Microcephaly, Chorioretinitis, IUGR, Petechiae, HepatosplenomegalyUrine/Saliva PCR (Gold Standard), IgM, CultureValganciclovir 6 Months (Symptomatic)
HSV (Neonatal)Perinatal (85%), Transplacental (5%), Postnatal (10%)SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth), CNS, DisseminatedPCR (CSF/Blood/Surface), CultureAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 14-21d (CNS: 21d)
ZikaTransplacentalMicrocephaly, Brain Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, OcularRT-PCR (Urine/Serum/Amniotic Fluid), IgMSupportive; Prevention = Mosquito Avoidance
SyphilisTransplacentalClassic Triad: Hutchinson's Teeth, Interstitial Keratitis, 8th Nerve DeafnessVDRL/RPR + TPPA, CSF VDRLPenicillin G (Aqueous Crystalline IV)
Rubella1st Trimester Highest RiskCataracts, Deafness, PDA, "Blueberry Muffin" RashIgM, PCRSupportive
Parvovirus B19TransplacentalHydrops Fetalis (Severe Anaemia), MyocarditisIgM, PCR (Maternal/Fetal Blood)IVIG, Intrauterine Transfusion
VZVVaricella (Chickenpox)Congenital Varicella Syndrome (Limb Hypoplasia, Skin Scarring, Eye/CNS)IgM/IgG, PCRAcyclovir (Severe/Neonate)
ZikaMosquito (Aedes), Sexual, TransplacentalMicrocephaly, Brain Calcifications, ArthrogryposisRT-PCR (Urine/Serum/Amniotic Fluid)Supportive; Prevention
[!tip] TORCH = Toxoplasmosis, Other (Syphilis, Varicella, Parvovirus, Zika), Rubella, CMV, Herpes Simplex. CMV = Most Common Congenital Infection.

📖 Maternal Infections in Pregnancy — Key Management

Syphilis in Pregnancy

StageTreatment
Primary, Secondary, Early Latent (<1 Year)Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Single Dose
Late Latent (>1 Year) / TertiaryBenzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 Doses
Penicillin AllergyDesensitisation → Penicillin (Doxycycline/Azithromycin NOT Effective for Congenital Prevention)
Neonatal AssessmentPhysical Exam, VDRL/RPR, CSF (If Mother Untreated/Inadequate Rx/Late Rx/Clinical Signs), Long Bone X-Ray
[!warning] Penicillin = Only Reliable Prevention of Congenital Syphilis. Doxycycline/Azithromycin Do NOT Prevent Congenital Syphilis. Penicillin Allergy → Desensitisation.

HIV in Pregnancy

AspectManagement
ART GoalViral Suppression (VL <50 copies/mL) by Delivery
ART RegimenDolutegravir + TDF/FTC (Preferred); Efavirenz (Avoid 1st Trimester Historically, Now OK)
Viral Load MonitoringBooking, 36wks, Delivery
DeliveryViral Load <50 → Vaginal OK; Viral Load 50-399 → Elective C-Section 38wks; Viral Load >400 → Emergency C-Section
Neonatal ProphylaxisZidovudine 4mg/kg BD × 4 Weeks (If Maternal VL <50) / Zidovudine + Nevirapine + Lamivudine (High Risk)
BreastfeedingContraindicated in High-Income Settings (Formula Feed); Low-Income: Exclusive BF + Maternal ART + Infant Prophylaxis

Hepatitis B in Pregnancy

ScenarioManagement
HBsAg+ MotherHBIG 100IU IM + HBV Vaccine 10µg IM to Infant Within 12h of Birth
High Infectivity (HBeAg+, VL >200,000 IU/mL)Tenofovir 300mg OD From 28-32 Weeks → Stop at Delivery/Postpartum
BreastfeedingSafe (If Infant Received HBIG + Vaccine)
Infant ScheduleHBIG + Vaccine at Birth → Vaccine at 1, 6 Months

Hepatitis C in Pregnancy

AspectManagement
Vertical Transmission~5-6% (Higher if HIV Co-infection, High VL)
TreatmentDAA Deferred Until Postpartum (Safety Data Limited in Pregnancy)
BreastfeedingSafe (If Nipples Not Cracked/Bleeding)
Infant TestingHCV RNA at 2-6 Months or Anti-HCV at 18 Months

GBS (Group B Streptococcus) Prevention

StrategyDetails
ScreeningRectovaginal Swab at 36-37 Weeks (Culture or PCR)
IAP IndicationGBS Positive, Previous GBS Neonate, Bacteriuria in Pregnancy, Unknown Status + Preterm Labour/Rupture/≥18h Rupture/Fever
IAP RegimenPenicillin G 5MU IV Load → 2.5-3MU Q4H (Until Delivery)
Penicillin AllergyCefazolin 2g IV Load → 1g Q8H (If No Anaphylaxis); Clindamycin/Vancomycin (If High Risk Anaphylaxis)
DurationUntil Delivery (≥4h Pre-Delivery Optimal)

👶 Neonatal Sepsis

Classification

TypeTimingCommon Pathogens
Early-Onset (EOS)<72h (≤3d) of LifeGBS, E. coli, Listeria, Klebsiella, Enterococcus
Late-Onset (LOS)>72h (>3d) of LifeCoNS, S. aureus, Enterobacterales, Candida, Enterococcus

Clinical Features

FeatureEOSLOS
Onset<72h>72h
Risk FactorsGBS+, PROM >18h, Maternal Fever, PretermPreterm, Invasive Devices, Prolonged Hospitalisation, Antibiotics
PresentationRespiratory Distress, Apnoea, Temperature Instability, Lethargy, Poor FeedingSimilar + Abdominal Distension, Feed Intolerance

Empirical Therapy (Neonatal Sepsis)

ScenarioEmpirical RegimenDuration
EOS (Term)Ampicillin 50mg/kg Q12H + Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg OD48-72h (If Cultures Negative, Stop) / 7-10 Days (If Positive)
EOS (Preterm <37w)Ampicillin 50mg/kg Q12H + Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg ODAs Above
LOSVancomycin + Meropenem/Ceftazidime (Local Resistance)7-14 Days (If Positive)
Suspected MeningitisAdd Cefotaxime (or Ceftriaxone if >3d)14-21 Days
[!warning] Ampicillin + Gentamicin = Standard EOS Regimen. Ceftriaxone in Neonates >3d (Risk Biliary Sludge). Ceftriaxone Contraindicated <28d Corrected Gestation (Kernicterus Risk).

📖 Congenital Infections — Specific Management

Congenital CMV (cCMV)

AspectDetails
EpidemiologyMost Common Congenital Infection (~0.5-1% Live Births); Symptomatic ~10-15%
TransmissionPrimary Maternal Infection (30-40% Transmission); Reactivation/Reinfection (<2%)
ClinicalAsymptomatic (85-90%) vs Symptomatic (10-15%): Petechiae, Hepatosplenomegaly, Jaundice, Microcephaly, Sensorineural Hearing Loss (Most Common Sequela), Chorioretinitis, Neurodevelopmental Delay
DiagnosisUrine/Saliva PCR (Gold Standard, <21 Days), CMV IgM, Culture
TreatmentSymptomatic: Valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD PO × 6 Months (Improves Hearing/Neurodevelopment)
Follow-UpAudiology (Every 6mo × 3yr), Developmental Assessment, Ophthalmology

Neonatal HSV

TypeFeaturesTreatment
SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth)Vesicles (45%), Conjunctivitis, Oral LesionsAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 14 Days
CNSSeizures, Lethargy, CSF PleocytosisAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 21 Days
DisseminatedMulti-Organ (Liver, Lung, CNS, DIC)Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H × 21 Days
[!warning] Neonatal HSV = Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H; CNS/Disseminated = 21 Days, SEM = 14 Days. CSF PCR = Gold Standard.

Congenital Syphilis

ScenarioManagement
Maternal Treatment AdequateBaby: Benzathine Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IM Single Dose (If Asymptomatic, Normal Exam, Normal CSF/Long Bones)
Maternal Treatment Inadequate/UnknownBaby: Aqueous Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IV Q12H × 10-14 Days
CSF Abnormal / Clinical SignsAqueous Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IV Q12H × 10-14 Days

🤱 Breastfeeding with Maternal Infections

InfectionBreastfeeding Recommendation
HIVContraindicated (High-Income); Low-Income: Exclusive BF + Maternal ART + Infant Prophylaxis
HBVSafe (After HBIG + Vaccine to Infant)
HCVSafe (If Nipples Intact)
CMVSafe (If Term Infant); Preterm <32w/1500g: Pasteurised Donor Milk / Freeze-Thaw
HSVSafe (If No Breast Lesions); Avoid if Active Lesion on Breast
TBSafe After 2 Weeks Effective Therapy; Separate Mother/Baby Initially
MalariaSafe (If Mother Treated)
ZikaSafe (No Live Virus in Breast Milk)

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
TORCHToxoplasmosis, Other (Syphilis, Varicella, Parvovirus, Zika), Rubella, CMV, Herpes Simplex
Congenital CMVMost Common Congenital Infection; Urinary PCR Gold Standard; Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Congenital SyphilisPenicillin G Only Effective Prevention; Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM (Maternal); Aqueous Penicillin G IV (Neonate)
HIV in PregnancyART Goal: VL<50 by Delivery; Zidovudine Neonatal Prophylaxis 4wks; C-Section if VL>400
Hepatitis BHBIG + Vaccine Within 12h Birth; Tenofovir 28-32wks if VL>200,000
Hepatitis C~5-6% Vertical Transmission; DAA Postpartum; Breastfeeding Safe
GBS PreventionScreen 36-37w; IAP: Penicillin G 5MU Load → 2.5-3MU Q4H
Neonatal SepsisEOS <72h: Amp-Gent; LOS: Vanco + Mero/Ceftazidime; Meningitis: Add Cefotaxime
Congenital CMVUrine PCR Gold Standard; Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Neonatal HSVAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H; CNS/Disseminated 21d, SEM 14d
BreastfeedingHIV: Contraindicated (High-Income); HBV/HCV/CMV: Safe (With Conditions)

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the most common congenital infection?
  • Cytomegalovirus (CMV).
  1. What is the treatment for congenital syphilis in the neonate?
  • Aqueous Penicillin G 50,000U/kg IV Q12H × 10-14 Days (If Maternal Treatment Inadequate/CSF Abnormal).
  1. How do you prevent mother-to-child transmission of HIV?
  • ART to Achieve VL<50 by Delivery, Elective C-Section if VL>400, Neonatal Zidovudine 4mg/kg BD × 4 Weeks, Avoid Breastfeeding (High-Income).
  1. How do you prevent GBS neonatal sepsis?
  • Screen at 36-37 Weeks (Rectovaginal Swab) → Penicillin G 5MU IV Load → 2.5-3MU Q4H Until Delivery if Positive.
  1. What is the management of a neonate born to HBsAg+ mother?
  • HBIG 100IU IM + HBV Vaccine 10µg IM Within 12 Hours of Birth; Complete Vaccine Series.
  1. What is the treatment for neonatal HSV infection?
  • Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H; 14 Days (SEM), 21 Days (CNS/Disseminated).
  1. What is the risk of vertical transmission of Hepatitis C?
  • ~5-6% (Higher if HIV Co-infection or High Viral Load).
  1. How do you diagnose congenital CMV?
  • Urine or Saliva PCR (Gold Standard) Within 21 Days of Life; Culture (Gold Standard); IgM Less Reliable.
  1. What is the management of congenital toxoplasmosis?
  • Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Folinic Acid × 12 Months.
  1. When do you give HBIG + Hepatitis B vaccine to a neonate?
  • Within 12 Hours of Birth (HBsAg+ Mother).
  1. What is the empirical antibiotic regimen for early-onset neonatal sepsis?
  • Ampicillin 50mg/kg Q12H + Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg OD (Term) — Adjust for Preterm.
  1. How do you prevent mother-to-child transmission of Hepatitis B?
  • HBIG + Vaccine at Birth → Vaccine at 1 & 6 Months; Tenofovir 300mg OD from 28-32 Weeks if Viral Load >200,000 IU/mL.
  1. What is the treatment for congenital toxoplasmosis?
  • Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Folinic Acid × 12 Months.

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TORCH ComponentsT=Toxo, O=Other (Syph, VZV, Parvo, Zika), R=Rubella, C=CMV, H=HSV
CMV vs RubellaCMV = Most Common, Hearing Loss; Rubella = Cataracts, Deafness, PDA
Congenital Syphilis RxPenicillin ONLY; Doxy/Azithro NOT Effective for Congenital Prevention
HIV BreastfeedingHigh-Income: Formula Only; Low-Income: Exclusive BF + ART + Infant Prophylaxis
CMV vs RubellaCMV: Hearing Loss, Microcephaly, Petechiae; Rubella: Cataracts, Deafness, PDA
CMV vs HSV NeonatalCMV: Hearing Loss, Microcephaly; HSV: Vesicles, CNS/Disseminated, Acyclovir
MnemonicMeaning
"TORCH = Toxo, Other, Rubella, CMV, Herpes"Congenital Infections
"CMV = Most Common Congenital Infection"CMV Prevalence
"Syphilis = Penicillin Only"Syphilis Treatment
"HIV = ART + Zidovudine Baby + C-Section if VL>400"HIV Pregnancy
"GBS = Screen 36-37w, Penicillin G IAP"GBS Prevention
"CMV = Urine PCR Gold Standard"CMV Diagnosis
"HSV Neonate = Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H"Neonatal HSV
"CMV = Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic"cCMV Treatment

🗺️ Mind Map

Infection in Pregnancy & Neonates
TORCH
Toxoplasma: Spiramycin <18w, Pyrimethamine/Sulfa >18w
Syphilis: Penicillin G Only, Congenital Triad
Rubella: Cataract, Deafness, PDA, MMR Prevention
CMV: Most Common, Urine PCR, Valganciclovir 6mo
HSV: SEM/CNS/Disseminated, Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV Q8H
Zika: Microcephaly, Prevention
Parvovirus B19: Hydrops Fetalis, IVIG
VZV: Congenital Varicella Syndrome
Maternal Infections
Syphilis: Penicillin Only, Benzathine 2.4MU IM
HIV: ART + VL<50, Zidovudine Baby 4wks, C-Section if VL>400
HBV: HBIG + Vaccine 12h, Tenofovir 28-32w if VL>200k
HCV: 5-6% Transmission, DAA Postpartum, BF Safe
GBS: Screen 36-37w, Penicillin G IAP
Neonatal Sepsis
EOS <72h: Amp + Gent
LOS >72h: Vanco + Mero/Ceftaz
Meningitis: Add Cefotaxime
Congenital Infections
CMV: Urine PCR, Valganciclovir 6mo
HSV: Acyclovir 20mg/kg Q8H (14d SEM, 21d CNS)
Syphilis: Aqueous Penicillin G IV
Zika: Microcephaly, Supportive
Parvovirus: Hydrops, IVIG + IUT
Breastfeeding
HIV: No (High-Income), Yes+ART (Low-Income)
HBV/HCV/CMV/HSV: Safe (Conditions)

📋 One-Page Revision Card

INFECTION IN PREGNANCY & NEONATES – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
TORCH: Toxo, Other (Syph/VZV/Parvo/Zika), Rubella, CMV, HSV
CMV: Most Common Congenital, Urine PCR Gold, Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Syphilis: Penicillin ONLY, Benzathine 2.4MU IM Maternal, Neonatal Aqueous Penicillin G IV
HIV: ART→VL<50, Zidovudine Baby 4wk, C-Section if VL>400, No BF (High-Income)
HBV: HBIG+Vaccine 12h, Tenofovir 28-32w if VL>200k
HCV: 5-6% Transmission, DAA Postpartum, BF Safe
GBS: Screen 36-37w, Penicillin G 5MU Load→2.5-3MU Q4H
Neonatal Sepsis: EOS<72h: Amp+Gent; LOS>72h: Vanco+Mero/Ceftaz; Meningitis: Add Cefotaxime
Congenital CMV: Urine PCR<21d, Valganciclovir 6mo if Symptomatic
Neonatal HSV: Acyclovir 20mg/kg Q8H (14d SEM, 21d CNS/Dissem)
Congenital Syphilis: Penicillin G Aqueous IV 50,000U/kg Q12H×10-14d
Breastfeeding: HIV No (High-Income), Yes+ART (Low-Income); HBV/HCV/CMV/HSV Safe

🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowTORCH components, CMV most common congenital infection, Syphilis penicillin only, HIV vertical transmission prevention (ART, C-section, zidovudine), GBS screening & IAP, HBV vertical transmission prevention (HBIG+vaccine), Neonatal sepsis EOS vs LOS antibiotics, Congenital CMV diagnosis (urine PCR) & treatment (valganciclovir), Neonatal HSV acyclovir dosing, Breastfeeding guidelines by infection
Should KnowCongenital rubella syndrome (cataracts, deafness, PDA), Toxoplasmosis management in pregnancy (spiramycin vs pyrimethamine), Parvovirus B19 hydrops fetalis management, Zika congenital syndrome, Neonatal HSV types (SEM/CNS/disseminated) & treatment, Congenital syphilis staging & treatment, Congenital CMV long-term sequelae (hearing loss), Hepatitis B tenofovir in pregnancy, Hepatitis C postpartum DAA, GBS penicillin allergy management, Neonatal sepsis meningitis antibiotic choice (cefotaxime), Breastfeeding with maternal infections
Nice to KnowCongenital infection long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes, Antiviral resistance in neonates, Emerging congenital infections (Zika, novel coronaviruses), Intrauterine transfusion for parvovirus, Neonatal immunization with maternal antibiotics, Probiotic prevention of NEC/sepsis, Cost-effectiveness of screening programs, Global burden of congenital infections, Ethical issues in prenatal diagnosis

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.25 Infective Diarrhoea And Food Poisoning

Related: [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection]], [[Traveller's Diarrhoea]], [[Inflammatory Bowel Disease]]

[!important]
Diarrhoea = ≥3 loose stools/24h. Acute <14d, Persistent 14–30d, Chronic >30d. Infective = majority acute. Assess hydration, red flags, need for stool culture/PCR, empirical therapy.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify diarrhoea by duration and mechanism
  • Identify red flags for invasive/bloody diarrhoea and systemic illness
  • Select appropriate investigations (stool culture, PCR, toxins, endoscopy)
  • Apply empirical and pathogen-directed therapy
  • Manage dehydration, electrolytes, and complications

2. Classification by Duration

CategoryDurationCommon Causes
Acute<14 daysViral (norovirus, rotavirus), bacterial (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, ETEC), protozoal (Giardia, Cryptosporidium)
Persistent14–30 daysGiardia, Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora, C. difficile, post-infectious IBS
Chronic>30 daysIBD, IBS, malabsorption, chronic infections (HIV, TB), medications, neoplasia

3. Classification by Mechanism

TypeMechanismExamples
SecretoryActive ion secretion (CFTR) → water followsCholera (CTX), ETEC (LT/ST), VIPoma, laxatives
OsmoticNon-absorbable solutes retain waterLactose intolerance, magnesium, sorbitol, malabsorption
Inflammatory/InvasiveMucosal damage → blood, pus, mucusShigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EIEC, C. difficile, IBD
Fatty (Steatorrhoea)Fat malabsorptionPancreatic insufficiency, coeliac, bile acid diarrhoea
Motility-relatedAltered transitHyperthyroidism, diabetic autonomic neuropathy, carcinoid

4. Clinical Assessment

History

ElementKey Questions
DurationAcute (<14d) vs persistent vs chronic
Frequency/VolumeLarge volume (secretory/osmotic) vs small frequent (inflammatory)
Blood/MucusYes = inflammatory/invasive (dysentery); No = secretory/osmotic
Severity≥6/day, nocturnal, weight loss
AssociatedFever, abdominal pain, vomiting, tenesmus
EpidemiologyTravel, antibiotics, hospitalisation, food source, contacts, immunocompromise

Red Flags (Admit / Urgent Investigation)

Red FlagSignificance
Bloody diarrhoeaInvasive (Shigella, Campylobacter, EHEC, Salmonella, EIEC, C. difficile, IBD)
High fever (>38.5°C)Systemic invasion (bacteraemia, sepsis)
Signs of dehydrationHypovolaemia, shock risk
Severe abdominal painSurgical abdomen, toxic megacolon, ischaemia
ImmunocompromiseHIV, chemo, steroids — broader differential, severe disease
Elderly / ComorbiditiesHigher mortality, atypical presentation
Recent antibiotics / hospitalisationC. difficile risk
Recent travelETEC, Giardia, Cyclospora, Cholera, parasitic
Neurological symptomsBotulism, listeria, ciguatera, shellfish poisoning

5. Investigations

When to Investigate (NICE / IDSA)

IndicationTests
Bloody/mucoid diarrhoeaStool culture + PCR, C. difficile toxin
Fever ≥38.5°CBlood cultures, stool culture/PCR
Severe / dehydratedU&E, CRP, lactate, VBG/ABG, FBC
ImmunocompromisedExtended PCR panel, O&P, viral PCR, fungal
Persistent >7dStool culture/PCR, O&P, Giardia/Crypto antigen
Recent antibiotics / hospitalC. difficile toxin/PCR
Recent travelO&P, Cyclospora, microsporidia, viral PCR
Foodborne outbreakCulture, WGS, public health notification

Stool Culture vs PCR

TestAdvantagesLimitations
Culture + ASTEssential for resistance, susceptibility, outbreakSlow (2–5d); lower sensitivity
Multiplex PCR panelRapid (1–2h), high sensitivity, multiple targetsNo AST; detects DNA not viability; cost
Ova & Parasites (O&P)Required for Giardia, Crypto, Cyclospora, helminthsLow sensitivity (3 samples); operator-dependent
C. difficile toxin/PCRGold standard for CDIPCR more sensitive; toxin more specific

Pathogen-Specific Tests

PathogenBest Test
C. difficilePCR (toxin B gene) +/− toxin EIA
Giardia / CryptoStool antigen (EIA) or PCR
Cyclospora / MicrosporidiaModified acid-fast stain / PCR
Vibrio choleraeCulture (TCBS) + PCR
EHEC (O157:H7)Culture (sorbitol-MacConkey) + PCR (stx1/stx2)
VDRL / RPRNot for diarrhoea! (syphilis)

6. Empirical Management Principles

Rehydration (Cornerstone)

SeverityORS / IV Fluid
No dehydrationORS ad lib (50–100mL/kg/24h); continue feeding
Some dehydrationORS 75mL/kg over 4h; reassess
Severe dehydrationIV Ringer's Lactate / Normal Saline 100mL/kg over 3h (adults: 1L bolus, reassess)
ORS = WHO reduced osmolarity (245 mOsm/L); Zinc 20mg/day × 10–14d in children <5y

Antimotility Agents

AgentUseContraindications
LoperamideAcute watery (no blood/fever); adjunct to antibioticsBloody diarrhoea, fever, suspected C. difficile, children <12y
RacecadotrilSecretory diarrhoea (adjunct)Similar to loperamide

Antibiotic Therapy (Indicated for Specific Pathogens / Severity)

Pathogen / Scenario1st-lineDuration
ShigellaCiprofloxacin 500mg BD / Azithromycin 500mg OD3–5d
CampylobacterAzithromycin 500mg OD3d
Salmonella (non-typhoidal, invasive)Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD / Ceftriaxone 2g IV7–10d (IV→oral)
Salmonella Typhi/ParatyphiCeftriaxone 2g IV / Azithromycin 1g OD7–14d
ETEC (traveller's diarrhoea)Azithromycin 500mg OD × 1–3d / Rifaximin 200mg TDS × 3d1–3d
Vibrio choleraeDoxycycline 100mg BD × 3d / Azithromycin 1g single3d / 1d
C. difficile (mild-mod)Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO / Fidaxomicin 200mg BD10d
C. difficile (severe)Vancomycin 500mg QDS PO/IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV QDS10–14d
GiardiaMetronidazole 400mg TDS / Tinidazole 2g single5–7d / 1d
CryptosporidiumNitazoxanide 500mg BD (if immunocompetent)3d
EHEC O157:H7No antibiotics (↑ HUS risk)Supportive only
Food poisoning (S. aureus, B. cereus)Supportive (toxin-mediated)—
Avoid antibiotics in: EHEC (↑ HUS), mild viral, self-limiting

7. Food Poisoning (Toxin-Mediated)

SyndromeOrganism / ToxinIncubationKey Features
StaphylococcalS. aureus enterotoxin1–6hVomiting prominent, abdominal cramps, diarrhoea; self-limiting 24–48h
Bacillus cereus (emetic)Cereulide1–6hVomiting > diarrhoea; fried rice
Bacillus cereus (diarrhoeal)Enterotoxin8–16hDiarrhoea > vomiting; meat, vegetables
Clostridium perfringensEnterotoxin8–24hDiarrhoea, cramps; no fever/vomiting; meat/gravy
BotulismC. botulinum neurotoxin12–72hDescending paralysis, diplopia, dysphagia, respiratory failure; ANTITOXIN urgent
CiguateraCiguatoxin2–24hGI + neurological (paradoxical temp reversal, paraesthesia)
ScombroidHistamine10–60minFlushing, headache, urticaria, palpitations; antihistamines

8. Complications

ComplicationHigh-Risk Pathogens
HUS (Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome)EHEC O157:H7 (Shiga toxin); Shigella dysenteriae
TTPSimilar to HUS; ADAMTS13 deficiency
Reactive ArthritisSalmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, Chlamydia (HLA-B27)
Guillain-Barré SyndromeCampylobacter jejuni (molecular mimicry)
SepticaemiaSalmonella, Campylobacter, invasive E. coli
Toxic MegacolonC. difficile, Shigella, IBD
Post-infectious IBSCampylobacter, Salmonella, Giardia

9. Traveller's Diarrhoea

AspectRecommendation
Definition≥3 loose stools/24h + cramps/nausea/fever during/after travel
AetiologyETEC (~50%), Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, norovirus, Giardia
ProphylaxisNot routine; Rifaximin 200mg BD (high-risk short-term)
Self-treatmentAzithromycin 500mg OD × 1–3d + Loperamide + ORS
Standby antibioticsAzithromycin 500mg OD × 3d (SE Asia); Rifaximin 200mg TDS (low-risk)

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Bloody diarrhoea = invasive → culture/PCR + empirical antibiotics
  • EHEC O157:H7 = NO antibiotics (↑ HUS risk)
  • C. difficile = recent antibiotics/hospital → PCR + vancomycin/fidaxomicin
  • Traveller's diarrhoea = ETEC most common; azithromycin standby
  • Cholera = rice-water stool, massive secretory → doxycycline/azithro + massive ORS
  • Botulism = descending paralysis → antitoxin + ventilatory support
  • Reactive arthritis = post-Salmonella/Shigella/Campylobacter (HLA-B27)
  • HUS = EHEC O157:H7 (no antibiotics!) + Shigella
  • Food poisoning = short incubation (S. aureus 1–6h) = vomiting predominant

11. Common Viva Questions

  1. When do you give antibiotics for diarrhoea?
  2. Why no antibiotics in EHEC O157:H7?
  3. How do you manage C. difficile?
  4. What is the management of traveller's diarrhoea?
  5. When do you suspect HUS?

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Antibiotics in EHEC = ↑ HUS (NEVER give)
  • Antibiotics in mild viral = unnecessary, ↑ resistance
  • Loperamide in bloody diarrhoea = contraindicated
  • Antibiotics for all traveller's diarrhoea = only moderate-severe
  • C. difficile = stop causative antibiotic first; metronidazole no longer 1st-line
  • Cholera = rice-water stool, massive fluid loss → doxycycline + massive ORS
  • Botulism = antitoxin + ventilatory support (not antibiotics)
  • HUS triad = microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI

13. Mnemonics

  • Bloody diarrhoea pathogens: Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC, C. difficile, EIEC = SSCEE
  • No antibiotics: EHEC, Viral, Mild self-limiting = EVM
  • HUS triad: Microangiopathic Haemolytic Anaemia + Thrombocytopenia + AKI = MHA-T
  • Traveller's diarrhoea: ETEC > Campylobacter > Salmonella > Shigella = ECSS
  • Food poisoning incubation: S. aureus/B. cereus (emetic) = 1–6h = SB-6

14. One-Page Revision Summary

Diarrhoea = Acute (<14d) / Persistent (14–30d) / Chronic (>30d)

  • Bloody = invasive → culture/PCR + antibiotics
  • Watery = secretory/osmotic → rehydration ± antiemetics
  • EHEC O157:H7 = NO antibiotics (HUS risk)
  • C. difficile = recent antibiotics → vancomycin/fidaxomicin
  • Traveller's = ETEC most common; azithromycin standby
  • Cholera = rice-water, massive loss → doxy/azithro + massive ORS
  • Food poisoning = short incubation = vomiting (S. aureus/B. cereus)
  • Botulism = descending paralysis → antitoxin + ventilator
  • HUS = EHEC (NO antibiotics!)

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Bloody diarrhoea = invasive → culture + antibiotics
  • EHEC = NO antibiotics (HUS risk)
  • C. difficile = vancomycin/fidaxomicin (metronidazole 2nd-line)
  • Cholera = rice-water + doxycycline + massive ORS
  • Traveller's diarrhoea = azithromycin standby
  • Botulism = antitoxin + ventilator
  • HUS = EHEC (no antibiotics!)

Should Know

  • Food poisoning syndromes (S. aureus, B. cereus, C. perfringens)
  • Parasitic (Giardia, Crypto, Cyclospora)
  • Reactive arthritis (post-infectious)
  • Paediatric dehydration assessment
  • Probiotic evidence

Nice to Know

  • FMT for recurrent C. difficile
  • New PCR panels (syndromic testing)
  • Post-infectious IBS mechanisms
  • Vaccine development (ETEC, Shigella, cholera)
  • Microbiome therapeutics

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Invasive diarrhoea (bloody/mucoid) — most common bacterial causes:

A. ETEC, Vibrio

B. Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC

C. Norovirus, Rotavirus

D. Giardia, Cryptosporidium

  1. EHEC O157:H7 — antibiotic treatment:

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

B. No antibiotics (↑ HUS risk)

C. Azithromycin 500mg OD

D. Ceftriaxone 2g IV

  1. C. difficile infection — 1st-line treatment (mild-moderate):

A. Metronidazole 500mg TDS

B. Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO (or Fidaxomicin 200mg BD)

C. Ciprofloxacin

D. Doxycycline

  1. Cholera — characteristic stool:

A. Bloody, mucoid

B. Rice-water, massive volume

C. Fatty, foul-smelling

D. Small volume, frequent

  1. Traveller's diarrhoea — most common cause:

A. Norovirus

B. ETEC (enterotoxigenic E. coli)

C. Salmonella

D. Giardia

  1. Food poisoning with vomiting predominant, incubation 2h — likely:

A. Salmonella

B. S. aureus / B. cereus (emetic)

C. C. perfringens

D. EHEC

  1. Botulism — definitive treatment:

A. Penicillin

B. Antitoxin + ventilatory support

C. Clindamycin

C. IVIG

  1. HUS triad:

A. Haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytosis + AKI

B. Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI

C. Haemolytic anaemia + leukocytosis + AKI

D. Thrombocytopenia + leukocytosis + AKI

  1. C. difficile — when to use fidaxomicin:

A. Mild only

B. Recurrent CDI / high recurrence risk / cost not barrier

C. Severe only

D. First episode only

  1. Reactive arthritis — classic triad post-diarrhoea:

A. Arthritis + conjunctivitis + urethritis

B. Arthritis + conjunctivitis + rash

C. Arthritis + conjunctivitis + urethritis (HLA-B27 associated)

D. Arthritis + rash + fever

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 25-year-old returns from India, 3 days watery diarrhoea, cramps, low-grade fever. No blood. Best empirical treatment:

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d

B. Azithromycin 500mg OD × 1–3d (resistance to fluoroquinolones in SE Asia)

C. Loperamide only

D. Rifaximin 200mg TDS × 3d

  1. A 3-year-old with bloody diarrhoea, fever 39°C, abdominal pain. Stool culture: Shigella flexneri. Best treatment:

A. Supportive only

B. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d (or Azithromycin 20mg/kg OD × 3d)

C. Loperamide + ORS

D. IV fluids only

  1. A 65-year-old, 5 days post-clindamycin for dental abscess, profuse watery diarrhoea ×8/day, fever 38.5°C, abdominal tenderness. PCR: C. difficile toxin positive. Best initial treatment:

A. Metronidazole 500mg TDS

B. Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO × 10d (mild-moderate)

C. Fidaxomicin 200mg BD

D. Oral metronidazole + vancomycin

  1. A 30-year-old returns from Mexico, severe watery diarrhoea "rice-water", vomiting, profound dehydration. Suspected cholera. Best antibiotic:

A. Ciprofloxacin

B. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 3d (or Azithromycin 1g single)

C. Metronidazole

C. Ceftriaxone

  1. A 6-year-old with bloody diarrhoea, HUS (Hb 70, platelets 50, creatinine 150). Stool: EHEC O157:H7. Management:

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

B. Supportive only (NO antibiotics — ↑ HUS); renal replacement if needed

C. Ceftriaxone 2g IV

D. Azithromycin

  1. A 40-year-old post-cholecystectomy, 10 days clindamycin, watery diarrhoea, mild abdominal pain. C. difficile PCR positive, mild. Treatment:

A. Metronidazole 500mg TDS

B. Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO × 10d

C. Stop clindamycin only

D. Fidaxomicin 200mg BD

  1. A traveller to Thailand, 2 days watery diarrhoea, no blood/fever. Prescribed standby azithromycin. When to take?

A. Immediately

B. If >6 stools/24h OR blood OR fever OR severe cramps (moderate-severe)

C. Only if persists >48h

D. Only if fever

  1. A patient with Campylobacter jejuni infection develops ascending paralysis 2 weeks later. Diagnosis:

A. Stroke

B. Guillain-Barré Syndrome (molecular mimicry)

C. Transverse myelitis

D. Botulism

  1. A 25-year-old returns from Bangladesh, 2 weeks watery diarrhoea, weight loss. Stool O&P: Giardia cysts. Treatment:

A. Metronidazole 400mg TDS × 5–7d

B. Tinidazole 2g single dose

C. Both A and B acceptable

D. Albendazole

  1. Scombroid poisoning — management:

A. Antibiotics

B. Antihistamines (H1/H2 blockers)

C. Adrenaline

D. Steroids

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Bloody diarrhoea pathogens

A: Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC, C. difficile, EIEC

  • Q: EHEC antibiotics

A: NEVER (↑ HUS)

  • Q: C. difficile 1st-line

A: Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO (fidaxomicin alt)

  • Q: Cholera

A: Rice-water stool + doxycycline + massive ORS

  • Q: Traveller's diarrhoea

A: ETEC most common; azithromycin standby

  • Q: Food poisoning 1–6h

A: S. aureus / B. cereus (emetic) = vomiting

  • Q: Botulism

A: Descending paralysis → antitoxin + ventilator

  • Q: HUS

A: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI (EHEC most common)

  • Q: Reactive arthritis

A: Post-Salmonella/Shigella/Campylobacter/Yersinia (HLA-B27)

  • Q: C. difficile 1st-line

A: Vancomycin 125mg QDS PO (not metronidazole)

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC = invasive.

MCQ 2: B — EHEC = NO antibiotics (↑ HUS risk with antibiotics).

MCQ 3: B — Vancomycin/fidaxomicin 1st-line; metronidazole 2nd-line.

MCQ 4: B — Rice-water stool = massive secretory diarrhoea.

MCQ 5: B — ETEC ~50% of traveller's diarrhoea.

MCQ 6: B — S. aureus/B. cereus emetic = 1–6h, vomiting predominant.

MCQ 7: B — Antitoxin + ventilatory support (paralysis).

MCQ 8: B — MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI.

MCQ 9: B — Fidaxomicin for recurrent/high recurrence risk.

MCQ 10: C — Arthritis + conjunctivitis + urethritis (Reiter's triad).

SBA 1: B — Azithromycin preferred in SE Asia (fluoroquinolone resistance).

SBA 2: B — Shigella = ciprofloxacin/azithromycin (invasive = antibiotics).

SBA 3: B — Mild-mod CDI = vancomycin 125mg QDS (metronidazole 2nd-line).

SBA 4: B — Cholera = doxycycline 100mg BD × 3d or azithromycin 1g.

SBA 5: B — EHEC + HUS = NO antibiotics, supportive ± RRT.

SBA 6: B — CDI mild = vancomycin 125mg QDS (metronidazole no longer 1st-line).

SBA 7: B — Standby antibiotics for moderate-severe (>6 stools/blood/fever/cramps).

SBA 8: C — Post-Campylobacter = Guillain-Barré.

SBA 9: C — Both metronidazole and tinidazole effective for Giardia.

SBA 10: B — Scombroid = antihistamines (histamine-mediated).

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13
