A: Bilateral medial temporal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± insula/cingulate; haemorrhage on GRE; DWI restriction

  • Q: HSV EEG hallmark

A: PLEDs / temporal periodic complexes

  • Q: HSV PCR sensitivity

A: 95–98%; may be negative <72h — repeat at 48–72h

  • Q: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis

A: Young women; psychiatric → seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, catatonia; ovarian teratoma; IVIG + steroids + rituximab

  • Q: LGI1 encephalitis

A: Older men; FBDS (faciobrachial dystonic seizures), hyponatraemia, limbic encephalitis; IVIG + steroids

  • Q: CASPR2 encephalitis

A: Morvan syndrome (neuromyotonia, insomnia, autonomic) + limbic encephalitis

  • Q: ADEM

A: Post-infectious/vaccinal (1–4w); children; multifocal demyelination; encephalopathy + polyfocal deficits; steroids + IVIG/PLEX

  • Q: Viral meningitis CSF

A: Lymphocytic, normal glucose, normal/mild ↑ protein; enterovirus #1

  • Q: Enterovirus meningitis

A: Most common viral meningitis; summer/autumn; supportive; full recovery

  • Q: West Nile virus

A: Acute flaccid paralysis; CSF IgM; supportive; IVIG sometimes

  • Q: Japanese encephalitis

A: Rural Asia; Culex mosquito; pig/water bird reservoirs; vaccine available

  • Q: Rabies

A: Fatal once symptomatic; hydrophobia, agitation; PEP = vaccine + HRIG

  • Q: Aciclovir for VZV encephalitis

A: 10–15mg/kg IV 8h ×10–14d (higher dose than HSV)

  • Q: Aciclovir neonatal HSV

A: 20mg/kg IV 8h ×21d

  • Q: HSV PCR negative early

A: Repeat at 48–72h; continue aciclovir if high suspicion

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Start aciclovir IMMEDIATELY (within 1h). Delay increases mortality. All other investigations follow.
  2. B — Bilateral medial temporal lobes ± insula ± cingulate is the classic HSV encephalitis MRI signature. Haemorrhage and DWI restriction common.
  3. B — IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h for encephalitis. 5mg/kg 8h = VZV (some guidelines); 20mg/kg 8h = neonatal HSV; PO = non-CNS herpes.
  4. B — Viral meningitis: lymphocytic pleocytosis (10–500), normal glucose, normal/mildly elevated protein. A = bacterial. C = TB/fungal. D/E = not typical.
  5. C — Anti-NMDAR encephalitis: young women, psychiatric prodrome, ovarian teratoma (50%).
  6. B — FBDS + hyponatraemia = LGI1 encephalitis (classic). Anti-NMDAR = dyskinesias, psychiatric. CASPR2 = Morvan.
  7. B — Rural India + encephalitis + flaccid paralysis = Japanese encephalitis (Culex, pigs/water birds). West Nile = US/Europe/Mediterranean. Dengue/Zika/chikungunya = less AFP.
  8. C — CrCl <10 (non-dialysis): aciclovir 10mg/kg IV 24h. CrCl 10–25: 12h. HD: post-dialysis.
  9. C — Enterovirus = most common viral meningitis; summer/autumn; self-limiting; CSF normal glucose; no specific antiviral.
  10. B — Category III exposure (bite): vaccine days 0, 3, 7, 14 + HRIG 20 IU/kg infiltrated around wound (rest IM distant). Previously vaccinated: vaccine only (days 0, 3).

SBAs

  1. B — Classic HSV encephalitis clinical-radiological picture → start aciclovir immediately. Do NOT wait for PCR.
  2. B — Young woman, psychiatric prodrome → dyskinesias/autonomic instability/catatonia = anti-NMDAR encephalitis.
  3. B — FBDS + hyponatraemia + temporal T2 hyperintensity in older man = LGI1 encephalitis.
  4. B — CrCl 15 → aciclovir 10mg/kg IV 12h. Continue treatment, adjust dose.
  5. D — West Nile virus = acute flaccid paralysis (anterior horn cell involvement). JE = encephalitis, less AFP. Dengue/Zika/chikungunya = not classic for AFP.
  6. B — First-line anti-NMDAR: IVIG + high-dose methylprednisolone ± plasma exchange. Rituximab second-line.
  7. B — Neonatal HSV (mother primary genital herpes at delivery) → aciclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×21d. Higher dose than adult. Also needs ceftriaxone/vancomycin empirically until bacterial ruled out.
  8. B — ADEM = post-infectious/post-vaccinal, 1–4 weeks latency, monophasic.
  9. B — VZV encephalitis/vasculopathy: can occur months after zoster, without active rash; aciclovir 10–15mg/kg IV 8h; PCR sensitivity ~70–90%.
  10. B — HSV PCR may be negative <72h. Repeat at 48–72h; continue aciclovir; pursue MRI, EEG, autoantibody panel.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.53 Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, VHF, viral-haemorrhagic-fevers, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers (VHF)

Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics)]], [[Infection Control]]

[!important]
VHF = group of viral infections causing fever + bleeding diathesis + multi-organ failure. Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue (severe), Yellow Fever. High case fatality (25-90%). Transmission: contact with infected body fluids (Ebola, Marburg), aerosols (Lassa), vectors (CCHF, Dengue, Yellow Fever). ISOLATE IMMEDIATELY on suspicion. PPE mandatory. Public health notification.

Learning Objectives

  • Classify VHF by virus family, vector, geography
  • Recognise clinical syndrome: fever + bleeding + multi-organ involvement
  • Apply immediate isolation and infection control measures
  • Initiate supportive management and specific antivirals where available
  • Apply contact tracing and infection control measures
  • Recognise when to suspect VHF in returned traveller

Classification

Virus FamilyVirusesVector/ReservoirGeography
FiloviridaeEbola virus (EBOV)Fruit bats; human-to-humanCentral/West Africa (DRC, Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia)
Marburg virus (MARV)Fruit bats; human-to-humanCentral/East/Southern Africa
ArenaviridaeLassa virus (LASV)Multimammate rat (Mastomys); aerosol/excretaWest Africa (Nigeria, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Guinea)
Lujo virusUnknown rodentSouthern Africa
Bunyaviridae (Nairoviridae)Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF) virusHyalomma ticks; livestock; human-to-humanAfrica, Middle East, Asia, Eastern Europe
Rift Valley Fever (RVF) virusMosquitoes; livestockAfrica, Arabian Peninsula
FlaviviridaeDengue virus (DENV) — severe dengue/DHF/DSSAedes mosquitoesTropical/subtropical worldwide
Yellow Fever (YFV)Aedes/Haemagogus mosquitoesAfrica, South America
Kyasanur Forest Disease (KFD) virusTicks; monkeysIndia (Western Ghats)
Omsk Haemorrhagic FeverTicks; rodentsRussia (Western Siberia)
Alkhurma Haemorrhagic FeverTicks; camelsSaudi Arabia, Egypt

Clinical Syndrome

FeatureTypical Presentation
Incubation2–21 days (Ebola 2–21; Lassa 6–21; CCHF 1–13; Dengue 4–10)
ProdromeFever, myalgia, headache, fatigue, anorexia, nausea/vomiting
Haemorrhagic ManifestationsPetechiae, ecchymoses, mucosal bleeding (gums, nose), GI bleeding, haematuria, vaginal bleeding, injection site bleeding
Organ InvolvementHepatitis (↑ AST/ALT), renal failure, encephalopathy, myocarditis, pancreatitis, ARDS, DIC, shock
LaboratoryThrombocytopenia, leukopenia (Ebola, Lassa), ↑ AST/ALT, ↑ creatinine, prolonged PT/aPTT, fibrinogen ↓, D-dimer ↑

Key Viruses — Clinical Pearls

Ebola Virus Disease (EVD)

AspectDetails
TransmissionDirect contact with blood/body fluids of symptomatic patient; fomites; bushmeat; sexual (persistent in semen)
ClinicalSudden fever, severe headache, myalgia → vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain → haemorrhage (day 5–7), multi-organ failure
CFR50–90% (Zaire ebolavirus); Sudan ebolavirus ~50%
TreatmentInmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) OR Ebanga (ansuvimab) — monoclonal antibodies; supportive care
VaccinerVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) — single dose, ring vaccination

Marburg Virus Disease (MVD)

AspectDetails
TransmissionSimilar to Ebola; bat exposure (caves/mines)
ClinicalIndistinguishable from Ebola; severe MACULOPAPULAR RASH (day 5)
CFR24–88%
TreatmentSupportive; no licenced antiviral/vaccine (experimental monoclonal)

Lassa Fever

AspectDetails
TransmissionAerosol/excreta of Mastomys rats; person-to-person (body fluids); nosocomial
ClinicalInsidious onset; fever, pharyngitis, retrosternal pain, facial oedema, pleural effusions, sensorineural hearing loss (common sequela), encephalopathy
CFR1–15% (hospitalised); higher in pregnancy (3rd trimester)
TreatmentRibavirin IV (early = better outcome); supportive; convalescent plasma (experimental)
VaccineNone licenced (candidates in development)

Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF)

AspectDetails
TransmissionHyalomma tick bite; contact with infected livestock blood/tissues; nosocomial
ClinicalSudden fever, myalgia, headache, dizziness → haemorrhage (day 3–5), hepatomegaly, confusion, multiorgan failure
CFR10–40%
TreatmentRibavirin IV (early = better outcome); supportive
VaccineNone licenced (inactivated vaccine in limited use)

Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever / Dengue Shock Syndrome (DHF/DSS)

AspectDetails
TransmissionAedes aegypti/albopictus mosquito
ClinicalDHF: fever, thrombocytopenia (<100k), haemorrhagic manifestations, plasma leakage (pleural effusion, ascites); DSS: shock, narrow pulse pressure
CFR<1% with proper fluid management; >20% untreated
TreatmentFluid management (crystalloids, colloids; avoid overload); no antiviral
VaccineDengvaxia — ONLY for seropositive individuals (9–45y)

Diagnosis

TestUtility
RT-PCR (blood)Gold standard; rapid (hours); available for Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF
IgM/IgG ELISAAcute/convalescent; Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF
Antigen Detection (Rapid Test)Dengue NS1; Ebola VP40 (field use)
Virus IsolationBSL-4; definitive; not for acute management
Routine LabsThrombocytopenia, ↑ AST/ALT, prolonged PT, DIC screen
[!critical]
VHF SUSPICION → IMMEDIATE ISOLATION + PPE + PUBLIC HEALTH NOTIFICATION. Do NOT send samples routine lab — use dedicated VHF pathway.

Management

Immediate (Hour 0)

ActionDetails
1. ISOLATENegative pressure room; PPE: double gloves, impermeable gown, N95/FFP3, face shield, hood, boots; trained buddy system for donning/doffing
2. NOTIFYPublic health authorities immediately (statutory notification); hospital IPC team
3. SUPPORTIVEFluid resuscitation (crystalloids, avoid overload), blood products (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate for DIC), vasopressors, renal replacement, mechanical ventilation as needed
4. SPECIFIC ANTIVIRALSEbola: Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) OR Ebanga (ansuvimab); Lassa/CCHF: Ribavirin IV (30mg/kg load → 16mg/kg q6h ×4d → 8mg/kg q8h ×6d); Dengue: Fluid management only; Marburg: Supportive only

Infection Control (IPC)

MeasureStandard
IsolationNegative pressure room; anteroom; dedicated equipment
PPEDouble gloves; impermeable gown; N95/FFP3; face shield; hood; boots; buddy system
WasteCategory A infectious; autoclave/incinerate
DecontaminationHypochlorite 10,000ppm (1%) for surfaces; 0.5% chlorine for skin
Contact Tracing21 days (Ebola/Marburg); 17 days (Lassa); 14 days (CCHF)
Dead Body ManagementTrained team; minimal handling; sealed body bag; cremation/deep burial

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP)

VirusPEP
EbolaMonoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga) ± rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine (ring vaccination)
Lassa/CCHFRibavirin (controversial; limited evidence)
DengueNone (no PEPreventative vaccine for seronegative)
MarburgNone licenced

VHF in Returned Traveller

ScenarioAction
Fever + travel to VHF-endemic area + bleeding/contact historyISOLATE IMMEDIATELY; notify IPC/public health; send blood for RT-PCR (Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF)
HCW needlestick from VHF patientImmediate washing; baseline labs; PEP per virus (Ebola: vaccine ± mAb; Lassa/CCHF: ribavirin controversial); monitor 21 days

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • VHF = fever + bleeding + multi-organ failure; high CFR
  • Ebola/Marburg = Filoviridae; contact fluids; CFR 50-90%; monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga); rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • Lassa = Arenaviridae; Mastomys rat; aerosol; facial oedema, hearing loss; Ribavirin IV early
  • CCHF = Nairoviridae; Hyalomma tick; livestock; Ribavirin IV early
  • Dengue DHF/DSS = plasma leakage, thrombocytopenia; fluid management; Dengvaxia seropositive only
  • ISOLATE IMMEDIATELY on suspicion; PPE; negative pressure; public health notification
  • RT-PCR = gold standard; send via dedicated VHF pathway
  • Ribavirin for Lassa/CCHF (early = better)
  • Ebola: Inmazeb/Ebanga monoclonals; rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • No person-to-person for CCHF except nosocomial; Ebola/Marburg = body fluids; Lassa = aerosol/fluid

Common Viva Questions

  1. First action on suspecting VHF? Isolate immediately (negative pressure), PPE, notify public health.
  2. Which VHF has specific antiviral? Lassa & CCHF = Ribavirin; Ebola = monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga).
  3. Lassa fever characteristic features? Facial oedema, pharyngitis, retrosternal pain, sensorineural hearing loss.
  4. CCHF transmission? Hyalomma tick bite; livestock contact; nosocomial.
  5. Ebola treatment? Inmazeb or Ebanga (monoclonal antibodies); supportive care; rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine for prevention.
  6. Dengue DHF vs DSS? DHF = plasma leakage + haemorrhage; DSS = shock (narrow pulse pressure).
  7. First action on VHF suspicion? ISOLATE (negative pressure), PPE, notify public health.
  8. Ribavirin use? Lassa fever & CCHF (early = better outcome).
  9. Ebola vaccine? rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) single dose.
  10. VHF in pregnancy? Lassa severe in 3rd trimester; Ebola high mortality; Ribavirin teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Ribavirin for all VHFOnly Lassa & CCHF (early); NOT for Ebola/Marburg/Dengue
Ebola = airborneContact with body fluids; NOT airborne (except lab aerosols)
Dengue vaccine for allDengvaxia ONLY seropositive (9-45y); seronegative = ↑ severe dengue
Ribavirin safe in pregnancyTeratogenic (Category X) — avoid in pregnancy; Lassa in pregnancy = high mortality
Ebola = always haemorrhageOnly 50% develop overt bleeding; fever + GI symptoms common presentation
Marburg = same as EbolaClinically similar but distinct virus; maculopapular rash more prominent in Marburg
CCHF = only AfricaAlso Middle East, Asia, Eastern Europe
Lassa = only ruralUrban transmission occurs (rat infestation); nosocomial outbreaks common
Vaccine for all VHFOnly Ebola (rVSV-ZEBOV), YF, Dengue (seropositive only) have licenced vaccines
VHF = always fatalSupportive care + early specific therapy ↓ mortality significantly

Mnemonics

  • VHF FIRST: Isolate Negative Pressure; PPE Buddy; Notify Public Health
  • EBOLA: Ebolavirus; Body fluids; Outbreak (Africa); Lethal (50-90%); Antiviral mAbs
  • LASSA: Lassa; Arenavirus; Sensorineural Hearing Loss; Aerosol (rat); Ribavirin
  • CCHF: Crimean-Congo; Hyalomma Tick; Ribavirin; Haemorrhage
  • DENGUE: Dengue; Epidemic; Ng Glucose Uea Electrolytes; Aedes mosquito; Haemorrhagic Fever (DHF) → Shock (DSS)
  • RIBAVIRIN: Lassa + CCHF (early) = Ribavirin; NOT Ebola/Marburg/Dengue
  • VHF ISOLATE: Immediate Single Occupancy Legative Pressure Anteroom Trained Expert

Mind Map

Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers
Filoviridae
Ebola: Contact fluids, CFR 50-90%, mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga), rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
Marburg: Bats/caves, CFR 24-88%, maculopapular rash, supportive only
Arenaviridae
Lassa: Mastomys rat, aerosol, facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin
Lujo: Rare, Southern Africa
Nairoviridae
CCHF: Hyalomma tick, livestock, Ribavirin
RVF: Mosquitoes, livestock
Flaviviridae
Dengue: Aedes mosquito, DHF/DSS, plasma leakage, fluid management, Dengvaxia
Yellow Fever: Aedes, vaccines, lifelong immunity
KFD/Omsk/Alkhurma: Ticks
Management
ISOLATE + PPE + Notify
Supportive: Fluids, Blood products, Vasopressors
Specific: Ebola mAbs, Lassa/CCHF Ribavirin, Dengue fluids
IPC: Negative pressure, PPE, Contact tracing 21d

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected VHF
↓
IMMEDIATE ISOLATION: Negative pressure + PPE + Buddy system
↓
NOTIFY: Public Health + IPC
↓
RT-PCR: Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF
↓
Virus Identified
↓
Inmazeb OR Ebanga + Supportive
Supportive only
Ribavirin IV EARLY + Supportive
Ribavirin IV EARLY + Supportive
Fluid Management (DHF/DSS); NO antiviral
Supportive; Experimental mAbs
↓
Contact Tracing 21d + Ring Vaccination rVSV-ZEBOV
Contact Tracing 17d
Contact Tracing 14d

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • PPE donning/doffing sequence for VHF
  • Ebola treatment centres layout
  • Dengue plasma leakage pathophysiology
  • CCHF tick lifecycle
  • Lassa fever facial oedema
  • Contact tracing timelines

Suggested Video References

  • WHO VHF outbreak response
  • Ebola treatment centre operations
  • Ribavirin use in Lassa/CCHF
  • Dengue fluid management (WHO guidelines)
  • VHF PPE donning/doffing

One-Page Revision Summary

VirusFamilyVector/ReservoirKey FeatureTreatmentVaccine
EbolaFiloviridaeBats; body fluidsHigh CFR 50-90%Inmazeb/Ebanga mAbsrVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo)
MarburgFiloviridaeBats; body fluidsMaculopapular rashSupportiveNone
LassaArenaviridaeMastomys rat; aerosolFacial oedema, hearing lossRibavirin IV (early)None
CCHFNairoviridaeHyalomma tick; livestockLiver involvementRibavirin IV (early)None
Dengue DHF/DSSFlaviviridaeAedes mosquitoPlasma leakage, thrombocytopeniaFluid managementDengvaxia (seropositive)
Yellow FeverFlaviviridaeAedes mosquitoHepatorenal failureSupportiverVSV-YF (lifelong)
General——ISOLATE + PPE + NotifySupportive + SpecificEbola, YF, Dengue

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • Ebola: mAbs, vaccine, CFR
  • Lassa: facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin early
  • CCHF: tick, livestock, Ribavirin early
  • Dengue DHF/DSS: plasma leakage, fluid management
  • Ebola: mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga), rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • First action VHF: ISOLATE + PPE + Notify
  • Ribavirin for Lassa/CCHF only
  • Dengue vaccine: seropositive only
  • Contact tracing: Ebola 21d, Lassa 17d, CCHF 14d

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • ISOLATE + PPE + Notify = first actions
  • Ebola: mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga), vaccine (rVSV-ZEBOV)
  • Lassa: facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin early
  • CCHF: Hyalomma tick, Ribavirin early
  • Dengue: fluid management for DHF/DSS
  • Ribavirin for Lassa/CCHF (NOT Ebola/Marburg/Dengue)
  • RT-PCR gold standard; isolate first

Should Know

  • Marburg: maculopapular rash, supportive only
  • Rift Valley Fever: livestock, mosquito
  • Yellow Fever: lifelong vaccine
  • Ebola ring vaccination
  • Post-exposure prophylaxis per virus
  • Contact tracing durations

Nice to Know

  • Experimental therapies (favipiravir, remdesivir for Ebola)
  • New vaccines (MVA-BN for Mpox/Ebola)
  • VHF in pregnancy
  • Nosocomial outbreak management
  • Diagnostic pathway in non-endemic settings

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Classification of VHF by family/vector/geography
  2. Ebola (transmission, clinical, treatment, vaccine, IPC)
  3. Marburg (clinical similarity, differences, supportive care)
  4. Lassa (transmission, clinical, hearing loss, Ribavirin)
  5. CCHF (tick transmission, livestock, Ribavirin)
  6. Dengue DHF/DSS (plasma leakage, fluid management, vaccine)
  7. Yellow Fever (brief)
  8. General management: ISOLATE + PPE + Notify + Supportive + Specific
  9. Infection control, contact tracing, PEP

Short Note Skeleton

  • Ebola: Filoviridae, body fluids, CFR 50-90%, Inmazeb/Ebanga, rVSV-ZEBOV
  • Marburg: Filoviridae, rash, supportive
  • Lassa: Arenaviridae, Mastomys, facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin IV early
  • CCHF: Nairoviridae, Hyalomma tick, livestock, Ribavirin IV early
  • Dengue: Aedes, DHF/DSS plasma leakage, fluid management, Dengvaxia seropositive
  • Yellow Fever: Aedes, lifelong vaccine
  • General: ISOLATE + PPE + Notify → RT-PCR → Supportive + Specific
  • Ribavirin: Lassa/CCHF only; NOT Ebola/Marburg/Dengue

Viva One-Liners

  • VHF suspicion → ISOLATE + PPE + Notify IMMEDIATELY
  • Ebola: mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga); vaccine rVSV-ZEBOV
  • Lassa: facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin early
  • CCHF: Hyalomma tick, Ribavirin early
  • Dengue DHF: plasma leakage → fluid management
  • Ribavirin: Lassa/CCHF only
  • Ebola vaccine: rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo)

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • HCW returning from DRC with fever, bleeding → ISOLATE, PPE, RT-PCR Ebola/Marburg/Lassa/CCHF, supportive, public health
  • Pregnant woman in Nigeria with fever, facial oedema → Lassa fever; Ribavirin IV controversial (teratogenic); multidisciplinary
  • Farmer in Turkey with tick bite, fever, bleeding → CCHF; Ribavirin IV early; contact tracing 14 days
  • Traveller from Thailand with fever, thrombocytopenia, shock → Dengue DHF/DSS; fluid management; avoid NSAIDs

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

VHF: ISOLATE + PPE + Notify. Ebola: Filoviridae, body fluids, Inmazeb/Ebanga, rVSV-ZEBOV. Marburg: Filoviridae, rash, supportive. Lassa: Arenaviridae, Mastomys, facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin IV early. CCHF: Nairoviridae, Hyalomma tick, livestock, Ribavirin IV early. Dengue DHF/DSS: plasma leakage, thrombocytopenia → fluid management; Dengvaxia seropositive. YF: lifelong vaccine. Ribavirin: Lassa/CCHF ONLY (NOT Ebola/Marburg/Dengue). First action: ISOLATE + PPE + Notify.

Summary

Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers (VHF) are a group of viral infections characterised by fever, haemorrhagic manifestations, and multi-organ failure with high case fatality. Filoviridae: Ebola (EBOV) — contact with body fluids, CFR 50–90%, treatment Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) or Ebanga (ansuvimab), vaccine rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo); Marburg (MARV) — clinically similar, maculopapular rash, supportive only. Arenaviridae: Lassa (LASV) — Mastomys rat excreta aerosol, facial oedema, sensorineural hearing loss, Ribavirin IV early; Lujo — rare. Nairoviridae: CCHF — Hyalomma tick bite, livestock contact, Ribavirin IV early; Rift Valley Fever — mosquito, livestock. Flaviviridae: Dengue (DENV) — Aedes mosquito, DHF/DSS = plasma leakage + thrombocytopenia → fluid management; Dengvaxia (seropositive only); Yellow Fever — lifelong vaccine; Kyasanur Forest/Omsk/Alkhurma — ticks. General management: ISOLATE IMMEDIATELY (negative pressure) + PPE (buddy system) + NOTIFY public health → RT-PCR (Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF) → Supportive (fluids, blood products, vasopressors, RRT, ventilation) + Specific antivirals: Ebola = Inmazeb/Ebanga; Lassa/CCHF = Ribavirin IV early; Dengue = fluid management; Marburg = supportive. IPC: negative pressure, PPE (double gloves, impermeable gown, N95/FFP3, face shield, hood, boots), contact tracing (Ebola 21d, Lassa 17d, CCHF 14d), waste Category A. No person-to-person for CCHF except nosocomial; Ebola/Marburg = body fluids; Lassa = aerosol/excreta. Ribavirin teratogenic — avoid in pregnancy. Ring vaccination for Ebola (rVSV-ZEBOV).

MCQs (10)

  1. First action on suspecting Viral Haemorrhagic Fever:

A. Start Ribavirin

B. Send blood for PCR

C. Isolate immediately in negative pressure room + PPE + notify public health

D. Start fluid resuscitation

E. Contact tracing

  1. Which VHF has a licenced specific antiviral treatment (monoclonal antibodies)?

A. Marburg

B. Lassa

C. Ebola (Inmazeb / Ebanga)

D. CCHF

D. Dengue

  1. Ribavirin is recommended as early treatment for which VHFs?

A. Ebola and Marburg

B. Lassa fever and CCHF

C. Dengue and Yellow Fever

D. Ebola and CCHF

E. All VHFs

  1. Lassa fever is associated with which characteristic long-term sequela?

A. Retinitis

B. Sensorineural hearing loss

C. Arthritis

C. Encephalitis

E. Nephropathy

  1. Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF) is transmitted by:

A. Aedes mosquito

B. Hyalomma tick

C. Multimammate rat aerosol

C. Fruit bat contact

E. Person-to-person (airborne)

  1. Ebola virus disease — specific treatment:

A. Ribavirin IV

B. Favipiravir

C. Monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb / Ebanga)

D. Remdesivir

E. Convalescent plasma

  1. Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever (DHF) / Dengue Shock Syndrome (DSS) — cornerstone of management:

A. Ribavirin

B. Fluid management (crystalloids/colloids; avoid overload)

C. Platelet transfusion

D. Corticosteroids

E. Antiviral therapy

  1. Which VHF has a single-dose vaccine providing lifelong immunity?

A. Ebola

B. Lassa

C. Yellow Fever

D. CCHF

E. Dengue

  1. First action on suspecting VHF in a returned traveller:

A. Start broad-spectrum antibiotics

B. Isolate in negative pressure room + full PPE + notify public health

C. Send blood for routine culture

D. Start Ribavirin empirically

E. Observe for 24 hours

  1. Ribavirin is contraindicated in pregnancy. Which VHF treatment is affected?

A. Ebola

B. Lassa fever and CCHF (Ribavirin is Category X teratogen)

C. Dengue

D. Marburg

E. Yellow Fever

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 35-year-old healthcare worker returns from a 4-week mission in DRC (Ebola outbreak zone). Day 3 post-return: fever 38.5°C, myalgia, headache. No bleeding. Immediate management?

A. Start Ribavirin

B. Isolate in negative pressure room + full PPE + notify public health + RT-PCR (Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF)

C. Start Inmazeb empirically

D. Observe with supportive care

E. Dengue NS1 test

  1. A 28-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks) in Nigeria develops fever, pharyngitis, facial oedema, and retrosternal pain. Lassa fever suspected. Best management?

A. Ribavirin IV (early)

B. Multidisciplinary team; Ribavirin controversial (teratogenic); supportive care; delivery planning; neonatal monitoring

C. Favipiravir

C. Supportive care only; no Ribavirin

E. Favipiravir + Ribavirin

  1. A 40-year-old farmer in Turkey presents with fever, myalgia, bleeding gums, and epistaxis 3 days after removing a tick. Platelets 20, AST 800. Suspected CCHF. Best empirical treatment?

A. Doxycycline

B. Ribavirin IV (30mg/kg load → 16mg/kg q6h ×4d → 8mg/kg q8h ×6d) + supportive

C. Favipiravir

C. Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline

E. Supportive care only

  1. A 30-year-old man returns from Thailand with 4 days of fever, headache, myalgia. Day 5: petechiae, platelet count 60, rising haematocrit. Suspected DHF. Management?

A. Ribavirin IV

B. Fluid management: crystalloids (RL) ± colloids; monitor haematocrit/platelets; avoid NSAIDs; platelet transfusion only if <10k or bleeding

C. Platelet transfusion immediately

D. Corticosteroids

D. Aspirin for fever

  1. A healthcare worker sustains needlestick injury from a patient with confirmed Ebola. PEP?

A. Ribavirin for 10 days

B. rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine (Ervebo) single dose + consider monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga)

C. Favipiravir for 10 days

D. Doxycycline for 21 days

E. No PEP available

  1. A patient with suspected Lassa fever is started on Ribavirin. Monitoring for toxicity:

A. Renal function

B. Haemolysis (reticulocytosis, ↓ Hb, ↑ LDH, ↑ bilirubin); monitor CBC, reticulocytes, LDH, bilirubin

C. Hepatic transaminases

D. QTc prolongation

E. Neutropenia

  1. A traveller returns from rural Nigeria with fever, facial oedema, and hearing difficulty. Most likely diagnosis?

A. Ebola

B. Lassa fever

C. CCHF

D. Dengue

E. Typhoid

  1. Contact tracing period for Ebola virus disease contacts:

A. 14 days

B. 17 days

C. 21 days

D. 28 days

E. 42 days

  1. Dengvaxia (Dengue vaccine) — correct indication:

A. All children 9–16 years

B. Seropositive individuals 9–45 years only

C. All travellers to endemic areas

D. Pregnant women

E. Immunocompromised only

  1. A patient with confirmed Marburg virus disease. Specific antiviral?

A. Ribavirin

B. Favipiravir

C. None licenced (supportive care only; experimental mAbs in trials)

D. Inmazeb

E. Remdesivir

  1. First action when a patient presents with fever and bleeding after travel to a VHF-endemic area:

A. Draw blood for PCR

B. Isolate immediately in negative pressure room with full PPE + buddy system + notify public health

C. Start Ribavirin

D. Start fluid resuscitation

E. Contact infectious diseases team

Flashcards

  • Q: VHF first action

A: ISOLATE + PPE + Notify

  • Q: Ebola treatment

A: Inmazeb/Ebanga (mAbs); rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine

  • Q: Lassa features

A: Facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin early

  • Q: CCHF

A: Hyalomma tick, Ribavirin early

  • Q: Ribavirin for

A: Lassa, CCHF ONLY

  • Q: Dengue DHF/DSS

A: Plasma leakage, fluid management

  • Q: Ebola vaccine

A: rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo)

  • Q: YF vaccine

A: Lifelong immunity

  • Q: VHF PCR

A: Gold standard

  • Q: Contact tracing

A: Ebola 21d, Lassa 17d, CCHF 14d

  • Q: Ribavirin pregnancy

A: Teratogenic (Category X)

  • Q: Ebola PEP

A: Vaccine + mAbs

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — ISOLATE (negative pressure), PPE, notify public health are the absolute first steps; clinical management follows.
  2. C — Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) and Ebanga (ansuvimab) are FDA-approved monoclonal antibodies for Ebola.
  3. B — Ribavirin has proven benefit early in Lassa fever and CCHF; no benefit in Ebola, Marburg, Dengue.
  4. B — Sensorineural hearing loss occurs in ~25% of Lassa fever survivors.
  5. B — CCHF transmitted by Hyalomma tick bite or contact with infected livestock blood/tissues.
  6. C — Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) and Ebanga (ansuvimab) are approved for Ebola.
  7. B — DHF/DSS = plasma leakage → meticulous fluid management (WHO algorithm); no antiviral.
  8. C — Yellow Fever vaccine (17D) = single dose, lifelong immunity (WHO).
  9. B — ISOLATE + PPE + Notify = absolute first step for any suspected VHF.
  10. B — Ribavirin is Category X teratogen; Lassa/CCHF treatment in pregnancy requires risk-benefit discussion.

SBAs

  1. B — Suspected VHF → immediate isolation, PPE, notification, RT-PCR panel before specific therapy.
  2. B — Lassa in pregnancy: Ribavirin teratogenic (Category X); multidisciplinary decision; often supportive care with careful risk-benefit.
  3. B — CCHF: Ribavirin IV early (30mg/kg load → 16mg/kg q6h ×4d → 8mg/kg q8h ×6d) + supportive care.
  4. B — DHF/DSS: WHO fluid management algorithm (crystalloids + colloids guided by haematocrit); avoid NSAIDs/platelet transfusion unless bleeding + <10k.
  5. B — Ebola PEP: rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) single dose + consider monoclonal antibodies; ring vaccination strategy.
  6. B — Ribavirin causes dose-dependent haemolytic anaemia; monitor Hb, reticulocytes, LDH, bilirubin.
  7. B — Nigeria + facial oedema + hearing loss = Lassa fever.
  8. C — Ebola/Marburg incubation max 21d; contact tracing 21 days.
  9. B — Dengvaxia ONLY for seropositive 9-45y; seronegative recipients have ↑ severe dengue risk.
  10. C — Marburg: no licenced antiviral; supportive care; experimental mAbs in development.
  11. B — ISOLATE + PPE + Notify = absolute first action before any testing/treatment.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.54 Viral Hepatitis

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, viral-hepatitis, hepatitis, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Viral Hepatitis Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Viral Hepatitis (A, B, C, D, E)

Related: [[HIV Infection and AIDS]], [[Liver Disease]], [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Infection in Pregnancy and Neonates]], [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]]

[!important]
Viral hepatitis = liver inflammation from hepatotropic viruses (A, B, C, D, E). Hep A/E = faecal-oral; acute self-limiting. Hep B/C/D = blood-borne; chronic infection risk. Hep D = defective virus requiring Hep B. Hep E = severe in pregnancy (genotype 1). Hep B/C = leading causes of cirrhosis/HCC. DAA cure Hep C (>95%); Hep B suppressible not curable. Vaccines: Hep A, Hep B (also prevents Hep D).

Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate hepatitis viruses by transmission, clinical course, and chronicity
  • Interpret serology and molecular markers for each hepatitis virus
  • Stage Hepatitis B and C (fibrosis, cirrhosis, HCC risk)
  • Select and monitor antiviral therapy for Hepatitis B and C
  • Manage complications (cirrhosis, HCC, reactivation)
  • Apply vaccination and prevention strategies
  • Manage special populations (pregnancy, HIV coinfection, immunosuppression, transplant)

Overview of Hepatitis Viruses

FeatureHAVHBVHCVHDVHEV
FamilyPicornaviridaeHepadnaviridaeFlaviviridaeDeltaviridae (defective)Hepeviridae
GenomeRNA (+)DNA (partially ds)RNA (+)RNA (-)RNA (+)
TransmissionFaecal-oralBlood, sexual, verticalBlood (mainly), sexual, verticalBlood, sexual (requires HBV)Faecal-oral (waterborne)
ChronicityNever5–10% adults; 90% neonates70–85%Only with HBVRare (except immunosuppressed)
Incubation15–50 days60–150 days14–180 days30–180 days15–60 days
VaccineYesYesNoHep B vaccine preventsYes (China only)
TreatmentSupportiveNUCs (suppress), PegIFNDAA cure >95%PegIFN (limited)Supportive (Ribavirin if chronic)

Hepatitis A (HAV)

AspectDetails
TransmissionFaecal-oral (contaminated food/water, person-to-person); shellfish; outbreaks in institutions
Risk GroupsTravellers to endemic areas, MSM, PWID, household contacts, institutionalised
ClinicalAcute self-limiting; prodrome (fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea) → jaundice, dark urine, pale stools; no chronic infection; fulminant rare (<0.5%)
DiagnosisAnti-HAV IgM (acute); Anti-HAV IgG (past immunity); HAV RNA (research)
ManagementSupportive; no specific antiviral; hospitalise if severe/fulminant; avoid hepatotoxins
PreventionHep A vaccine (inactivated): 2 doses (0, 6–12m); Post-exposure: vaccine ± IgG (within 2w); hygiene
SpecialPregnancy: increased severity in 3rd trimester; vaccine safe

Hepatitis B (HBV)

Natural History

PhaseHBsAgHBeAgHBV DNAALTHistology
Immune Tolerant++Very high (>10⁷)NormalMinimal inflammation
Immune Active (HBeAg+)++High (>10⁵)ElevatedModerate-severe inflammation
Inactive Carrier+−Low (<2000)NormalMinimal
HBeAg-Negative Chronic Hepatitis+−Intermittent >2000ElevatedModerate-severe
Resolved−−UndetectableNormalResolved (anti-HBs+, anti-HBc+)

Serology Interpretation

MarkerMeaning
HBsAgCurrent infection (acute or chronic)
Anti-HBsImmunity (vaccine or resolved)
Anti-HBc IgMAcute/recent infection (<6m)
Anti-HBc IgGPast or chronic infection
HBeAgHigh replicative, high infectivity
Anti-HBeLower replicative (usually)
HBV DNAViral load (quantifies replication)

Fibrosis Staging & HCC Surveillance

ToolIndication
Transient Elastography (FibroScan)Non-invasive fibrosis staging (kPa)
APRI / FIB-4Serum-based fibrosis scores
Liver BiopsyGold standard (if noninvasive discordant)
HCC Surveillance6-monthly US ± AFP if cirrhosis OR high-risk chronic HBV (Asian male >40, female >50, family HCC, cirrhosis)

HBV Treatment Indications (EASL/AASLD/APASL)

IndicationCriteria
Immune Active (HBeAg+)HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL + ALT >2×ULN (≥2× over 3–6m)
HBeAg-Negative Chronic HepatitisHBV DNA >2,000 IU/mL + ALT >ULN (≥2× over 3–6m)
Cirrhosis (Compensated)Any detectable HBV DNA
Decompensated CirrhosisAny detectable HBV DNA
Reactivation RiskImmunosuppression/chemo (prophylactic NUCs)

HBV Treatment — Nucleos(t)ide Analogues (NUCs)

DrugDosePotencyResistanceRenal AdjustNotes
Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate (TDF)245mg ODHighExtremely low↓ if CrCl <50Bone/renal toxicity; 1st-line
Tenofovir Alafenamide (TAF)25mg ODHighExtremely lowSafer renal/bonePreferred over TDF
Entecavir (ETV)0.5mg OD (1mg if NUC-experienced)HighLow (naïve); higher if LAM-experienced↓ if CrCl <501st-line
Lamivudine (LAM)100mg ODLowHigh (70% at 5y)↓ if CrCl <50Not 1st-line
Adefovir10mg ODLowModerate↓ if CrCl <50Nephrotoxic
Telbivudine600mg ODModerateModerate↓ if CrCl <50Myopathy, neuropathy
[!critical]
TAF/TDF/ETV = 1st-line (high barrier, potent). Lifelong therapy usually needed. Stop criteria: HBeAg+ → HBeAg seroconversion + consolidation 12m; HBeAg− → sustained suppression 3y + HBsAg loss (rare). PegIFN-α: finite duration (48w); lower SVR; side effects.

Special HBV Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyTDF/TAF safe; avoid ETV (limited data); TDF 3rd trimester if HBV DNA >200,000 (reduce vertical transmission); infant HBIG + vaccine 12h
HIV CoinfectionTDF/TAF + 3TC/FTC covers both; DTG preferred; avoid 3TC monotherapy
Immunosuppression/ChemoProphylactic NUCs (TDF/TAF/ETV) if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+ (reactivation risk)
HBV ReactivationNUCs immediately; monitor HBV DNA; continue ≥12m post-immunosuppression
Hep D CoinfectionPegIFN-α 48w only effective therapy; NUCs alone insufficient

Hepatitis C (HCV)

Natural History

StageOutcome
Acute HCV15–25% spontaneous clearance; 75–85% chronic
Chronic HCV20–30% cirrhosis over 20–30y; risk factors: age >50, male, alcohol, HIV, obesity, steatosis
CirrhosisDecompensation (ascites, varices, encephalopathy), HCC (1–4%/yr)

HCV Genotypes & Treatment Selection

GenotypePrevalencePreferred DAA RegimenDuration
GT140–50%Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (GLE/PIB) 300/120mg OD OR Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (SOF/VEL)8w (no cirrhosis) / 12w (cirrhosis)
GT215–20%GLE/PIB OR SOF/VEL8w / 12w
GT330–40%GLE/PIB (preferred) OR SOF/VEL8w (no cirrhosis) / 16w (cirrhosis)
GT410–15%GLE/PIB OR SOF/VEL12w
GT5/6RareGLE/PIB12w
[!key]
Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (GLE/PIB) = pan-genotypic, 8w for non-cirrhotic, 12w cirrhotic. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (SOF/VEL) = pan-genotypic alternative. SVR >95% all genotypes.

HCV Treatment — Key Principles

AspectDetail
SVR DefinitionHCV RNA undetectable 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12) = cure
Pre-treatmentGenotype, Fibrosis (FibroScan/APRI/FIB-4), NS5A RAS testing (GT3/1a if cirrhosis), Drug interactions, Renal function
MonitoringHCV RNA at week 4 (rapid virological response), end of treatment, SVR12
Retreatment FailureGLE/PIB + SOF + RBV (16–24w) OR SOF/VEL/VOX (Vosevi) 12w

Special HCV Situations

SituationManagement
HIV CoinfectionSame DAA regimens; check interactions (DTG/GLE/PIB OK); same SVR
Renal Impairment (CrCl<30)GLE/PIB (no renal adjust) OR SOF/VEL (avoid SOF if <30); Grazoprevir/Elbasvir alternative
Decompensated Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C)SOF/VEL + RBV 12w (Child-Pugh B); avoid protease inhibitors (GLE/PIB contraindicated)
Post-Liver TransplantSOF/VEL 12w (preferred); GLE/PIB alternative; monitor interactions with CNI
Acute HCVDAA if persists >12w; treat as chronic

Hepatitis D (HDV)

AspectDetails
Defective VirusRequires HBsAg for assembly/transmission
TransmissionCo-infection (simultaneous HBV+HDV) or superinfection (HDV on chronic HBV)
ClinicalSuperinfection = more severe; rapid cirrhosis, high HCC risk
DiagnosisAnti-HDV IgM/IgG; HDV RNA (quantitative)
TreatmentPegIFN-α 48 weeks (only effective therapy); SVR ~25%; NUCs alone insufficient
New TherapiesBulevirtide (entry inhibitor) — approved EU; Lonafarnib (assembly inhibitor) in trials

Hepatitis E (HEV)

AspectDetails
GenotypesGT1/2 (human, faecal-oral, waterborne, endemic Asia/Africa); GT3/4 (zoonotic, pigs, undercooked pork, Europe/USA)
ClinicalAcute self-limiting (GT1/2); GT3/4 = chronic in immunosuppressed (transplant, HIV, chemo)
Severe in PregnancyGT1: 20–30% mortality in 3rd trimester (fulminant hepatic failure)
DiagnosisAnti-HEV IgM (acute); HEV RNA (blood/stool); Anti-HEV IgG (past)
TreatmentAcute: supportive; Chronic (GT3/4 in immunosuppressed): Ribavirin 600mg OD ×3m (reduce immunosuppression); PegIFN-α if ribavirin fails

Reactivation & Special Populations

HBV Reactivation

RiskProphylaxis
HBsAg+ (any immunosuppression)TDF/TAF/ETV prophylaxis (start before/during, continue ≥12m post)
HBsAg−/anti-HBc+ (rituximab, high-dose steroids, HSCT)TDF/TAF/ETV prophylaxis or close monitoring (HBV DNA q1–3m)
Resolved HBV (anti-HBc+ only)Low risk; monitor HBV DNA if high-risk (rituximab, HSCT, alemtuzumab)

HCV Reinfection

RiskManagement
Post-SVR reinfectionRe-treat with DAA; same SVR rates; harm reduction (PWID: OAT, needle exchange)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • HAV/HEV = faecal-oral, acute only; HBV/HCV/HDV = blood-borne, chronic risk
  • HBV: immune tolerant → immune active → inactive carrier → HBeAg-negative CHB
  • HBV treatment: TDF/TAF/ETV 1st-line (high barrier); lifelong usually; stop if HBeAg seroconversion + 12m consolidation
  • HCV: DAA cure >95%; GLE/PIB pan-genotypic 8w (non-cirr) / 12w (cirr); GT3 cirrhosis = 16w SOF/VEL or 12w GLE/PIB
  • HDV: requires HBsAg; superinfection worse; PegIFN only effective therapy; bulevirtide emerging
  • HEV: GT1/2 acute; GT3/4 chronic in immunocompromised; pregnancy GT1 = 20-30% mortality; chronic = ribavirin
  • HBV reactivation: HBsAg+ = prophylactic NUCs; anti-HBc+ (rituximab/HSCT) = prophylactic NUCs
  • Pregnancy: HAV/HEV severe in 3rd tri; HBV TDF 3rd tri if DNA>200k; HCV treat postpartum; HEV severe in pregnancy
  • HCC surveillance: 6-monthly US ± AFP if cirrhosis or high-risk chronic HBV

Common Viva Questions

  1. HBV serology interpretation? HBsAg = infection; Anti-HBs = immunity; Anti-HBc IgM = acute; Anti-HBc IgG = past/chronic; HBeAg = high replication.
  2. HBV treatment indications? Immune active (DNA>20k + ALT>2×ULN), HBeAg-neg CHB (DNA>2k + ALT>ULN), cirrhosis (any DNA).
  3. HCV treatment — pan-genotypic regimen? GLE/PIB 300/120mg OD ×8w (non-cirr) / 12w (cirr); SOF/VEL alternative.
  4. HCV GT3 cirrhosis treatment? SOF/VEL 16w OR GLE/PIB 12w (with RBV if prior treatment failure).
  5. HDV treatment? PegIFN-α 48w only proven; bulevirtide emerging; NUCs alone insufficient.
  6. HEV in pregnancy? GT1 3rd trimester = 20-30% mortality; supportive only.
  7. HBV reactivation prophylaxis? HBsAg+ = TDF/TAF/ETV; anti-HBc+ (rituximab/HSCT) = TDF/TAF/ETV.
  8. HCV cure definition? SVR12 = undetectable HCV RNA at 12 weeks post-treatment.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
HBV "cure" with NUCsRare HBsAg loss; NUCs suppress, don't eradicate cccDNA
HCV SVR = cure = no more liver diseaseFibrosis/cirrhosis may persist; HCC surveillance if cirrhosis persists
HDV can exist without HBVDefective virus; requires HBsAg
HEV always acuteGT3/4 chronic in immunocompromised
PegIFN for HCV still 1st-lineDAA >95% SVR, fewer SE, shorter; PegIFN obsolete for HCV
TDF/TAF need renal adjustTDF yes; TAF minimal adjust (safer renal/bone)
HCV GT3 = same as GT1GT3 cirrhosis = 16w SOF/VEL or 12w GLE/PIB (sometimes +RBV)
HBV DNA >20,000 always treatOnly if ALT elevated (immune active); immune tolerant = observe
HEV vaccine available globallyOnly licensed in China (Hecolin)
HCV GT1a cirrhosis = 12w GLE/PIBGT1a cirrhosis = 12w GLE/PIB (no RBV needed); GT1a non-cirr = 8w

Mnemonics

  • HEPATITIS TRANSMISSION: HAV = Faecal-Oral; HBV/HCV/HDV = Blood; HEV = Faecal-Oral
  • HBV PHASES: Immune Tolerant → Immune Active → Inactive Carrier → HBeAg-Negative CHB
  • HBV TREATMENT: TDF/TAF/ETV = 1st-line; Lifelong; Stop: Seroconversion +12m / HBsAg loss
  • HCV DAA: GLE/PIB = Pan-genotypic; 8w non-cirr, 12w cirr; SOF/VEL alternative
  • HCV GT3: Cirr = 16w SOF/VEL or 12w GLE/PIB
  • HDV: Defective; Needs HBsAg; Superinfection = Worse; PegIFN Only
  • HEV: GT1/2 = Acute; GT3/4 = Chronic (immuno); Pregnancy GT1 = Death 20-30%
  • HBV REACTIVATION: HBsAg+ = Prophylaxis; Anti-HBc+ + Rituximab/HSCT = Prophylaxis

Mind Map

Viral Hepatitis
HAV
Faecal-oral; Acute only; Anti-HAV IgM dx; Vaccine 2-dose
HBV
Blood/Vertical; Phases: Tolerant→Active→Inactive→HBeAg-neg CHB
Markers: HBsAg, Anti-HBs, Anti-HBc IgM/IgG, HBeAg, DNA
Tx: TDF/TAF/ETV 1st-line (high barrier); Lifelong; PegIFN finite
Pregnancy: TDF 3rd tri if DNA>200k; Infant HBIG+Vaccine
Reactivation: HBsAg+ = prophylaxis; anti-HBc+ + rituximab = prophylaxis
HCV
Blood; 75-85% chronic; Genotypes 1-6
DAA cure >95%; GLE/PIB pan-genotypic 8/12w; SOF/VEL alt
GT3 cirrhosis: SOF/VEL 16w or GLE/PIB 12w
SVR12 = cure; Retreatment: Vosevi or GLE/PIB+SOF+RBV
HDV
Defective; Needs HBsAg; Co-infection vs Superinfection
PegIFN 48w only proven; Bulevirtide emerging
HEV
GT1/2: Faecal-oral, Acute; GT3/4: Zoonotic, Chronic (immuno)
Pregnancy GT1: 20-30% mortality
Chronic: Ribavirin 3m; reduce immunosuppression
Complications
Cirrhosis, HCC, Decompensation
HCC surveillance: 6m US ± AFP if cirr or high-risk HBV

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Viral Hepatitis
↓
Serology Panel: HAV IgM, HBsAg, Anti-HBs, Anti-HBc IgM/IgG, HBeAg, HCV Ab, HEV IgM
↓
HBsAg+?
↓
HBV: HBeAg, DNA, Genotype, Fibrosis
HCV Ab+?
Anti-HAV IgM+?
↓
Phase
HBeAg+ & HBsAg+?
HCC Surveillance: 6m US ± AFP if Cirr/High-risk
HCV RNA, Genotype, Fibrosis
Anti-HEV IgM+?
↓
Treat: TDF/TAF/ETV
Treat if DNA>2k + ALT>ULN
Monitor 6m
DAA: GLE/PIB 8w (non-cirr) / 12w (cirr)

One-Page Revision Summary

VirusTransmissionChronicityKey SerologyTreatmentSpecial
HAVFaecal-oralNoneAnti-HAV IgMSupportiveVaccine 2-dose
HBVBlood/Vertical5–10% adultsHBsAg, HBeAg, DNATDF/TAF/ETV lifelongReactivation prophylaxis
HCVBlood75–85%HCV Ab → RNA, GenotypeGLE/PIB 8/12w (pan-genotypic)SVR12 = cure
HDVBlood (needs HBsAg)With HBVAnti-HDV, HDV RNAPegIFN 48w (only)Bulevirtide emerging
HEVFaecal-oral (GT1/2), Zoonotic (GT3/4)Rare (GT3/4 immuno)Anti-HEV IgMAcute: supportive; Chronic: RibavirinPregnancy GT1: 20-30% mortality

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • HBV serology interpretation
  • HBV phases and treatment indications
  • HBV 1st-line NUCs (TDF/TAF/ETV)
  • HCV DAA pan-genotypic (GLE/PIB, SOF/VEL)
  • HCV GT3 cirrhosis (16w SOF/VEL or 12w GLE/PIB)
  • HDV = defective, needs HBV, PegIFN only
  • HEV pregnancy mortality (GT1 3rd tri)
  • HBV reactivation prophylaxis (HBsAg+, anti-HBc+ risk)

Should Know

  • HBV phases detail
  • HCV genotype-based treatment details
  • HDV bulevirtide
  • HEV GT3/4 chronic in immunocompromised
  • HBV in pregnancy (TDF 3rd tri)
  • HIV/HBV coinfection management
  • Fibrosis assessment (FibroScan, APRI, FIB-4)
  • HCC surveillance criteria

Nice to Know

  • Novel HBV therapies (capsid inhibitors, siRNA, HBsAg loss strategies)
  • HCV resistance-associated substitutions (RAS)
  • HDV bulevirtide/lonafarnib mechanisms
  • HEV extrahepatic manifestations
  • HBV functional cure definitions
  • Cost-effectiveness of DAA
  • Post-transplant HBV/HCV management

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. HBV (transmission, natural history phases, serology, treatment indications/regimens, pregnancy, reactivation)
  2. HCV (transmission, genotypes, DAA regimens by genotype/fibrosis, SVR, retreatment)
  3. HDV (defective, superinfection, diagnosis, PegIFN, emerging therapies)
  4. HAV/HEV (transmission, clinical, diagnosis, pregnancy, treatment)
  5. Complications (cirrhosis, HCC surveillance, decompensation)
  6. Special populations (pregnancy, HIV coinfection, immunosuppression, transplant)

Short Note Skeleton

  • HBV: Blood/vertical; 4 phases; Serology (HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc, HBeAg, DNA); Treat immune active/cirrhotics: TDF/TAF/ETV; Pregnancy: TDF 3rd tri
  • HCV: Blood; 75-85% chronic; Genotypes 1-6; DAA: GLE/PIB 8w non-cirr/12w cirr; GT3 cirr: SOF/VEL 16w; SVR12=cure
  • HDV: Needs HBsAg; Superinfection worse; PegIFN 48w only; Bulevirtide emerging
  • HAV/HEV: Faecal-oral; HAV acute only; HEV GT1 pregnancy 20-30% mortality; GT3/4 chronic in immuno
  • Reactivation: HBsAg+ = NUC prophylaxis; anti-HBc+ + rituximab/HSCT = NUC prophylaxis

Viva One-Liners

  • HBV: 4 phases; Treat immune active/cirrhotics; TDF/TAF/ETV 1st-line
  • HCV: GLE/PIB pan-genotypic 8/12w; GT3 cirr 16w SOF/VEL
  • HDV: Needs HBsAg; PegIFN 48w only
  • HEV: GT1 pregnancy 20-30% mortality
  • Reactivation: HBsAg+ = prophylaxis; anti-HBc+ + rituximab = prophylaxis
  • HCC surveillance: 6m US ± AFP if cirr or high-risk HBV

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 30M, HBsAg+, HBeAg+, DNA 10⁸, ALT 120 → Immune active HBV → TDF 245mg OD; monitor DNA/ALT
  • 40F, HCV GT3, cirrhosis (FibroScan 18kPa) → SOF/VEL 400/100mg OD ×16w (or GLE/PIB 12w)
  • 35M, HBsAg+, anti-HBc+, starting rituximab for lymphoma → TDF prophylaxis throughout + 12m post
  • 28F pregnant 28w, HBsAg+, DNA 5×10⁷ → TDF 3rd trimester; infant HBIG + vaccine 12h
  • 50M, HCV GT3, cirrhosis, failed SOF/VEL → SOF/VEL/VOX (Vosevi) 12w or GLE/PIB+SOF+RBV 16w

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

VIRAL HEPATITIS: HAV/HEV=faecal-oral; HBV/HCV/HDV=blood. HBV: 4 phases; Serology: HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc IgM/IgG, HBeAg, DNA. Treat: Immune active, HBeAg-neg CHB, Cirrhosis → TDF/TAF/ETV lifelong. HCV: GLE/PIB 8w non-cirr / 12w cirr; GT3 cirr: SOF/VEL 16w or GLE/PIB 12w; SVR12=cure. HDV: Needs HBsAg; PegIFN 48w only. HEV: GT1 pregnancy 20-30% mortality; GT3/4 chronic if immuno → Ribavirin. Reactivation: HBsAg+=prophylaxis; anti-HBc+ + rituximab=prophylaxis. HCC: 6m US±AFP if cirr/high-risk HBV.

Summary

Viral hepatitis encompasses five hepatotropic viruses: A, B, C, D, E. HAV & HEV = faecal-oral transmission; acute only (no chronicity); HAV = vaccine preventable; HEV genotype 1 in pregnancy = 20–30% mortality (fulminant); HEV GT3/4 = zoonotic, chronic in immunosuppressed (ribavirin). HBV, HCV, HDV = blood-borne; HBV = partially dsDNA, HBV DNA, HBeAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs, HBsAg serology; 4 phases: immune tolerant → immune active → inactive carrier → HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis; Treatment indications: immune active (DNA>20k + ALT>2×ULN), HBeAg-negative CHB (DNA>2k + ALT>ULN), cirrhosis (any DNA); 1st-line NUCs: TDF/TAF/ETV (high barrier, potent); lifelong usually; PegIFN finite but lower SVR. HCV: RNA virus, 75–85% chronic; genotypes 1–6; DAA cure >95%; pan-genotypic GLE/PIB 300/120mg OD ×8w (non-cirrhotic) / 12w (cirrhotic); GT3 cirrhosis = SOF/VEL 16w or GLE/PIB 12w; SVR12 = cure. HDV = defective, needs HBsAg; superinfection worse; PegIFN 48w only proven; bulevirtide emerging. HEV: GT1/2 faecal-oral acute; GT3/4 zoonotic chronic in immunocompromised (ribavirin); GT1 pregnancy 3rd trimester = 20–30% mortality. HBV reactivation: HBsAg+ → prophylactic NUCs (TDF/TAF/ETV); anti-HBc+ (rituximab, HSCT) → prophylactic NUCs. HCC surveillance: 6-monthly US ± AFP if cirrhosis or high-risk chronic HBV.

MCQs (10)

  1. Hepatitis B "immune active" phase is defined by:

A. HBsAg+, HBeAg+, HBV DNA >10⁷, normal ALT

B. HBsAg+, HBeAg+, HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL, ALT >2×ULN

C. HBsAg+, HBeAg−, HBV DNA <2,000, normal ALT

D. HBsAg−, anti-HBs+, anti-HBc+

E. HBsAg+, HBeAg+, HBV DNA >10⁵, ALT normal

  1. First-line treatment for chronic Hepatitis B:

A. Lamivudine

B. Interferon-alpha

C. Tenofovir (TDF/TAF) or Entecavir

D. Adefovir

E. Pegylated Interferon + Ribavirin

  1. Preferred pan-genotypic DAA regimen for HCV (non-cirrhotic):

A. Sofosbuvir + Daclatasvir 12 weeks

B. Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 8 weeks

C. Sofosbuvir + Ledipasvir 12 weeks

D. Elbasvir/Grazoprevir 12 weeks

E. Sofosbuvir + Velpatasvir + Voxilaprevir 12 weeks

  1. HCV Genotype 3 with cirrhosis — recommended treatment duration with Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir:

A. 8 weeks

B. 12 weeks

C. 16 weeks

D. 24 weeks

E. 12 weeks with Ribavirin

  1. Hepatitis D virus (HDV) — which statement is TRUE?

A. Can replicate independently of HBV

B. Requires HBsAg for assembly and transmission

C. Treated effectively with nucleos(t)ide analogues

D. Transmitted faeco-orally

E. Vaccine available

  1. Hepatitis E in pregnancy — genotype 1 in 3rd trimester mortality:

A. <5%

B. 10–15%

C. 20–30%

D. 40–50%

E. >80%

  1. HBV reactivation prophylaxis is indicated for:

A. Anti-HBs positive only

B. HBsAg positive (any immunosuppression)

C. Anti-HBc positive only (all cases)

D. Anti-HBc negative only

E. Anti-HBc positive with low-dose steroids only

  1. HCV cure is defined as:

A. Undetectable HCV RNA at end of treatment

B. Undetectable HCV RNA at 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12)

C. Normal ALT at end of treatment

C. Anti-HCV antibody negative

E. Viral load <1000 IU/mL at week 4

  1. Preferred treatment for HCV Genotype 3 with compensated cirrhosis:

A. Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 8 weeks

B. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 16 weeks (or Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 12 weeks)

C. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 weeks

D. Sofosbuvir + Ribavirin 24 weeks

E. Peginterferon + Ribavirin 24 weeks

  1. Hepatitis E genotype 1 in 3rd trimester pregnancy — most feared complication:

A. Chronic hepatitis

B. Fulminant hepatic failure (mortality 20–30%)

C. Vertical transmission causing neonatal hepatitis

D. Pancreatitis

E. Preterm labour

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old man has HBsAg+, HBeAg+, HBV DNA 5×10⁷ IU/mL, ALT 150 (ULN 40). FibroScan 6 kPa. Best management?

A. Observe (immune tolerant)

B. Start Tenofovir alafenamide 25mg OD (immune active)

C. PegIFN-α 48 weeks

D. Lamivudine 100mg OD

E. No treatment needed

  1. A 40-year-old woman with chronic HBV (HBeAg−, HBV DNA 5,000 IU/mL, ALT 80 ULN 40, FibroScan 9 kPa). Best management?

A. Observe (inactive carrier)

B. Start Entecavir 0.5mg OD (HBeAg-negative CHB)

C. PegIFN-α 48 weeks

D. Lamivudine

E. Repeat DNA in 6 months

  1. A 50-year-old man with HCV Genotype 1a, no cirrhosis (FibroScan 5 kPa). Preferred DAA regimen?

A. Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 300/120mg OD ×8 weeks

B. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 weeks

C. Sofosbuvir/Ledipasvir 12 weeks

D. Elbasvir/Grazoprevir 12 weeks

E. Sofosbuvir/Daclatasvir 12 weeks

  1. A 55-year-old man with HCV Genotype 3, cirrhosis (FibroScan 18 kPa). Best DAA regimen?

A. Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 8 weeks

B. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 16 weeks (or Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 12 weeks)

C. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 weeks

C. Sofosbuvir + Ribavirin 24 weeks

E. Elbasvir/Grazoprevir 12 weeks

  1. A 25-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks) in a hepatitis E outbreak area develops jaundice, ALF. Most likely genotype?

A. Genotype 3

B. Genotype 1

C. Genotype 2

D. Genotype 4

E. Cannot determine

  1. A 40-year-old man with HIV (CD4 350, VL<50 on DTG/TAF/FTC) has HBsAg+, anti-HBc+, HBV DNA 200 IU/mL. Starting rituximab for lymphoma. Management?

A. Monitor HBV DNA

B. Start Tenofovir alafenamide prophylaxis (continue ≥12 months post-rituximab)

C. Entecavir only if DNA >2000

D. No prophylaxis needed (low DNA)

E. HBIG + vaccine

  1. A 35-year-old man with HIV (CD4 400, VL<50) diagnosed with HCV GT1a, no cirrhosis. Preferred DAA?

A. Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 8 weeks (check DTG interaction — OK)

B. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 weeks

C. Sofosbuvir/Ledipasvir + Ribavirin

D. Elbasvir/Grazoprevir

E. PegIFN + Ribavirin

  1. An HBsAg-positive patient on anti-CD20 therapy (rituximab) develops HBV reactivation (DNA 10⁸). Management?

A. Stop rituximab only

B. Start Tenofovir alafenamide + continue rituximab; continue antiviral ≥12 months post-rituximab

C. Switch to obinutuzumab

D. Lamivudine

E. HBIG

  1. A 60-year-old post-liver transplant patient on tacrolimus/MMF develops chronic HEV (GT3). Management?

A. Supportive only

B. PegIFN-α

C. Reduce immunosuppression + Ribavirin 600mg OD ×3 months

D. Sofosbuvir

E. No treatment (self-limiting)

  1. A 30-year-old woman with chronic HCV GT1a (failed SOF/VEL). Retreatment?

A. Repeat SOF/VEL

B. SOF/VEL/VOX (Vosevi) 12 weeks (or GLE/PIB + SOF + RBV 16w)

C. GLE/PIB 8 weeks

D. SOF/LDV 24 weeks

E. PegIFN + RBV 24 weeks

  1. Hepatitis B in pregnancy — 28-week pregnant woman, HBsAg+, HBeAg+, HBV DNA 5×10⁷ IU/mL. Management to prevent vertical transmission?

A. No intervention needed

B. Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate 245mg OD from 28 weeks → stop at delivery/6w post; Infant HBIG + vaccine within 12h

C. Lamivudine from 28 weeks

D. Caesarean section

E. Infant vaccine only

Flashcards

  • Q: HBV immune active phase

A: HBeAg+, DNA>20k, ALT>2xULN

  • HBV inactive carrier

HBeAg-, DNA<2000, ALT normal

  • HBV treat 1st line

TDF/TAF/ETV

  • HBV cirrhosis treat

Any DNA → NUCs

  • HCV DAA pangenotypic

GLE/PIB 8/12w

  • HCV GT3 cirr

SOF/VEL 16w or GLE/PIB 12w

  • SVR12

HCV RNA undetectable 12w post-tx = cure

  • HDV

Needs HBsAg; PegIFN 48w only

  • HEV pregnancy

GT1 3rd tri 20-30% mortality

  • HBV reactivation

HBsAg+ = NUC prophylaxis; anti-HBc+ + rituximab = prophylaxis

  • HBV pregnancy

TDF 3rd tri if DNA>200k; Infant HBIG+Vaccine 12h

  • HCV GT3 cirr

SOF/VEL 16w or GLE/PIB 12w

  • HEV GT3/4 chronic

Immunocompromised; Ribavirin 3m

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Immune active phase: HBeAg+, HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL, ALT >2×ULN.
  2. C — TDF/TAF/ETV are 1st-line (high barrier, potent, low resistance).
  3. B — GLE/PIB 8 weeks is preferred pan-genotypic for non-cirrhotic HCV.
  4. C — HCV GT3 with cirrhosis: SOF/VEL 16 weeks (or GLE/PIB 12 weeks).
  5. B — HDV is defective and requires HBsAg for assembly/transmission.
  6. C — HEV GT1 in 3rd trimester: 20–30% mortality from fulminant hepatic failure.
  7. B — HBsAg+ = prophylactic NUCs for any immunosuppression.
  8. B — SVR12 (undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks post-treatment) = cure.
  9. B — GT3 cirrhosis: SOF/VEL 16 weeks (or GLE/PIB 12 weeks preferred).
  10. B — HEV GT1 in 3rd trimester = fulminant hepatic failure, 20–30% mortality.

SBAs

  1. B — HBeAg+, DNA>20k, ALT>2×ULN = immune active → TDF/TAF/ETV.
  2. B — HBeAg−, DNA>2000, ALT>ULN = HBeAg-negative CHB → ETV/TDF/TAF.
  3. A — HCV GT1a non-cirrhotic: GLE/PIB 8w preferred (pan-genotypic).
  4. B — GT3 cirrhosis: SOF/VEL 16w or GLE/PIB 12w.
  5. B — HEV genotype 1 causes severe disease in pregnancy (20–30% mortality).
  6. B — Anti-HBc+ + rituximab = high reactivation risk → TDF/TAF prophylaxis.
  7. A — HIV/HCV coinfection: same DAA regimens; DTG + GLE/PIB OK (no significant interaction).
  8. B — HBV reactivation on rituximab: start TAF/TDF + continue ≥12m post-rituximab.
  9. C — Chronic HEV GT3 in immunocompromised: reduce IS + ribavirin 600mg OD ×3m.
  10. B — Post-DAA failure: SOF/VEL/VOX (Vosevi) 12w is preferred salvage.
  11. B — HBV in pregnancy: TDF from 28w if DNA>200,000; infant HBIG+vaccine within 12h.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 14.1.55 Pneumocystis Pneumonia Pcp

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, pneumocystis, pcp, hiv, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: high

exam_relevance: "FCPS: AIDS-defining illness, CD4<200, ground-glass CXR, TMP-SMX | MRCP: Hypoxia disproportionate to CXR, β-D-glucan"

see_also: "[[HIV Infection and AIDS]], [[Fungal Pneumonias]], [[Immunocompromised Host]], [[Cryptococcal Meningitis]]"

created: 2025-06-17

modified: 2025-06-17

Pneumocystis Pneumonia (PCP / PJP)

[!info] Davidson Ch 11/14 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Fungi → Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly P. carinii)
FCPS/MRCP Focus: AIDS-defining OI, CD4<200, subacute dyspnoea, ground-glass CXR, β-D-glucan, TMP-SMX first-line

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Recognise Pneumocystis jirovecii (fungus, atypical, can't be cultured)
  • [ ] Identify Risk groups: HIV CD4<200, transplant, high-dose steroids, biologics, malignancy
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical features: Subacute dyspnoea, dry cough, fever, hypoxia disproportionate to CXR
  • [ ] Diagnose: CXR (bilateral perihilar ground-glass), HRCT (ground-glass), β-D-glucan ↑, PCP PCR/IF on BAL/sputum, induced sputum
  • [ ] Treat: TMP-SMX first-line (15-20mg TMP/kg/day, 21 days) + Prednisolone if PaO2<70mmHg
  • [ ] Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX when CD4<200; alternatives (Dapsone, Atovaquone, Pentamidine)

📚 Microbiology

FeatureDetails
ClassificationFungus (atypical; lacks ergosterol in membrane)
Former namePneumocystis carinii (zoonotic misnomer → now P. jirovecii for human)
SourceUbiquitous; airborne; reacquisition/reactivation; person-to-person nosocomial
CultureCannot be cultured (in vitro); diagnosis by microscopy, antigen, PCR
StainsGomori methenamine silver (GMS) - cysts (black); Giemsa/toluidine blue - intracystic bodies; Direct IF (DFA) - most sensitive

👥 Risk Groups

GroupCD4 / RiskNotes
HIV/AIDSCD4<200 (oropharyngeal candidiasis, prior PCP)Most common OI; peak risk CD4<100
Solid organ transplant1-6 months post (peak immunosuppression)Lung/kidney high risk
HSCTDay 30-100 (engraftment), GVHD treatmentHigh mortality
MalignancyAcute leukaemia, lymphoma, chemotherapyProlonged neutropenia
AutoimmuneSLE, vasculitis, IBD on high-dose steroids (≥20mg pred >4 weeks)Biologics (rituximab, TNF-α)
CGD, SCIDPrimary immunodeficienciesChildren

🩺 Clinical Features

FeatureDetails
OnsetSubacute (1-2 weeks) in HIV; acute (days) in non-HIV
SymptomsProgressive dyspnoea (90%), dry/non-productive cough (90%), fever (80%), malaise, weight loss
ExaminationTachypnoea, tachycardia, cyanosis, fine inspiratory crackles (often minimal)
Key SignHypoxia disproportionate to physical findings/CXR (PaO2<70mmHg, A-a gradient ↑, SpO2<95% on exertion)
Extrapulmonary (rare)Lymph nodes, spleen, liver, bone marrow, choroid, thyroid
[!warning] Exam Pearl: "Pneumocystis = hypoxia without CXR signs" — a young HIV+ patient with sudden severe dyspnoea + minimal CXR findings = PCP until proven otherwise.

🔬 Investigations

TestFindingsNotes
ABGPaO2 <70 mmHg (room air); A-a gradient >35 mmHg; often hypocapnia (respiratory alkalosis)Triggers steroids
CXRBilateral perihilar/diffuse interstitial/reticulonodular ("bat-wing"); ground-glass opacities (GGO); pneumatoceles, pneumothorax (10%); normal in 10%"Bat-wing" pattern
HRCT chestBilateral GGO (sensitive); mosaic attenuation; cystsBetter than CXR
LDHMarkedly elevated (>500 U/L); non-specific (also in lymphoma, haemolysis)Severity/prognosis marker
β-D-glucan (BDG)Elevated (≥80 pg/mL); pan-fungal marker (Aspergillus, Candida, PJP)Negative Mucorales/Cryptococcus
Sputum (induced)IF/DFA (cysts), GMS, PCRSensitivity 50-90% with induction
BAL (bronchoalveolar lavage)Gold standard; IF/DFA, GMS, Giemsa, PCRSensitivity >90%
PCP PCR (nasopharyngeal, BAL)High sensitivity; specificity lower (colonisation)Quantitative threshold helps
Exercise testSpO2 ↓ ≥4% on 6-min walkOld but specific
[!tip] Exam Pearl: TMP-SMX prophylaxis reduces yield of all tests (including PCR). Don't stop empirically if clinical suspicion high.

💊 Management

First-line Therapy

SeverityRegimenDurationAdjuncts
Mild-Moderate (PaO2 ≥70)TMP-SMX (15-20 mg TMP/kg/day) PO in 3-4 divided doses21 days-
Severe (PaO2 <70 or A-a >35)TMP-SMX (15-20 mg TMP/kg/day) IV in 3-4 divided doses21 daysPrednisolone (see below)

Adjunctive Corticosteroids (Severe Disease)

Indication: PaO2 <70 mmHg on room air OR A-a gradient >35 mmHg

DrugDoseDuration
Prednisolone40 mg bd × 5 days → 40 mg od × 5 days → 20 mg od × 11 days21 days (taper)
Methylprednisolone IV (if unable oral)75% of prednisolone doseSame
[!critical] Start steroids EARLY (within 72h of treatment) — reduces mortality by ~50% in moderate-severe PCP.

Alternative Therapies (TMP-SMX Intolerance/Allergy)

DrugDoseNotes
Pentamidine IV4 mg/kg/day IV (max 300-600 mg)Severe: nephrotoxicity, pancreatitis, hypoglycaemia/hyperglycaemia, QT prolongation
Clindamycin + PrimaquineClindamycin 600 mg IV/PO q6h + Primaquine 30 mg base PO odCheck G6PD; haemolysis risk
Atovaquone (mild-moderate only)750 mg PO bd (with fatty meal)GI upset, rash; avoid in diarrhoea
Dapsone + TrimethoprimDapsone 100 mg PO od + TMP 5 mg/kg PO q6hCheck G6PD; haemolysis, methaemoglobinaemia

🛡️ Prophylaxis

Indications

IndicationThreshold
HIVCD4 <200 cells/μL (or oropharyngeal candidiasis, prior PCP, AIDS-defining illness)
TransplantPer protocol (lung/kidney high risk)
High-dose steroids≥20 mg prednisolone/day >4 weeks (controversial)
HSCTAllogeneic recipients; seropositive recipients

Regimens

DrugDoseNotes
TMP-SMX (FIRST-LINE)160/800 mg PO daily (DS) OR 80/400 mg daily (SS)Also covers Toxo (CD4<100)
Dapsone100 mg PO daily (or 200 mg weekly)Check G6PD first; haemolysis risk
Atovaquone750 mg PO bd (with fatty meal)Mild-moderate only; expensive
Pentamidine (inhaled)300 mg monthly via Respigard II nebuliserNo systemic protection (no Toxo); pneumothorax risk

Discontinuation

  • HIV: When CD4 >200 for ≥3 months on ART (and VL suppressed)
  • Restart if CD4 falls <200

📋 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PathogenPneumocystis jirovecii (fungus, can't be cultured)
RiskHIV CD4<200; transplant, steroids, biologics
ClinicalSubacute dyspnoea, dry cough, fever; hypoxia disproportionate to CXR
CXRBilateral perihilar GGO, "bat-wing"; can be normal (10%)
DiagnosisBAL IF/PCR (gold); induced sputum; β-D-glucan elevated
TreatmentTMP-SMX 15-20 mg TMP/kg/day × 21 days (PO/IV by severity)
SteroidsPrednisolone if PaO2<70mmHg or A-a>35 (start within 72h)
ProphylaxisTMP-SMX DS daily when CD4<200; stop when CD4>200 ×3 months on ART
LDHElevated (severity marker)

❓ Viva Questions (FCPS/MRCP)

  1. What is Pneumocystis jirovecii? Why can't it be cultured?
  2. A HIV+ patient with CD4 150 presents with dyspnoea and SpO2 88% on room air. CXR is near-normal. Diagnosis and next step?
  3. When do you add steroids in PCP? What dose and duration?
  4. Compare first-line and second-line PCP treatment options.
  5. Why is dapsone contraindicated without G6PD testing?
  6. A renal transplant patient on tacrolimus develops fever, dry cough, and bilateral infiltrates. Differential and approach?
  7. What is the role of β-D-glucan in PCP diagnosis?
  8. Differentiate inhaled vs IV pentamidine.
  9. When do you start PCP prophylaxis in HIV? When do you stop?
  10. What is the sensitivity of induced sputum vs BAL for PCP diagnosis?

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
PCP prophylaxis for ToxoplasmaTMP-SMX covers both PCP (CD4<200) and Toxo (CD4<100+IgG+); same drug
PCP PCR vs colonisationPCR very sensitive; quantitative cutoffs distinguish colonisation vs active disease; clinical correlation essential
Pentamidine IV vs inhaledIV: severe systemic PCP; inhaled: prophylaxis only (no Toxo coverage)
PCP vs bacterial pneumonia in HIVPCP: subacute, dry cough, hypoxia>>CXR, GGO, ↑LDH, ↑BDG; Bacterial: acute, purulent sputum, consolidation, leucocytosis
Steroid timing in PCPStart WITHIN 72h of treatment; not after 7 days; do NOT taper too quickly (3-week regimen)
Stop prophylaxis when CD4>200CD4>200 for ≥3 months ON ART with suppressed VL

Mnemonic: "PCP" → Pneumocystis jirovecii (fungus), CD4<200 (HIV), Proteinaceous alveoli (foamy exudate), Ground-glass CXR, TMP-SMX 21 days + Steroids if PaO2<70, Prophylaxis when CD4<200, β-D-glucan ↑

Mnemonic - Steroids: "70-35-21" → PaO2<70 mmHg OR A-a>35 mmHg → Prednisolone 21 days (40 bd→40 od→20 od taper)

🗺️ Mind Map

Pneumocystis jirovecii
Risk Groups
HIV CD4<200
Transplant (lung, kidney)
HSCT
High-dose steroids

Clinical
Subacute dyspnoea
Dry cough, fever
Hypoxia >> CXR

CXR/HRCT: GGO
LDH ↑
β-D-glucan ↑

TMP-SMX 15-20mg/kg/d × 21d

– Clindamycin + Primaquine

Dapsone (G6PD neg)
Atovaquone
Pentamidine inhaled

📄 One-Page Revision Card

Pneumocystis Pneumonia (PCP)Key Facts
OrganismP. jirovecii (fungus; cannot be cultured)
RiskHIV CD4<200; transplant, steroids, biologics
ClinicalSubacute dyspnoea, dry cough, fever; hypoxia disproportionate to CXR
CXRBilateral GGO ("bat-wing"); normal in 10%
LDH / BDGLDH ↑; β-D-glucan elevated
DiagnosisBAL IF/PCR (gold); induced sputum
TreatmentTMP-SMX 15-20 mg TMP/kg/day × 21 days (PO mild / IV severe)
SteroidsPrednisolone 21-day taper if PaO2<70 or A-a>35 (start <72h)
ProphylaxisTMP-SMX DS daily when CD4<200; stop when CD4>200 ×3 months on ART
